# N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS
    

    
    *Acrostic Letter: G (GLASS)*  
    *Turning: GLASS (Novellas 01-05)*

# N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS
    
    ## CHAPTER 1: SOMETHING WRONG
    
    The grid-hum had changed.
    
    Jhace stood motionless in the Junction Seven access tunnel, one hand pressed flat against the support beam, counting heartbeats. His breath held. The vibration thrummed through his palm—steady, constant, doubled.
    
    Not doubled. Harmonizing. Two frequencies where there should be one.
    
    He pulled his hand away and marked the position with chalk: white line at shoulder height, notation in his small, precise script. *J7-B4. Doubled harmonic. Tide 0, Kindling, third bell.*
    
    Twenty-three names on the memorial plaque. He didn't need to look at it anymore. The brass had gone green with age, mounted in the administrative hall three levels up, but the names stayed sharp in his mind. Twenty-three people who'd been standing in Sublevel Four when the support lattice failed. Twenty-three names because he'd filed the report six months prior—stress fractures, load redistribution recommended, priority urgent—and Master Hellik had logged it into the archives where warnings went to die.
    
    Jhace moved to the next beam. Pressed his palm. Counted.
    
    Same doubled hum. Amplitude slightly higher.
    
    He logged it. Moved again. The tunnel stretched ahead, copper-gold dust motes drifting through the lamplight. Dust that shouldn't be here. Dust that had started appearing two Tides ago, tasting of metal when he breathed through his mouth, leaving a faint shimmer on his skin when he wiped his hands on his work trousers.
    
    Fourteen anomalies documented so far. Pressure differential in the eastern columns. Micro-displacement in the anchor points. Grid-hum that felt wrong in ways he couldn't articulate in the official forms, so he wrote them in his personal log instead: *Frequency feels anxious. Beam responding to something external. Load pattern doesn't match usage.*
    
    Master Hellik would've told him metal didn't feel anxious. Master Hellik had told him exactly that, actually, three days before Junction Seven collapsed.
    
    The tunnel opened into the main junction chamber. Vast. Twenty-eight support columns radiating from a central hub, each one thick as three men standing shoulder-to-shoulder. The grid-hum resonated here like the inside of a drum, complex harmonics layering into something almost musical. Jhace had loved this sound when he'd first started as a grid inspector seven Turns ago. It had felt like listening to the city breathe.
    
    Now it felt like listening to the city hold its breath.
    
    He approached the central column. Set his hand against it. The copper-gold dust coated the surface—more here than in the access tunnel. His palm came away shimmering.
    
    *Doubled harmonic. Amplitude: significant. Pressure reading—*
    
    The pitch dropped.
    
    Just for a heartbeat. Just enough that Jhace felt it in his chest before he heard it. The entire junction chamber resonated at a lower frequency, and then snapped back.
    
    He stepped away from the column, pulse hammering. Looked up. The ceiling braces showed no visible displacement. No cracks. No separation at the welds.
    
    But the hum had changed. Definitely changed.
    
    Jhace pulled out his documentation slate and wrote with hands that wanted to shake: *Tide 0, Kindling, third bell, forty-seventh mark. Central column J7-C. Pitch drop observed, duration approx. four heartbeats, returned to doubled harmonic baseline. Structural integrity visually intact. Recommend immediate—*
    
    Footsteps.
    
    He turned. Chloen emerged from the eastern access tunnel, tool belt jingling, her face streaked with the pale dust that came from working the deepest tunnels. She raised a hand in greeting, then stopped.
    
    "You feel it too?" she asked.
    
    "The hum?"
    
    "My hands." She held them up. Even from three strides away, Jhace could see the tremor. Fine amplitude, rapid frequency. Her fingers shook like she'd been holding a manifestation, except Chloen wasn't a manifestor. Nobody in Saint Merro was—not before the anomalies started. Manifestation was Temple business, Crown business, something that happened in the coastal cities where the air tasted different and the old bloodlines still mattered.
    
    "How long?" Jhace asked.
    
    "Started when I touched the support beam in Tunnel East-Four. Went away when I let go. Came back when I grabbed my hammer." She flexed her fingers. The tremor persisted. "This isn't normal."
    
    "Nothing's normal." Jhace approached, pulled out his measuring tape. "Can I?"
    
    She held out her hands. He measured the amplitude by eye—approximately two millimeters at rest, increasing to four when she tried to make a fist. He noted it on his slate.
    
    "Should I report to medical?" Chloen asked.
    
    "I don't know what they'd do about it." Jhace met her eyes. "Has it happened before?"
    
    "No. Just today. Just after—" She stopped. Frowned. "Just after the pitch changed. Did you hear it?"
    
    He had. "Central column. Four heartbeats ago."
    
    "I was in East-Four. Felt it through the beam. Thought the whole grid was about to let go." Her hands still trembled. "Jhace. What's happening?"
    
    He wanted to say something reassuring. Something about material fatigue or standard deviation or acceptable variance. But Chloen had been working the tunnels for twelve Turns. She knew when the grid felt wrong.
    
    "I'm documenting everything," he said instead. "If this is a pattern, we'll catch it."
    
    "If?"
    
    "*When.*" He corrected himself, though the word felt like a lie. Patterns were supposed to make sense. Doubled harmonics and copper-gold dust and contact-dependent tremors weren't a pattern—they were symptoms of something he didn't have framework to understand.
    
    Chloen flexed her hands again. The tremor had diminished slightly, but hadn't disappeared. "I'm logging out early. Going to see if it stops when I'm away from the grid."
    
    "Good. Let me know."
    
    She left through the western tunnel. Jhace watched her go, then turned back to the central column. The grid-hum thrummed through the chamber, doubled and anxious and wrong in thirteen ways he'd documented and probably forty more he hadn't.
    
    He pressed his palm to the column again. Counted heartbeats. Felt the vibration travel up his arm, into his chest, settling somewhere behind his ribs like a second pulse.
    
    The copper taste arrived on his next breath.
    
    He pulled his hand away. Stared at his palm. The copper-gold dust had transferred to his skin, but now it seemed to glow faintly—not with reflected lamplight, but with its own internal luminescence. Faint. Barely perceptible. But there.
    
    Jhace wiped his hand on his trousers. The glow didn't transfer. It faded from his skin within three heartbeats, leaving only the metallic shimmer behind.
    
    He wrote in his log: *Contact with central column produces faint luminescence in transferred dust. Duration: three heartbeats. Intensity: minimal. Copper taste concurrent with phenomenon.*
    
    Footnote, smaller script: *Am I the only one seeing this?*
    
    He didn't write the next thought, but it sat in his chest anyway, heavy as the doubled hum: *This is Junction Seven again. Something's wrong and nobody's listening and when it fails, people die.*
    
    The grid-hum dropped in pitch again.
    
    This time it didn't snap back.
    
    Jhace felt it in his sternum—a sustained vibration at a frequency that made his teeth ache. The copper taste intensified. The dust in the air seemed to pulse with that same faint glow he'd seen on his palm.
    
    He ran.
    
    Not away from the junction. Toward the administrative hall. Up three levels. Through corridors that suddenly felt too narrow, support beams too thin, ceiling too low. The grid-hum followed him, resonating through the stone, and with each stride the frequency dropped lower.
    
    By the time he burst into the hall, gasping, the hum had become a *pressure*—something he felt more than heard, pushing against his chest from the inside out.
    
    Master Hellik looked up from his desk, annoyed. "Torrins. What—"
    
    "Evacuation," Jhace said. "Now. Junction Seven and everything above it. The grid's failing."
    
    "The grid's been stable for—"
    
    "The harmonic dropped. Sustained drop. It's not recovering." Jhace slapped his documentation slate on the desk. "Fourteen anomalies logged over two Tides. Contact-dependent tremors starting in the workforce. Dust showing luminescent properties. Master Hellik, this is Junction Seven again, and if you file this report without acting on it, the deaths are on you."
    
    Hellik's face went pale. Then red. "You don't have authority to—"
    
    The pressure in Jhace's chest *spiked*.
    
    Not metaphorical pressure. Actual physical force, like someone had shoved a fist through his ribs and grabbed his lungs. He staggered. The air tasted like copper, like burnt metal, like something fundamental breaking.
    
    And then the world split open.
    
    Not the ground. Not the walls. The *air itself*, high above Saint Merro, tearing like fabric.
    
    Jhace knew this because every person in the administrative hall could see it through the observation ports—a seam of copper-gold light opening in the sky, widening, and something vast and incomprehensible pushing through. Not a weapon. Not a ship. A *spindle*, massive beyond description, puncturing down through atmosphere and stone and grid and everything, and the city screamed.
    
    Not the people. The city. The grid-hum became a wail, every support beam and anchor point and lattice joint resonating in sympathetic agony, and Jhace felt it in his chest like a bell being struck.
    
    Master Hellik was shouting. Everyone was shouting. But Jhace couldn't hear them over the sound of the world breaking.
    
    He pressed his palm against the nearest wall. Felt the doubled harmonic become tripled, quadrupled, a dozen overlapping frequencies fighting for dominance. The copper-gold dust fell like snow through the observation ports. The spindle kept descending—through the city, through the undercity, through the planetary crust.
    
    *Through.*
    
    Jhace understood, in the part of his mind still capable of documentation: this wasn't an impact. This was a *piercing*. Whatever that spindle was, it was going all the way through.
    
    The pressure in his chest stabilized. The frequencies stopped fighting. The grid-hum settled into a new configuration—still wrong, still anxious, but *stable* in its wrongness.
    
    The Piercing was complete.
    
    Jhace lowered his hand from the wall. Looked at Master Hellik. The administrator stared at the observation ports, face slack with shock.
    
    "Evacuate Junction Seven," Jhace said. His voice sounded distant, muffled. "And Sublevel Four. And every grid-dependent structure within eight columns of the central junction."
    
    "Why?" Hellik whispered.
    
    "Because I can feel it breaking. And this time, you're going to listen."
    
    Hellik looked at him. At the copper-gold dust coating Jhace's hand. At whatever expression Jhace wore that made the administrator's face go pale again.
    
    "Sound the bells," Hellik said to his assistant. "Evacuation protocol. Junction Seven and associated structures."
    
    The assistant ran.
    
    Jhace stood in the administrative hall, hand still glowing faintly with transferred dust, and counted heartbeats while the city learned what happened when the world's rules broke.
    
    He made it to ninety-seven before the first building started to collapse.
    
    ---
    
    *End Chapter 1*
    

---



## CHAPTER 2: GLASS THAT LISTENS
    
    Tiffani had been tracking the Seven-Three star for eight hours when it moved.
    
    Not drifted. Not wobbled in the atmospheric distortion that plagued observation during Pouring phase. *Moved*—a clean displacement of four arc-seconds northwest, visible through the primary lens as a discontinuity in the tracking plate's exposure line.
    
    She blinked. Checked the lens alignment. Checked the mounting stability. Checked her own pulse, because sometimes exhaustion produced hallucinations and she'd been awake for nineteen hours documenting the stellar drift phenomenon that had started three Tides ago.
    
    The star had still moved.
    
    Tiffani marked the plate with her notation stylus: *Tide 0, Kindling, third bell, fifty-first mark. Seven-Three displacement: 4.2 arc-seconds, bearing 312 degrees. Instrument error: none detected. Atmospheric interference: minimal. Conclusion—*
    
    The copper taste arrived before she could finish writing.
    
    She lifted her head from the eyepiece. The taste coated her tongue like she'd been sucking on old coins. Not unpleasant, exactly, but *wrong*—a flavor that didn't belong in her mouth, in the observatory, in the world as she understood it.
    
    The air shimmered.
    
    Tiffani stood. Crossed to the eastern observation port. Saint Merro spread below in its characteristic terrace configuration, each level carved into the planetary crust like a vast inverted amphitheater. Lamplight marked the grid streets. The central spindle of the Crown Archive rose from the lowest level, visible as a dark spire against the ambient glow.
    
    The shimmer intensified. Copper-gold particulate matter, falling like snow, catching the light as it drifted past the observation port.
    
    Tiffani pressed her palm against the glass. The shimmer coated the outer surface. Where her hand made contact, the particles seemed to pulse—a faint luminescence, barely perceptible, lasting perhaps three heartbeats before fading.
    
    She pulled out her documentation journal. Wrote with ink-stained fingers that wanted to shake: *Atmospheric particulate, metallic composition apparent, luminescent properties when in contact with—*
    
    The sky tore open.
    
    Not metaphorically. Not gradually. The atmospheric envelope above Saint Merro *ripped*, a seam of copper-gold light opening like someone had taken a blade to the fabric of reality itself. Tiffani watched through the observation port, stylus frozen mid-word, as the seam widened.
    
    And something came through.
    
    Vast. Incomprehensibly vast. A spindle—that was the only word her mind could supply—descending through the tear in the sky. Not falling. *Descending*, with clear directionality and purpose, the way a needle descends through cloth.
    
    The spindle was copper-gold, the same color as the dust, the same color as the luminescence. It tapered to a point that could have impaled cities, widened to a diameter that could have swallowed districts. And it kept coming.
    
    Tiffani ran to the primary telescope. Swung it toward the descending spindle. Pressed her eye to the lens.
    
    The magnification made it worse. The spindle's surface showed texture—not smooth metal, but something complex, layered, almost organic. Patterns that hurt to look at directly. Geometries that didn't resolve properly in her visual field.
    
    She pulled back. Grabbed her journal. Drew what she'd seen with rapid, shaking strokes: the taper, the texture, the impossible angles. Her training as a glassworker gave her the vocabulary—*chamfer here, bevel here, compound curve that shouldn't exist in three-dimensional space here*—but the drawing looked wrong. Looked like something a child would sketch from a nightmare.
    
    The spindle descended through the upper city.
    
    Tiffani watched it penetrate the Crown Archive spire without slowing. Watched it continue down through the terrace levels. No explosion. No impact shockwave. Just *penetration*, clean and absolute, like the spindle was passing through Saint Merro the way a needle passes through water.
    
    The observatory shook.
    
    Not from impact. From *resonance*. The entire structure vibrated at a frequency Tiffani felt in her sternum, in her jaw, in the base of her skull. The lens plates rattled in their mounts. The tracking mechanisms whined. The copper-gold dust swirled in complex vortices, following patterns she would have called beautiful if they weren't accompanied by the overwhelming sensation that the world was ending.
    
    She pressed both hands against the observation port glass. Felt the vibration travel up her arms. The luminescence pulsed brighter where her palms made contact—not just visible now, but *bright*, copper-gold light threading through her skin like her veins had been replaced with molten metal.
    
    Tiffani pulled her hands away. Stared at her palms. The glow faded within five heartbeats, leaving only the metallic shimmer of transferred dust.
    
    She wrote in her journal: *Contact with atmospheric particulate produces sustained luminescence. Duration: five heartbeats. Intensity: significant. Suggests reactive properties, possibly—*
    
    The spindle completed its descent.
    
    Tiffani felt it more than saw it—a fundamental *shift* in the world's underlying structure. The resonance stopped. The dust settled. The seam in the sky remained open, a wound in the atmosphere pouring copper-gold light across the city, but the spindle was gone.
    
    No. Not gone. *Through.*
    
    She ran to the southern observation port. Looked down. The spindle emerged from the underside of the planetary crust, continuing its descent into the void below. Perfectly straight. Perfectly aligned. A geometric impossibility made manifest, punching through an entire world like the world wasn't even there.
    
    The Piercing was complete.
    
    Tiffani stood at the observation port, hands still shimmering with transferred dust, and watched her understanding of physical law dissolve.
    
    Stellar displacement. Atmospheric particulate with reactive properties. A geometric form that passed through solid matter without resistance. A seam in reality that remained open after the intrusion ended.
    
    She opened her journal to a fresh page. Dated it: *Tide 0, Kindling, third bell, post-Piercing.* Underlined the last word three times.
    
    Then she began to document.
    
    *Observable phenomena, priority sequence:*
    
    *1. Seven-Three star displaced 4.2 arc-seconds bearing 312, suggests gravitational disruption at stellar distances*
    
    *2. Atmospheric seam remains open, copper-gold light emission continuous, no apparent closure mechanism*
    
    *3. Spindle transit complete, estimated velocity constant throughout descent, no deceleration detected*
    
    *4. Resonance effects ceased immediately upon spindle emergence from planetary underside, suggests phenomenon was transit-dependent rather than impact-dependent*
    
    *5. Copper-gold particulate continues to fall, accumulation rate approx—*
    
    The bells started ringing.
    
    Evacuation protocol. Tiffani recognized the sequence—three rapid peals, pause, three more. She'd heard it twice in her life: once during the fire in the textile district, once during the flooding when the underground reservoir breached.
    
    She looked out the eastern port. Saw people streaming from buildings on the upper terraces. Saw the Crown Archive spire—or what remained of it. The spindle had passed directly through the central shaft, leaving a perfectly circular void. The structure still stood, but Tiffani could see clean through it, sky visible where stone should be.
    
    No collapse. No rubble. Just *absence*, as if the matter that had occupied that space had been translated elsewhere.
    
    She grabbed her documentation journal and her instrument case. Left the observatory at a run. The stairs spiraled down seven levels, and with each level the signs of the Piercing became more apparent. Copper-gold dust coating every surface. The air tasting of metal. People in the streets staring upward at the seam, at the light, at the impossibility bleeding through their sky.
    
    Tiffani emerged onto Tallow Street. The crowd flowed around her, everyone moving northwest toward the evacuation staging areas. She moved against the current, heading for the Crown Archive district.
    
    "Are you insane?" someone shouted at her. "They're evacuating—"
    
    "I know." Tiffani pushed past. "I need to see the penetration site."
    
    "It's going to collapse!"
    
    "Then I need to see it before it does."
    
    She ran. The crowd thinned. The bells kept ringing. The copper-gold light from the seam cast everything in a sickly radiance that made distances hard to judge and shadows fall in the wrong directions.
    
    The Archive district was chaos. People screaming. Crown administrators trying to organize evacuations while staring at the hole in their central spire. Tiffani pushed through to the penetration site itself—a circle of empty space eight strides in diameter, edges perfectly smooth, no debris.
    
    She knelt at the edge. Extended her hand into the void. Felt nothing. No heat. No cold. No residual resonance. Just air, occupying space where stone should be.
    
    A man in inspector's colors appeared beside her. Compact build, scarred hands, face pale with shock. He was staring at the void with an expression Tiffani recognized—the look of someone whose professional understanding had just been invalidated.
    
    "It's geometrically perfect," Tiffani said. Not to him specifically. To herself. To her journal, which she was already pulling out. "No fracture lines. No displacement of surrounding material. The matter was removed, not destroyed."
    
    The inspector looked at her. "You're documenting this?"
    
    "Someone has to." She measured the diameter with her tape measure. "Eight strides, three hand-widths. Circular cross-section. Surface texture—" She ran her fingers along the edge. Perfectly smooth. "—absent. This is a manufacturing precision, not a natural phenomenon."
    
    "Manufacturing suggests intention."
    
    "Yes." Tiffani met his eyes. "Whatever did this meant to do it."
    
    A low rumble vibrated through the ground. The inspector grabbed her arm. "We need to go. Now."
    
    "I haven't finished—"
    
    "The building is failing." He pulled her to her feet. "I'm a grid inspector. I know what structural compromise feels like. This whole district has maybe ten minutes before something catastrophic happens."
    
    Tiffani looked at the void. At the Archive spire. At the copper-gold light bleeding through the seam overhead.
    
    She wanted to stay. Wanted to measure every angle, document every surface, understand the mechanism of matter removal. But the rumble intensified, and the inspector's hand on her arm was insistent, and somewhere beneath her need to document was the basic survival instinct that recognized imminent danger.
    
    "Help me carry this," she said, shoving her instrument case at him.
    
    They ran.
    
    Behind them, the first building started to collapse. Not the Archive spire—that held, impossibly, despite the void through its core. But a residential structure three streets north, its support grid compromised by the Piercing's resonance effects. Tiffani heard it go—a sound like glass shattering on a massive scale, followed by screaming.
    
    The inspector stopped. Turned back. His face showed an expression Tiffani couldn't parse—grief, guilt, calculation, all layered together.
    
    "Keep going," he said, handing her the instrument case. "Get to the evacuation point."
    
    "Where are you—"
    
    "Someone's still in there." He was already running back toward the collapse. "Keep the documentation safe. Whatever you recorded, it matters."
    
    Tiffani watched him go. Wanted to follow. Wanted to tell him that documentation didn't matter if they all died, that the data would be meaningless without context, that—
    
    The heat arrived.
    
    Not external heat. Internal. A pressure in her chest, behind her ribs, like something fundamental had caught fire. Tiffani staggered. Pressed her hand to her sternum. The pressure intensified. The copper taste flooded her mouth. Her vision sparked with copper-gold light that had nothing to do with the seam overhead.
    
    And then she saw it: threads of light connecting everything. The collapsing building. The inspector running toward it. The people trapped inside. The structural beams. The grid-hum frequency. The dust in the air. Everything linked by copper-gold filaments that pulsed in time with her heartbeat.
    
    *I could pull those threads,* something in her mind whispered. *I could hold the building. Just like—*
    
    No. She didn't know how to do that. Didn't know what that pressure in her chest meant or what the threads were or why she could suddenly see the structural integrity of a building she'd never inspected.
    
    The pressure faded. The threads disappeared. Tiffani stood in the middle of Tallow Street, gasping, while the collapse continued and the inspector vanished into the dust cloud.
    
    She ran for the evacuation point. Clutched her journal and instrument case like they were the only real things left. The heat in her chest didn't fade completely, but it dropped to a manageable level—a warmth, persistent, waiting.
    
    Two hours later, sitting in an evacuation shelter with two hundred other people, Tiffani opened her journal to a fresh page and wrote: *Post-Piercing phenomena, preliminary observations:*
    
    *1. Matter removal mechanism unknown, requires further investigation*
    
    *2. Structural failures beginning across affected districts, evacuation ongoing*
    
    *3. Personal physiological response: heat in chest, visual hallucination of connective threads, duration approx. twelve heartbeats, did not recur*
    
    She paused. Stared at the third entry. Almost crossed it out.
    
    Instead, she underlined it twice and wrote in the margin: *Requires monitoring. Possibly unique to observer. Possibly universal. Sample size: one.*
    
    The heat in her chest pulsed once, as if in agreement.
    
    Tiffani closed the journal and waited for the world to finish breaking.
    
    ---
    
    *End Chapter 2*
    

---



## CHAPTER 3: FIRST CONTACT
    
    The building on Tallow Street hadn't collapsed yet, but Jhace could feel it dying.
    
    He stood in the dust cloud, coughing copper-gold particulate from his lungs, counting the structural compromises by sound. Beam stress: high-pitched whine. Stone fracture: low crack propagating through load-bearing walls. Grid-hum: chaotic, twelve overlapping frequencies fighting for dominance.
    
    Two hundred people inside. Maybe more. The evacuation bells had rung too late.
    
    Jhace pushed through the rubble field. The building's western wall had already failed—not from the Piercing itself, but from the resonance that followed. The spindle had passed three streets north, leaving a perfect void through the Crown Archive, but the vibration had propagated through the entire grid. Every structure with compromised integrity, every building that had been marked for repair and ignored, every report filed and archived—all of them failing now, simultaneously, because the world's rules had changed and Saint Merro's infrastructure couldn't adapt fast enough.
    
    Voices inside. Screaming. A child crying.
    
    The eastern support column was holding. Barely. Jhace could see the stress fractures radiating from the base, see the load redistributing in real-time as the western collapse shifted the building's entire weight profile. 
    
    He had maybe sixty heartbeats before the eastern column failed.
    
    After that, the upper four floors would pancake down onto the ground level, and everyone still inside would be crushed.
    
    Jhace ran toward the main entrance. The doorway had partially collapsed, leaving a gap maybe two hand-widths wide. He pressed his face to the opening. "Can anyone hear me?"
    
    "Here!" A woman's voice. "We're trapped in the western stairwell. The wall came down—there's maybe forty of us, we can't get to the exit!"
    
    Forty people. Sixty heartbeats. No time for proper evacuation procedure.
    
    "Stay away from the western wall," Jhace shouted. "Get to the eastern side of the stairwell. As far from the column as you can."
    
    "Why?"
    
    "Because that's where it's going to fail next."
    
    He pulled back from the doorway. Looked up at the building. The eastern column was visible through the collapsed western wall—a massive support beam, twice his height in diameter, holding the entire upper structure. Stress fractures visible even from ground level. Grid-hum emanating from it like a scream.
    
    Jhace pressed his hand against the column's base.
    
    The heat arrived immediately.
    
    Not in his hand—in his chest. Behind his ribs. The same sensation he'd felt in the Junction Seven chamber when the grid-hum dropped, but amplified a hundredfold. Like someone had replaced his lungs with a forge.
    
    He gasped. Pulled his hand away. The heat didn't fade.
    
    The copper-gold dust on his palm *glowed*.
    
    Not faint luminescence this time. Actual light, bright enough to cast shadows, pulsing in time with his heartbeat. Jhace stared at his hand. Felt the heat in his chest expand, spread through his shoulders, down his arms. Felt something fundamental shift in his understanding of what his body could do.
    
    He could see the threads.
    
    Not with his eyes. With something else, some sense he didn't have a name for. Copper-gold filaments connecting him to the column, to the building, to the grid beneath the street. Every connection pulsing with that same internal light, showing him the load distribution, the stress points, the exact moment the eastern column would fail.
    
    Forty-three heartbeats.
    
    *I could hold it,* something in his mind whispered. Not his voice. Something older, something that had been sleeping in his chest and woke when the Piercing opened the seam. *I could pull those threads. Reinforce the column. Give them time to evacuate.*
    
    Jhace had no idea how he knew this. Had no framework for understanding what the threads were or what it meant to pull them. But the heat in his chest *knew*, and the glow on his palm knew, and forty people were going to die if he didn't act.
    
    He pressed both palms against the column.
    
    The heat *erupted*.
    
    Copper-gold light poured from his hands, threading into the stone like liquid metal filling cracks. Jhace felt the connection solidify—felt his body link to the building's structure in a way that made him part of its load-bearing system. The column's weight settled onto his shoulders, into his spine, through his legs into the ground.
    
    He was holding the building.
    
    Not metaphorically. Literally. The eastern column's structural integrity was being supplemented by something pouring out of his chest, through his arms, into the stone. The stress fractures stopped propagating. The grid-hum stabilized. The building's death was suspended, held in abeyance by an impossibility Jhace was manifesting through sheer necessity.
    
    His hands started shaking.
    
    Fine tremor, rapid frequency. Jhace felt it begin in his fingertips and spread up his arms. Not from the weight—the weight felt distant, manageable, distributed through the threads. The tremor came from somewhere else, somewhere deeper. A cost being extracted.
    
    He ignored it. "Evacuate!" he shouted toward the entrance. "You have time now—get everyone out!"
    
    People emerged. Running, stumbling, carrying children. Jhace counted them. Eight. Twelve. Twenty. Thirty-three. The tremor in his hands intensified. His vision sparked with copper-gold light. The heat in his chest felt less like a forge and more like something vital burning away.
    
    Forty people. That was what the woman had said. Forty trapped in the stairwell.
    
    Jhace held. Counted heartbeats. Ninety. One hundred twenty. The column settled more weight onto him with each passing moment, as if the building was learning to lean on this new support and trust it to bear loads the stone alone couldn't handle.
    
    One hundred eighty heartbeats.
    
    The tremor had spread to his shoulders. His breathing came in gasps. The threads connecting him to the column showed stress patterns—copper-gold light flickering, dimming in places, as if the connection was fraying.
    
    "That's everyone!" someone shouted from the doorway. "We're clear!"
    
    Jhace wanted to count them. Wanted to verify. But his vision was too blurred with light, his body too compromised by the tremor. He had to trust they'd counted correctly.
    
    He pulled his hands away from the column.
    
    The disconnection felt like tearing muscle. The threads didn't release cleanly—they stretched, thinned, finally snapped with a sensation Jhace felt in his sternum like a physical blow. The heat in his chest guttered. The copper-gold light faded from his palms.
    
    The column held for another twelve heartbeats.
    
    Then it failed.
    
    The eastern support gave way with a sound like the world cracking. The upper floors collapsed inward, pancaking down exactly as Jhace had predicted, and the building that had stood for sixty Turns ceased to exist in the span of six heartbeats.
    
    Dust billowed outward. Jhace staggered back, still shaking, and sat down hard on the rubble-strewn street. His hands trembled continuously now—not the micro-amplitude from exhaustion, but a sustained tremor that looked like he'd been holding a manifestation for hours instead of three minutes.
    
    Someone knelt beside him. Female. Dark hair tied back. Ink-stained fingers holding a journal. The woman from earlier—the one who'd been measuring the Archive void.
    
    "You held that," she said. Not a question. An observation delivered with clinical precision.
    
    "I don't know what I did." Jhace stared at his hands. The tremor persisted. The copper-gold shimmer had faded, but he could still feel the echo of those threads in his chest. "I just—there were people inside."
    
    "How many did you save?"
    
    "They said everyone evacuated. Forty people."
    
    "I counted twelve who came out." The woman opened her journal. Wrote with rapid strokes. "Do you feel different? Physiologically, I mean. Besides the tremor."
    
    "My chest feels hollow. Like something burned out of it." Jhace tried to make a fist. The tremor made it difficult. "What's happening to me?"
    
    "I don't know. But I'm going to document it." She met his eyes. "My name is Tiffani. I'm an astronomical observer and glassworker. What you just did—holding that building—I felt something similar two hours ago. Heat in the chest, visual phenomenon, sense of connection to structural elements. Mine didn't manifest. Yours did. I need to understand why."
    
    Jhace looked at her journal. Saw pages of dense notation, diagrams, measurements. Saw someone who responded to the impossible by trying to quantify it.
    
    "Jhace Torrins," he said. "Grid inspector. Metalworker. I filed fourteen anomaly reports about the grid before the Piercing. Nobody listened."
    
    "Twenty-three people died at Junction Seven because a structural report was ignored." Tiffani's voice was flat, factual. "I read about it. You filed that report."
    
    "Six months before the collapse. Master Hellik archived it." Jhace flexed his hands. The tremor continued. "I wasn't going to let it happen again."
    
    "And now you can't make your hands stop shaking." Tiffani touched his wrist—clinical contact, measuring pulse. "This is a cost. Manifestation produces measurable physiological changes. We need to document the progression."
    
    "I need to help with the evacuation."
    
    "You need to let me measure your tremor amplitude and establish a baseline before you manifest again."
    
    "I'm not going to—" Jhace stopped. Looked at the collapsed building. At the dust still settling. At the forty people standing in the street, alive because he'd held a column for three minutes. "Before I manifest again?"
    
    "You felt it, didn't you? The heat in your chest. It's still there. Diminished, but present." Tiffani wrote in her journal. "Whatever capacity you accessed, it didn't disappear when you used it. Which means you'll use it again. Which means we need to understand the cost progression before someone dies from manifestation instead of being saved by it."
    
    Jhace stood. His legs shook, but held. The tremor in his hands continued—two millimeters at rest, maybe four when he tried to grip. He could still work. Could still inspect. But his body had changed in some fundamental way, and Tiffani was right: the heat hadn't disappeared. It waited in his chest like a banked forge, ready to ignite again if he needed it.
    
    "Document it then," he said. "But while you're measuring, I'm going to find the next collapse."
    
    "I'll come with you."
    
    "You're an observer, not a rescue worker."
    
    "And you're a grid inspector who just held a building through force of will. We're both working outside our professional parameters now." Tiffani closed her journal. "You need someone to tell you when to stop. To measure the cost and enforce termination criteria. Otherwise you'll manifest until it kills you."
    
    Jhace wanted to argue. But he looked at his shaking hands, felt the hollow ache in his chest, and recognized truth when he heard it.
    
    "What's the termination criterion?" he asked.
    
    "I don't know yet. That's what we're going to establish." Tiffani stood. "But I know it's not 'when everyone is saved.' Because if that's the threshold, you'll never stop, and you'll die doing this."
    
    The bells rang again. Different sequence. Another collapse, six streets east.
    
    Jhace felt the heat in his chest pulse in response. Felt the threads trying to manifest, reaching toward the structural failure even at this distance. His hands shook harder.
    
    "Come on," he said.
    
    They ran toward the sound of breaking stone, toward the screaming, toward the next impossible thing Jhace would have to hold together while his body learned what it cost to manifest.
    
    Behind them, the building on Tallow Street settled into its final configuration of rubble. Twelve people stood alive in the street because Jhace had held the eastern column for one hundred eighty heartbeats.
    
    The other twenty-eight had died in the western collapse before he'd arrived.
    
    Jhace counted them as he ran. Added them to the twenty-three from Junction Seven. Forty-one names he'd carry. Forty-one people he should have saved if he'd been faster, stronger, better at reading the grid's warnings.
    
    The tremor in his hands felt like those names, counted out in shakes per heartbeat.
    
    Tiffani ran beside him, journal clutched to her chest, already writing her next observation without breaking stride.
    
    The partnership formed in motion, built on competence and necessity and the shared recognition that the world had broken and someone needed to document what happened next.
    
    ---
    
    *End Chapter 3*
    

---



## CHAPTER 4: PROTOCOL DEVELOPMENT
    
    Jhace's workshop smelled like metal and copper-gold dust.
    
    The dust had settled everywhere in the eight hours since the Piercing—coating his workbench, his tools, the half-finished bracket assemblies he'd been fabricating before the world broke. Tiffani sat on a stool near the forge, journal open, measuring the tremor in Jhace's hands with a calibrated gauge she'd retrieved from the observatory.
    
    "Two point three millimeters at rest," she said, writing. "Four point seven under stress. That's increased from the initial measurement."
    
    "It's getting worse?" Jhace tried to hold his hand steady. Failed. The tremor persisted, constant and fine, like his muscles had forgotten how to be still.
    
    "Not worse. *Progressing.*" Tiffani adjusted the gauge. "Initial manifestation was eight hours ago. The amplitude has increased point-six millimeters in that period. If the progression is linear, we can project—"
    
    "How do I make it stop?"
    
    Tiffani looked up from her journal. "I don't think you can. This appears to be a permanent cost." She met his eyes. "You manifested. Your body paid a price. The tremor is the visible marker of that exchange."
    
    Jhace flexed his fingers. The tremor continued. He could still grip his tools—could still work, could still inspect—but the fine motor control he'd relied on for precision metalwork had degraded. Not catastrophically. But measurably.
    
    "Twelve people," he said. "Twelve lives for a permanent tremor."
    
    "Is that an acceptable exchange rate?"
    
    "I don't know." Jhace picked up a small wrench. The tremor made it difficult to thread the bolt cleanly. "Ask me when I can't work anymore."
    
    Tiffani wrote in her journal: *Manifestor expresses uncertainty about cost-to-outcome ratio. Functional capacity degraded but not eliminated. Long-term progression unknown.*
    
    "We need more data points," she said. "One manifestation isn't enough to establish a pattern. If you manifest again—"
    
    "*When* I manifest again." Jhace set down the wrench. "There are collapses happening across eight districts. The grid's compromised. Buildings that were barely stable before the Piercing are failing now. I counted fourteen more evacuation bells between the Tallow Street collapse and when I came back here."
    
    "And did you respond to any of them?"
    
    "No. You told me we needed to establish baseline measurements."
    
    "Good." Tiffani made a note. "That's the first protocol: documentation before deployment. We measure your current state, establish what changed from the previous manifestation, project the cost trajectory."
    
    Jhace wanted to argue. Wanted to say that people were dying while they sat in his workshop taking measurements. But he looked at his shaking hands and recognized the logic: if he manifested without understanding the cost, he'd burn through his capacity in a day and be useless. Dead, probably. Tiffani was trying to keep him functional long enough to save more than twelve people.
    
    "What's the second protocol?" he asked.
    
    "Duration limits." Tiffani flipped to a fresh page. "You held that column for one hundred eighty heartbeats. Three minutes. That produced a permanent tremor. If you'd held it longer, would the cost have been worse?"
    
    "I don't know."
    
    "Then we test it. Next manifestation, you hold for ninety heartbeats. Half duration. We measure the resulting cost and compare."
    
    "And if ninety heartbeats isn't enough time to evacuate?"
    
    "Then we deploy multiple manifestors." Tiffani wrote rapidly. "Don't rely on one person to hold an entire structure. Distribute the load. Shorter durations, more operators, coordinated evacuation."
    
    "There are no other manifestors. Just me."
    
    "There's me. I felt the heat. Saw the threads. I didn't manifest, but the capacity is present."
    
    "You're not manifesting." Jhace's voice came out harder than intended. "Not until we understand the cost progression. I'm not watching you acquire a permanent tremor because I needed backup."
    
    Tiffani looked at him for a long moment. "That's the third protocol, then. Someone has to remain uncompromised. Someone has to document without paying the manifestation cost. Otherwise we're just burning through operators with no one tracking what happens."
    
    The workshop door opened. Bram entered—broad-shouldered, Guild foreman's colors, face grim. He looked at Jhace's hands, at the tremor, at Tiffani's journal.
    
    "Crown's asking questions," Bram said without preamble. "About the man who held the building on Tallow Street. About whether he's Guild-sanctioned, whether he's operating under Crown license, whether the Temple has jurisdiction over manifestation."
    
    "I'm a grid inspector who kept a building from collapsing," Jhace said. "There's nothing to sanction."
    
    "Crown doesn't see it that way. They want control. Registration, deployment protocols, institutional oversight." Bram crossed his arms. "Master Hellik is claiming you violated structural safety codes by interfering with a natural collapse progression."
    
    "Natural? The Piercing compromised the entire grid. Those collapses are—"
    
    "I know what they are. I read your reports. Fourteen anomalies, all documented, all ignored." Bram's expression softened slightly. "I'm not here to enforce Crown policy. I'm here to tell you that if you keep manifesting without institutional cover, they'll shut you down. Arrest you, probably. Can't have unregulated manifestors running around Saint Merro saving lives without proper authorization."
    
    Tiffani closed her journal. "What do we need to do to get authorization?"
    
    "Register. Submit to Crown documentation. Agree to deployment only when Crown approves the crisis." Bram pulled a folded document from his coat. "I brought the forms. You sign, they'll license you. You don't sign, they'll arrest you next time you manifest."
    
    Jhace took the document. Read through it. Standard Crown bureaucracy: name, profession, manifestation capacity (unknown), cost markers (permanent tremor, stage one), deployment restrictions (Crown-approved crises only).
    
    "This says I can only manifest when Crown gives permission," Jhace said. "What about residential collapses? Personal emergencies? Situations where waiting for authorization means people die?"
    
    "Then people die." Bram's voice was flat. "Crown wants order. Wants manifestation under institutional control. Anything else threatens their authority structure."
    
    Tiffani stood. "If we don't sign, they arrest Jhace. If we do sign, we're constrained by Crown bureaucracy that will delay response and increase casualties. Neither option is acceptable."
    
    "Those are the options you have."
    
    "No." Tiffani opened her journal to the page where she'd outlined the protocols. "There's a third option. We document everything. Every manifestation, every cost, every life saved. We create a methodology that's so systematically sound that Crown has to acknowledge it. We make ourselves indispensable through competence, not compliance."
    
    Bram raised an eyebrow. "You think Crown cares about competence?"
    
    "I think Crown cares about looking incompetent when buildings collapse and manifestors save lives without needing institutional permission." Tiffani met his eyes. "If we establish the standard—if our methodology becomes the reference everyone uses—Crown will adopt it because not adopting it makes them look weak."
    
    Jhace looked at her. At the journal filled with measurements and protocols. At the woman who'd responded to the impossible by building a framework to contain it.
    
    "You want to create documentation that becomes institutional policy," he said.
    
    "I want to create documentation that saves lives. If it becomes institutional policy, that's a side effect." Tiffani turned to Bram. "Can you give us time? Keep Crown from arresting Jhace while we establish the methodology?"
    
    "Maybe. If you register." Bram tapped the document. "Give them the paperwork. Let them think they have control. Build your methodology underneath that compliance layer."
    
    "And when Crown tries to restrict deployment?"
    
    "Document every person who dies because you weren't allowed to manifest. Make the cost of institutional control visible." Bram's expression was grim. "Crown wants order. Show them what their order costs in bodies."
    
    Jhace signed the document. Hated doing it. But Bram was right: he couldn't save anyone from a Crown detention cell.
    
    Tiffani made a note in her journal: *Tide 0, Shaping, eighth bell. Manifestor registration completed under institutional coercion. Deployment now subject to Crown approval. Cost: reduced operational autonomy. Benefit: continued function.*
    
    "What now?" Jhace asked.
    
    "Now we establish the rotation protocol." Tiffani pulled out her measuring tape. "Because you're not the only person in Saint Merro who can manifest. You're just the first one who did it and survived long enough to be measured."
    
    "How do you know there are others?"
    
    "Because I felt the heat. Because Chloen showed contact-dependent tremor before she ever manifested. Because the Piercing opened something fundamental, and copper-gold dust is falling across the entire city." Tiffani wrote with rapid precision. "We need to find them before they manifest without protocol. Before they burn out in a single deployment and die because no one told them about duration limits."
    
    Bram shifted his weight. "Guild has three workers showing tremor symptoms. Started yesterday. I sent them home, told them to rest. If they're pre-manifestation markers—"
    
    "They are." Tiffani stood. "Where are they?"
    
    "Grain warehouse district. I can take you."
    
    "Do it." Tiffani looked at Jhace. "You stay here. Rest. Let the tremor stabilize. We measure you again in six hours."
    
    "I should—"
    
    "You should let me recruit the next manifestors while you recover. That's the protocol: rotate operators, prevent burnout, distribute the cost across multiple people so no one person carries it all." Tiffani's voice was firm. "Trust the system. Even when it means sitting out the next crisis."
    
    Jhace wanted to argue. But his hands were shaking, his chest felt hollow, and Tiffani was right: if he manifested again immediately, before understanding the cost progression, he'd compromise himself faster.
    
    "Six hours," he said. "Then we measure again."
    
    "Six hours," Tiffani agreed.
    
    She left with Bram, journal under her arm, already planning the recruitment protocol. Jhace sat alone in his workshop, surrounded by copper-gold dust and unfinished projects, and counted the tremor frequency in his hands.
    
    Two point three millimeters at rest. Four point seven under stress.
    
    Twelve lives saved.
    
    One body permanently marked.
    
    The exchange rate documented in shaking flesh and Tiffani's clinical notation.
    
    Outside, the evacuation bells rang again. Jhace counted them. Listened to the pattern. Residential district, northern terrace, immediate priority.
    
    He stood. Looked at his hands. The tremor continued.
    
    Six hours, Tiffani had said. Trust the system. Let someone else respond.
    
    Jhace sat back down. Picked up the wrench. Tried to thread the bolt with shaking hands while people died six streets north because he was following protocol instead of running toward the collapse.
    
    The cost of methodology felt heavier than the cost of manifestation.
    
    He threaded the bolt. The tremor made it difficult, but not impossible.
    
    Twelve lives for permanent tremor.
    
    How many more lives for the discipline to wait?
    
    Jhace counted heartbeats and trusted that Tiffani's system would save more people than his impatience would kill.
    
    ---
    
    *End Chapter 4*
    

---



## CHAPTER 5: FIRST NETWORK
    
    The grain warehouse collapsed at the ninth bell of Tide 1.
    
    Jhace felt it through the grid before the evacuation bells rang—a sustained vibration in the wrong frequency, propagating from the commercial district. He was in Tiffani's makeshift documentation center, a converted observatory room she'd requisitioned through Bram's Guild connections, when the tremor in his hands spiked to five millimeters.
    
    "That's not stress response," Tiffani said, looking up from her measurements. "That's resonance. You're detecting a structural failure."
    
    "Grain warehouse. Eastern commercial district." Jhace stood. The heat in his chest pulsed in response to the grid's distress. "Big building. Maybe three hundred people working day shift."
    
    "You're not going alone." Tiffani grabbed her journal and a bell she'd started carrying—three rapid rings meant terminate manifestation immediately. "Corven's been waiting for deployment."
    
    Corven was one of the three Guild workers Tiffani had recruited two days ago. Tremor symptoms, contact-dependent heat response, pre-manifestation markers identical to what Jhace had experienced. He'd manifested once under controlled conditions—held a test beam for sixty heartbeats, acquired a minor tremor, proved he could access the threads.
    
    They found him in the warehouse district, already running toward the collapse.
    
    "I felt it," Corven said when they caught up. "Through the grid-hum. Like the whole structure was screaming."
    
    "That's the doubled harmonic." Jhace assessed the building. The eastern wall had buckled, but the main support columns still held. "Load redistribution pattern suggests the central column will fail next. Maybe two hundred heartbeats before catastrophic collapse."
    
    "How many inside?" Tiffani asked.
    
    A foreman emerged from the chaos of evacuating workers. "We got most of first shift out, but second shift was coming in. Maybe two hundred twenty people still inside, maybe more. The western stairwell collapsed—they're trapped on the upper storage level."
    
    Two hundred twenty people. Two hundred heartbeats. The math didn't work with a single manifestor.
    
    "Rotation protocol," Tiffani said. "Jhace takes the central column, Corven takes the eastern beam. I'll coordinate evacuation and monitor duration."
    
    "I haven't held anything that big," Corven said. His voice was steady, but Jhace saw the fear underneath. First real deployment. First time lives depended on his capacity.
    
    "You held the test beam for sixty heartbeats with minimal cost," Jhace said. "This is the same principle. Connect to the structure, reinforce the stressed points, hold until Tiffani rings the bell."
    
    "And if I can't?"
    
    "Then I extend my duration and we both pay higher costs." Jhace met his eyes. "But you can. Trust your capacity."
    
    They approached the warehouse. The central column showed stress fractures radiating from the base—same pattern as the Tallow Street building, same imminent failure indicators. Jhace pressed his palms against the stone. Felt the heat in his chest ignite immediately.
    
    The threads manifested. Copper-gold filaments connecting him to the column, to the grid, to the building's entire structural system. The load settled onto his shoulders with familiar weight.
    
    Thirty strides away, Corven pressed his hands against the eastern beam. Light poured from his palms—brighter than Jhace's, less controlled, but effective. The beam's stress fractures stopped propagating.
    
    "Evacuate!" Tiffani shouted toward the warehouse entrance. "You have time—move quickly, stay organized!"
    
    People poured out. Jhace counted them while holding the column. Twenty. Forty. Sixty. His tremor intensified—five millimeters at rest becoming seven under manifestation load. The hollow feeling in his chest deepened. He'd held for maybe ninety heartbeats.
    
    "How's Corven?" he called to Tiffani.
    
    She was watching the other manifestor with clinical focus. "Tremor present but stable. Breathing compromised but manageable. He's holding."
    
    One hundred heartbeats.
    
    The evacuation continued. Eighty people out. One hundred. The warehouse foreman appeared in the doorway. "Upper level's clear except for the northwest storage room—door's jammed, maybe twenty people trapped!"
    
    "How long to break through?" Tiffani asked.
    
    "Five minutes with proper tools."
    
    Five minutes was three hundred heartbeats. Jhace had been holding for one hundred twenty. Corven for one hundred ten. The protocol said terminate at one hundred eighty heartbeats maximum duration.
    
    "We extend," Jhace said.
    
    "That violates the duration limit." Tiffani's voice was sharp. "You terminate at one eighty, we get the tools, we evacuate the trapped workers after the building stabilizes."
    
    "The building won't stabilize. The central column is compromised at the foundation. If we release now, it fails within thirty heartbeats." Jhace felt the threads fraying at the edges, stress concentrating where his manifestation met the stone's natural structure. "We hold until everyone's out."
    
    "That's not the protocol."
    
    "The protocol assumes we can evacuate and return. This is a one-chance deployment." Jhace's hands shook harder. Seven millimeters becoming nine. "Twenty people die if we follow protocol. Zero die if we extend duration. Make the call."
    
    Tiffani stared at him. At Corven, whose light was starting to flicker. At the warehouse foreman who was already running back inside with a crowbar.
    
    She didn't ring the bell.
    
    One hundred fifty heartbeats.
    
    Jhace's vision sparked with copper-gold light. The hollow feeling in his chest had progressed to actual pain—not muscle pain, but something deeper, like his capacity was burning away to sustain the manifestation. His tremor hit ten millimeters. He could barely feel his hands anymore, just the threads connecting him to the column.
    
    "Corven's at Stage Two threshold," Tiffani called. "Vein-lines developing on his forearms."
    
    "Tell him to hold." Jhace gritted his teeth. His own vein-lines had appeared after the second manifestation, thin copper-gold traces visible through his skin. Corven was acquiring his in a single extended deployment. Higher cost for the same duration.
    
    One hundred eighty heartbeats.
    
    The foreman emerged with fifteen people. "Five more inside—they're coming!"
    
    "Terminate," Tiffani said. "Both of you. Now."
    
    "Thirty more heartbeats," Jhace said.
    
    "Jhace—"
    
    "*Thirty heartbeats.*"
    
    Corven's light flickered again. The eastern beam shifted slightly—not collapse, but redistribution. His manifestation was failing.
    
    "Corven, release!" Jhace shouted. "I'll take both loads!"
    
    "You can't—" Tiffani started.
    
    Corven pulled his hands away from the beam. The threads snapped. The eastern support's load transferred immediately to the central column, to Jhace's manifestation, doubling the weight on his shoulders.
    
    Jhace's knees buckled. The heat in his chest felt like it would burn through his ribs. The tremor in his hands hit twelve millimeters—visible shaking, loss of fine motor control, approaching the threshold Tiffani had marked as Stage Two.
    
    The last five workers ran out of the warehouse.
    
    "Clear!" the foreman shouted.
    
    Jhace released.
    
    The disconnection hurt worse than the first time. The threads tore out of the column like pulling tendons, and the backflash hit his chest hard enough to drop him to his knees. The heat guttered. The copper-gold light faded from his palms.
    
    The central column held for eight heartbeats.
    
    Then it failed, and the warehouse collapsed inward with the same catastrophic finality as the Tallow Street building.
    
    Tiffani knelt beside Jhace. "Two hundred nineteen people evacuated. Zero casualties. You held for two hundred twelve heartbeats." She pulled out her measuring gauge. "Let me see your hands."
    
    Jhace held them up. The tremor was violent—twelve millimeters at rest, barely able to form a fist. The vein-lines on his forearms had spread to his wrists, glowing faintly even in daylight.
    
    "Stage Two markers," Tiffani said, writing. "Severe tremor, vein-line progression, respiratory compromise. You exceeded duration limits by thirty-two heartbeats." She met his eyes. "You violated protocol."
    
    "Twenty people would have died."
    
    "And you've compromised your long-term capacity to save them. The cost progression is accelerating." She measured Corven next. He was sitting against a wall, breathing hard, staring at his hands. The tremor was less severe than Jhace's—maybe eight millimeters—but the vein-lines were present. Copper-gold traces threading through his forearms like he'd been marked by molten metal.
    
    "I held it," Corven said. "I actually held it."
    
    "You did." Tiffani noted his measurements. "And now you have permanent vein-lines and a sustained tremor. Welcome to the network."
    
    Jhace stood on shaking legs. Looked at the collapsed warehouse. At the two hundred nineteen people standing in the street, alive because two manifestors had held stressed structures past recommended limits.
    
    The foreman approached. "I need to file a report with Crown. Manifestor deployment, casualty count, structural damage assessment." He looked uncomfortable. "They're asking for your names. For the registry."
    
    "Jhace Torrins and Corven..." Tiffani looked at Corven.
    
    "Reld," Corven supplied. "Corven Reld. Guild metalworker."
    
    The foreman wrote it down. "Crown's going to want to interview you. Administrator Corlexi is reviewing all post-Piercing structural documentation. He'll probably want your manifestation reports."
    
    Jhace had heard that name. Corlexi. Infrastructure Compliance Office. The bureaucrat who'd actually responded to the Piercing with something resembling organized crisis management.
    
    "We'll file the reports," Tiffani said. "Complete documentation. Deployment duration, cost progression, lives saved. Everything Corlexi needs to understand manifestation mechanics."
    
    The foreman left. Tiffani opened her journal to a fresh page.
    
    "Tide 1, Kindling, ninth bell. Grain warehouse collapse. Two manifestors deployed, coordinated hold, two hundred nineteen lives saved. Jhace: two hundred twelve heartbeats, Stage Two cost progression. Corven: one hundred ninety heartbeats, Stage One progressing to Stage Two. Protocol violation: duration exceeded recommended limits by thirty-two heartbeats. Outcome: zero casualties, two operators compromised."
    
    She looked at both of them. "This is what rotation protocol prevents. If we'd had four manifestors, each could have held for ninety heartbeats. Lower individual cost, same outcome. We need more operators."
    
    "You're not manifesting," Jhace said.
    
    "I know. But there are others. Chloen showed contact tremor. There are probably fifty people in Saint Merro with pre-manifestation markers." Tiffani stood. "We find them. We train them. We build a network that can deploy without burning out individual operators."
    
    Corven flexed his hands. Watched the tremor shake his fingers. "How long until this stops?"
    
    "It doesn't," Jhace said. "This is permanent. You paid the cost. Now you live with it."
    
    "Was it worth it?"
    
    Jhace looked at the two hundred nineteen people being processed through the evacuation checkpoint. Looked at his own hands, shaking at twelve millimeters, vein-lines glowing faintly through his skin.
    
    "Ask me in six months when I can't hold a wrench anymore."
    
    They walked back to the documentation center. Tiffani measured them every thirty strides, tracking the cost progression as their bodies adjusted to the new baseline. By the time they arrived, Jhace's tremor had stabilized at ten millimeters. Corven's at seven.
    
    Both sets of vein-lines glowed brighter in the dimmer light of the converted observatory.
    
    Tiffani filed the manifestation report with Crown Infrastructure Office that evening. Detailed documentation: duration, cost, outcome, methodology. She included her protocol recommendations: rotation deployment, duration limits, termination criteria.
    
    Three days later, a Crown courier delivered a response. Official letterhead, neat handwriting, signed at the bottom: *Administrator Corlexi, Infrastructure Compliance Office.*
    
    The letter said: *Manifestor registry protocols approved. Duration limits established at 180 heartbeats maximum per deployment. Rotation requirements: minimum two operators per crisis event. Cost documentation mandatory. All manifestors must report to Infrastructure Office for baseline assessment. Further deployments subject to Crown approval.*
    
    "They took your protocols," Jhace said, reading over Tiffani's shoulder.
    
    "They took the structure. Added institutional control." Tiffani set the letter aside. "But the methodology holds. Duration limits, rotation, documentation. That's what saves lives, not Crown approval."
    
    "Think Corlexi understands that?"
    
    "I think Corlexi understands systems. Whether he understands people is a different question." She opened her journal. "Next deployment, we follow the one-eighty limit. No exceptions. We prove the protocol works within its own constraints."
    
    Jhace looked at his hands. Ten millimeters. Permanent. The price of saving twenty people beyond the protocol's recommendation.
    
    "What if the protocol's constraints mean people die?" he asked.
    
    "Then we document the deaths and refine the protocol. But we don't compromise operators trying to save everyone. That path leads to dead manifestors and zero long-term capacity."
    
    She was right. Jhace knew she was right. But the heat in his chest pulsed every time evacuation bells rang, and the threads tried to manifest even when he wasn't touching stressed structures, and somewhere in his body was the hunger to hold more, save more, manifest until his capacity was completely exhausted.
    
    Tiffani saw it in his face. "That's why you need me. To ring the bell when you won't terminate yourself."
    
    "And who terminates you when you finally manifest?"
    
    "You will. If you're still functional." She closed her journal. "That's the network. We hold each other accountable. We enforce the limits we can't enforce on ourselves."
    
    Outside, the seam in the sky continued to pour copper-gold light across Saint Merro. The Piercing site glowed like a wound that wouldn't close. And in eight districts, buildings compromised by resonance effects waited to collapse.
    
    The network had two operators, one documentarian, and protocols that violated themselves when the math said people would die.
    
    Jhace counted his tremor frequency and wondered how many more times he'd violate duration limits before the cost made it impossible.
    
    ---
    
    *End Chapter 5*
    

---



## CHAPTER 6: THE SHARD
    
    Brother Corran found them on the third day of Tide 10, in the documentation center that had become their permanent headquarters.
    
    He arrived without announcement, wearing Crown investigator colors—dark grey with the Archive seal embroidered on the shoulder. Tall, lean, with the kind of methodical gaze that suggested he counted everything he saw. He stood in the doorway for three heartbeats before speaking, observing Jhace's tremor, Tiffani's ink-stained fingers, the walls covered in manifestation data.
    
    "You're documenting it," Corran said. Not a question.
    
    Tiffani looked up from her journal. "Brother Corran. Crown Archives, Investigation Division. You've been watching our deployments."
    
    "For eight days. Twelve manifestations across four operators. Two hundred seventy-three lives saved. Three operators now showing Stage Two cost markers." He entered the room, moved to the wall where Tiffani had posted the cost progression charts. "You're building a methodology."
    
    "We're trying not to die while saving people," Jhace said. His tremor had stabilized at ten millimeters over the past nine days, but the vein-lines continued to spread. They covered his forearms now, visible even through his work shirt, glowing faintly in dim light.
    
    "The trying-not-to-die part is what interests me." Corran pulled a folder from his coat. "I have data you need. Stage Three case studies from the coastal cities. Manifestors who exceeded safe duration limits and crossed the autonomic failure threshold."
    
    Tiffani stood immediately. "How many cases?"
    
    "Forty-seven documented over the past century. Manifestation was rarer before the Piercing, but it happened. Usually during natural disasters—earthquakes, floods, structural collapses." Corran opened the folder. "Mortality rate at Stage Three: ninety-seven percent. Of the three survivors, all experienced severe permanent disability. Paralysis, respiratory failure requiring mechanical support, cognitive impairment."
    
    He laid out photographs on the table. Jhace didn't want to look, but couldn't stop himself. Manifestors in the final stages—bodies marked with vein-lines so extensive they looked like copper-gold had replaced their entire circulatory system. Tremors so severe they couldn't hold themselves upright. Eyes with metallic traces that suggested something fundamental had changed in their neural structure.
    
    "This is what happens when you exceed the threshold," Corran said. "When manifestation duration or intensity pushes past what the human body can sustain. You don't just acquire tremor or vein-lines. Your autonomic nervous system starts to fail. Breathing becomes voluntary. Heart rhythm requires conscious maintenance. Eventually, the body forgets how to be alive without active mental control."
    
    Tiffani was writing rapidly. "What's the threshold? Exact duration where Stage Two becomes Stage Three?"
    
    "It varies by individual capacity. But the pattern is consistent: third manifestation shows accelerated cost. Fourth manifestation approaches Stage Three regardless of duration. Fifth manifestation is almost always fatal." Corran met Jhace's eyes. "You've manifested twice. Corven Reld has manifested three times. Your other two operators—Senne and Marict—once each. You need to understand the progression before someone crosses into Stage Three during deployment."
    
    "Why are you telling us this?" Jhace asked. "Crown wants manifestation under institutional control. This data could help them restrict deployment, claim it's too dangerous to allow."
    
    "Crown wants many things. Some of us in the Archives want truth preserved, regardless of political convenience." Corran gestured to the documentation on the walls. "Your methodology is sound. Duration limits, rotation protocols, termination criteria. You're doing what the coastal cities never managed—systematic operation that respects capacity constraints. I'm here to make sure you have the data to keep doing it."
    
    "And if Crown orders you to shut us down?"
    
    "Then I'll document that too. But Administrator Corlexi is more interested in functional systems than institutional suppression. He's the one who approved your protocols. He's the one establishing the Manifestor Registry as official Crown policy." Corran pulled out another document. "This was posted in Crown Hall this morning."
    
    Tiffani read it aloud: "*Manifestor Registry established by order of Infrastructure Compliance Office, Administrator Corlexi presiding. All individuals showing manifestation capacity must register with Crown Archives. Deployment protocols: maximum duration 180 heartbeats, minimum two operators per crisis, mandatory cost documentation. Registry documentation available at Crown Hall, Western District Administrative Office.*"
    
    "He made it official," Jhace said.
    
    "He made *your system* official," Corran corrected. "The duration limits, the rotation requirements, the documentation standards—those came from your reports. Corlexi recognized functional methodology and institutionalized it. Whether that's a gift or a cage depends on how you use it."
    
    Tiffani set down the announcement. "What do you want from us?"
    
    "Continued documentation. Ground-truth data on manifestation mechanics, cost progression, threshold boundaries. The Archives are the institutional memory of Crown authority. What you document now becomes the reference for every future manifestor." Corran's expression was serious. "Saint Merro isn't the only city affected by the Piercing. The coastal cities are reporting manifestation events. The northern territories. Even Torne Reach, where they haven't had a manifestor in living memory. This is a planetary phenomenon. Your methodology could save thousands of lives if it's documented properly."
    
    "Or kill thousands if Crown uses it to restrict manifestation when people need help," Jhace said.
    
    "That's the risk of institutionalization. But the alternative is chaos—manifestors operating without framework, burning out in days, dying without anyone learning from their costs." Corran gathered his photographs. "You have a choice. Document for Crown, accept institutional constraints, gain access to resources and protection. Or operate independently, retain autonomy, lose institutional support when Crown decides you're a threat."
    
    "Those aren't good options," Tiffani said.
    
    "No. But they're the options you have." Corran moved toward the door. "I'll be observing your next deployment. Not to enforce Crown policy—to witness. To document what happens when systematic methodology meets actual crisis. If you succeed, I'll file reports that support your approach. If you fail, I'll document that too."
    
    He left. Jhace and Tiffani sat in silence, staring at the Stage Three photographs.
    
    "Fourth manifestation approaches Stage Three regardless of duration," Tiffani finally said. "That means you have two more manifestations before you're at catastrophic risk. Corven has one."
    
    "We need more operators." Jhace flexed his hands. The tremor continued, constant and fine. "Distribute the deployments across more people so no one hits fourth manifestation."
    
    "I found three more pre-manifestation candidates. Chloen finally agreed to training. That gives us seven potential operators." Tiffani made notes. "If we rotate properly, keep individual deployment frequency low, we can extend everyone's operational lifespan."
    
    "Until crisis frequency exceeds rotation capacity and someone has to manifest for the fourth time anyway."
    
    "Then we document it. We measure the cost. We learn what Stage Three actually looks like in real-time." Tiffani's voice was clinical, but Jhace heard the strain underneath. "This is the work. Keeping people alive as long as possible, documenting what happens when we can't."
    
    Jhace looked at the photographs again. At manifestors who'd crossed the threshold and paid with their autonomic nervous systems. At bodies that had forgotten how to be alive.
    
    "I'm at two manifestations," he said. "If fourth is the danger point, I have two more deployments before I'm done."
    
    "Then we make them count. We use you strategically. Save you for crises where your experience makes the difference." Tiffani opened her journal to a fresh page. "New protocol: experienced operators deploy only when rotation capacity is exceeded or crisis complexity requires it. New manifestors handle standard deployments. We preserve your capacity for when methodology alone isn't enough."
    
    "That assumes I'll follow protocol."
    
    "You will. Because I'll enforce it." She met his eyes. "You trusted me to ring the bell. Now trust me to keep you off the deployment roster when someone less experienced can do the work."
    
    The evacuation bells rang. Residential district, eastern terrace. Standard collapse pattern.
    
    Jhace felt the heat pulse in his chest. Felt the threads trying to manifest. His tremor spiked to twelve millimeters.
    
    "Senne and Marict are on rotation," Tiffani said, checking her schedule. "They deploy. You stay here."
    
    "I should—"
    
    "You should let the protocol work." She stood, grabbed her bell and journal. "I'll coordinate. You document. That's the new division of labor."
    
    She left. Jhace sat alone in the documentation center, surrounded by cost progression charts and Stage Three photographs, while the heat in his chest burned and someone else ran toward the collapse.
    
    This was the discipline Tiffani had been building toward: the ability to not manifest when manifestation was possible. To trust the network to function without him. To preserve his capacity instead of spending it every time the bells rang.
    
    Jhace counted heartbeats. Listened to the evacuation bells fade. The tremor in his hands gradually decreased from twelve millimeters back to ten as the immediate crisis moved beyond his sensory range.
    
    Two more manifestations before Stage Three risk.
    
    Maybe four hundred more heartbeats of holding structures before his body forgot how to be alive.
    
    The exchange rate documented in clinical notation and Brother Corran's cautionary photographs.
    
    Jhace opened Tiffani's journal to the page where she'd outlined the new protocols. Read her handwriting—precise, methodical, absolutely certain that systematic operation could beat individual heroics.
    
    He wanted to believe her.
    
    The tremor in his hands suggested his body had other opinions.
    
    ---
    
    Three hours later, Tiffani returned. Senne and Marict had held a residential collapse for one hundred sixty heartbeats. Twenty-seven people evacuated. Zero casualties. Both operators acquired minor tremor increases but stayed well below Stage Two threshold.
    
    "The protocol worked," Tiffani said. "Standard deployment, standard outcome, two less-experienced operators proving they can function without you."
    
    "Good," Jhace said. Meant it. But the heat in his chest had pulsed the entire three hours, wanting to manifest, wanting to hold something, and the discipline of staying in the documentation center felt harder than any physical load.
