    "Brother Corran filed his observation report," Tiffani added. "Sent it to Administrator Corlexi with a recommendation to expand manifestor training programs. He's advocating for our methodology at the institutional level."
    
    "Think Corlexi will listen?"
    
    "I think Corlexi listens to functional systems. If our protocols keep producing results, he'll support them. If they fail, he'll restrict them." She sat down, exhausted. "That's the bargain. We prove methodology beats chaos, Crown gives us operational latitude. We fail to prove it, Crown shuts us down."
    
    "No pressure."
    
    "Infinite pressure. But that's why we document everything." Tiffani opened her journal. "Next manifestation, we measure Corven. He's at three deployments. If he shows accelerated cost progression, we pull him from rotation before he approaches fourth manifestation."
    
    "And if he refuses? If he wants to keep manifesting?"
    
    "Then I ring the bell and enforce termination whether he agrees or not. That's the network. We protect each other from our own hunger to do more than our bodies can sustain."
    
    Jhace looked at his hands. Ten millimeters. Permanent. The price of two manifestations and three hundred sixty heartbeats of holding structures that wanted to collapse.
    
    Two more manifestations before catastrophic risk.
    
    He counted them like a countdown. Like names on a plaque. Like heartbeats in a hold that would eventually end with his autonomic nervous system forgetting how to function.
    
    The methodology said he could make those two manifestations count for something.
    
    The tremor in his hands said his body was already learning what it cost to hold the world together.
    
    ---
    
    *End Chapter 6*
    

---



## CHAPTER 7: CRISIS POINT
    
    The Merchant Hall collapsed at the fourth bell of Tide 7.
    
    Four hundred people inside. Eight manifestors deployed. Tiffani coordinating from the command position she'd established thirty strides from the primary support column. This was the largest crisis since the Piercing—a building that served as commercial hub, administrative center, and residential complex. When the resonance effects finally compromised its foundation, the entire eastern district felt the grid-hum drop.
    
    Jhace held the central column. Corven and Senne held the eastern supports. Marict, Chloen, and three newer operators—Daved, Renne, and Solk—held perimeter beams. Eight manifestors, coordinated deployment, strict duration limits.
    
    Tiffani had planned this for twelve days. Rotation sequences, backup positions, evacuation routes. The Merchant Hall had been on her watch list since Tide 3—stress fractures visible, grid-hum doubled, copper-gold dust accumulating in the foundation joints. She'd filed reports with Crown Infrastructure Office, recommended preemptive evacuation, been told the building was scheduled for assessment in six months.
    
    It lasted six days.
    
    "Primary evacuation route clear," Tiffani called. "Three hundred twelve people out. Eighty-eight still inside, concentrated on upper floors."
    
    Jhace's hands shook at fifteen millimeters. This was his third manifestation since the grain warehouse. Each one pushing him closer to the fourth deployment threshold. But the Merchant Hall required experienced operators, and he was the only one who'd held anything this massive.
    
    "Duration check," Tiffani shouted. "Call out your heartbeats!"
    
    "One hundred forty!" Jhace.
    
    "One thirty-five!" Corven.
    
    "One twenty!" Senne.
    
    The newer operators called lower numbers. Daved—holding a critical support on the northwestern corner—called one hundred ten.
    
    They had seventy heartbeats before mandatory termination. Forty heartbeats before recommended termination. The evacuation was proceeding, but not fast enough.
    
    "Upper floor access is blocked," someone shouted from the evacuation coordination point. "Stairwell collapsed—fifty people trapped on the third level!"
    
    Fifty people. Forty heartbeats. The math didn't work.
    
    "How long to clear the blockage?" Tiffani asked, her voice steady despite the calculation running in her head.
    
    "Ten minutes with proper equipment. Maybe fifteen."
    
    Fifteen minutes was nine hundred heartbeats. They had forty.
    
    "Terminate in sequence," Tiffani ordered. "Perimeter operators first, work inward. We release the building, get equipment, clear the blockage, extract the trapped workers afterward."
    
    "The building won't hold afterward," Jhace said. His voice was strained, the column's weight crushing down through the threads he'd manifested into the stone. "The central column is compromised at three points. When we release, it fails within sixty heartbeats. Everyone on the third floor dies."
    
    "And if we extend duration, we push operators past Stage Two thresholds." Tiffani looked at her cost progression charts—the documentation she'd been maintaining for every operator, every manifestation. "Corven's at three deployments. Daved's at two. Extension puts them both at catastrophic risk."
    
    "Fifty people," Jhace said.
    
    Tiffani closed her eyes. Ran the calculation again. Same answer. Terminate now, save the three hundred twelve already evacuated, lose fifty trapped on the third floor. Extend duration, save all three hundred sixty-two, potentially kill two manifestors through threshold crossing.
    
    Expected value said terminate. Methodology said enforce the limits. Protocol said ring the bell and let the building fail.
    
    She looked at the Merchant Hall. At the people visible through third-floor windows, waving, shouting, trapped behind rubble they couldn't clear in time.
    
    "Perimeter operators terminate," she called. "Marict, Chloen, Renne, Solk—release on my mark. Three, two, one, mark."
    
    Four sets of threads snapped. Four operators staggered back, cost paid, tremor acquired or amplified. The building's load redistributed to the remaining four manifestors. Jhace's knees buckled before he stabilized. Corven's light flickered. Senne's breathing became labored. Daved's vein-lines, already visible through his shirt, started glowing brighter.
    
    "Central operators hold," Tiffani said. "Duration extension authorized. Breakthrough team, get that stairwell clear. You have nine hundred heartbeats."
    
    "Tiffani—" Brother Corran was there, observing, documenting. "Extension violates protocol. You're pushing them past safe limits."
    
    "I know what I'm doing."
    
    "Do you? Because what you're doing is choosing between fifty lives now and potentially more lives later if you burn out your experienced operators."
    
    "I'm choosing to save everyone I can with the capacity I have." Tiffani rang her bell—not the termination sequence, but the extension warning. Three slow rings. "Operators, acknowledge extension order."
    
    "Acknowledged," Jhace said through gritted teeth.
    
    "Acknowledged," Corven and Senne echoed.
    
    "Acknowledged," Daved called from his position.
    
    One hundred eighty heartbeats passed. The breakthrough team was halfway through the rubble. The trapped workers on the third floor had started organized evacuation prep—clearly visible through the windows now.
    
    Two hundred heartbeats. Jhace's tremor hit eighteen millimeters. His vein-lines spread across his chest, glowing bright enough to cast shadows. He was approaching Stage Three markers. One more manifestation after this and he'd be at catastrophic risk.
    
    Two hundred twenty heartbeats. Corven started coughing. Respiratory compromise. Stage Two progressing to Stage Two-plus. Senne's legs gave out—she was manifesting from her knees now, hands pressed to the beam, light pouring from her palms in unstable pulses.
    
    Two hundred forty heartbeats. Daved's light changed.
    
    Tiffani saw it happen. The copper-gold glow that had been steady and controlled suddenly intensified, became almost white-hot at the edges. Daved's entire body started shaking—not fine tremor, but violent convulsions. His vein-lines spread in real-time, threading across his shoulders, up his neck, across his face.
    
    "Daved's at threshold!" Tiffani shouted. "Terminate immediately!"
    
    "Can't!" Daved's voice was barely recognizable. "Locked in—can't release—"
    
    Locked. That was the Stage Three indicator Brother Corran had warned about. When manifestation became involuntary. When the threads wouldn't release even when the manifestor tried to disconnect.
    
    "Breakthrough complete!" the evacuation team shouted. "Third floor accessible!"
    
    People poured down the cleared stairwell. Tiffani counted them. Twenty. Thirty. Forty-five. Fifty.
    
    "All clear!" the team leader called.
    
    "Terminate!" Tiffani rang the bell three times rapidly. "Everyone release, now!"
    
    Jhace, Corven, and Senne pulled away from their support points. The threads snapped. All three staggered, fell, cost extracted brutally.
    
    Daved couldn't release.
    
    His hands were fused to the beam—not physically, but through threads that had become something more than temporary manifestation. The copper-gold light poured from him in a continuous flow, and his body shook so hard Tiffani could see it from thirty strides away.
    
    "Daved, release!" she shouted.
    
    "Trying—can't—it won't let go—"
    
    Brother Corran was already running toward him. "Stage Three lock. The manifestation is sustaining itself. His autonomic nervous system is being overridden."
    
    "How do we break it?"
    
    "You don't. It breaks when his capacity is completely exhausted or when the structure he's holding fails." Corran reached Daved's position. "We need to force structural failure. Collapse the beam he's holding so the manifestation has nothing to sustain itself on."
    
    "That could kill him!"
    
    "Staying locked will definitely kill him. This gives him a chance." Corran looked at the beam. "How much weight is it bearing?"
    
    "Enough that if it fails, the northwestern corner collapses."
    
    "Is that section clear?"
    
    Tiffani checked the evacuation status. "Yes. Everyone's out of that quadrant."
    
    "Then we collapse it intentionally." Corran pulled a prybar from the breakthrough equipment. "Help me compromise the base support."
    
    They worked fast. Tiffani, Corran, and two evacuation workers attacking the beam's foundation with tools and controlled demolition techniques. The beam's integrity degraded rapidly. Daved's light pulsed brighter, as if the manifestation was trying to compensate for the physical damage.
    
    The beam failed.
    
    The northwestern corner of the Merchant Hall collapsed with a sound like the world cracking. Daved's threads snapped all at once—Tiffani saw them tear out of the stone, saw the backflash hit him like a physical blow. He flew backward, hit the ground, didn't move.
    
    Corran was there immediately, checking pulse and respiration. "He's alive. Breathing. Pulse erratic but present."
    
    Tiffani knelt beside Daved. His vein-lines covered his entire upper body now—face, neck, arms, chest, all threaded with copper-gold that glowed bright enough to illuminate the dust cloud from the collapse. His tremor was catastrophic—twenty millimeters, maybe more, his entire body shaking continuously. His eyes showed the metallic traces Brother Corran's photographs had displayed: irises with copper-gold shimmer, pupils that reflected light wrong.
    
    "Stage Three," Tiffani said. Wrote it in her journal with shaking hands. "Crossed threshold at two hundred forty heartbeats. Manifestation locked, required forced structural failure to terminate. Subject unconscious, autonomic function compromised."
    
    "Will he recover?" one of the evacuation workers asked.
    
    "I don't know," Tiffani said. "Brother Corran's data says three percent survival rate. Of those, all experienced severe permanent disability."
    
    They carried Daved to the medical station. The other operators were being assessed—Jhace with eighteen-millimeter tremor and chest-spanning vein-lines, Corven with respiratory compromise requiring breathing assistance, Senne with motor control degradation that made walking difficult.
    
    Three hundred sixty-two people evacuated from the Merchant Hall. Zero casualties.
    
    Four operators severely compromised. One at Stage Three, possibly dying.
    
    The building collapsed completely six minutes later. The central column failed exactly as Jhace had predicted, and the entire structure pancaked down into rubble.
    
    If they'd terminated at one hundred eighty heartbeats, fifty people would have died.
    
    If they'd terminated at two hundred forty heartbeats before Daved locked, maybe he wouldn't have crossed Stage Three.
    
    Tiffani sat in the documentation center that night, staring at the cost progression charts. Ran the calculations again. Same answer. Extension had saved fifty lives. Extension had potentially killed Daved. Expected value said the exchange was positive. Methodology said she'd violated protocol. Her hands said she'd made the right call and the wrong call simultaneously.
    
    A Crown courier arrived at midnight. Delivered a package from Administrator Corlexi's office. Registry paperwork—official documentation for all manifestors in Saint Merro. Tiffani's name was on the forms. Jhace's. Corven's. All eight operators who'd deployed at the Merchant Hall.
    
    At the bottom of the registry document, Corlexi's signature: neat, precise, institutionally authoritative.
    
    Tiffani filed the paperwork. Added it to the documentation archive. Wrote in her journal: *Tide 7, fourth bell. Merchant Hall crisis. Eight operators deployed, duration extended to 240 heartbeats, Stage Three threshold crossed, all civilians evacuated. Cost: one operator at catastrophic risk, three operators severely compromised, methodology integrity questioned. Outcome: 362 lives saved, network capacity reduced.*
    
    *Expected value: positive. Moral status: unknown.*
    
    She closed the journal. Went to the medical station where Daved lay unconscious, body shaking continuously, vein-lines glowing bright enough to read by.
    
    He woke three Tides later.
    
    ---
    
    *End Chapter 7*
    

---



## CHAPTER 8: RECOVERY
    
    Daved woke on the second bell of Tide 10.
    
    Tiffani was there, measuring vein-line luminescence with a calibrated light meter, when his eyes opened. Copper-gold irises. Pupils that reflected lamplight with metallic shimmer. He blinked three times, slowly, as if remembering how.
    
    "Can you hear me?" Tiffani asked.
    
    Daved's mouth moved. No sound emerged. His entire body shook with tremor so severe it made the cot vibrate. Twenty-three millimeters, Tiffani estimated. Maybe twenty-five. His hands couldn't grip, couldn't gesture, couldn't do anything except shake.
    
    She handed him a slate and chalk. "Write if you can."
    
    He tried. The tremor made it impossible. The chalk skittered across the slate in meaningless scratches. Daved stared at his hands—covered in vein-lines from fingertips to shoulders, glowing bright enough that the medical station staff had complained about the light keeping other patients awake.
    
    Tiffani pulled out a letter board. "I'll point to letters. Blink once for yes, twice for no. Understand?"
    
    One blink.
    
    She started spelling. Got through the alphabet three times before Daved had assembled his first word: BREATHING.
    
    "Your breathing," Tiffani said. "Is it voluntary? Do you have to think about it?"
    
    One blink. Yes.
    
    "Heart rhythm?"
    
    One blink. Yes.
    
    Stage Three autonomic failure. His body had forgotten how to maintain basic life functions without conscious control. Brother Corran's data had predicted this—the three percent who survived Stage Three all experienced severe autonomic compromise. Breathing required active mental maintenance. Heart rhythm could be disrupted by distraction. Sleep became life-threatening because unconsciousness meant respiratory cessation.
    
    "We have a breathing monitor," Tiffani said. "Mechanical alarm if your respiration drops below safe threshold. It will wake you if you start to stop breathing during sleep."
    
    Daved blinked once. Then spelled: WORTH IT.
    
    Not a question. A statement. Or maybe a question he was asking himself.
    
    "Fifty people would have died if you hadn't held that beam," Tiffani said. "You saved them. The cost was crossing Stage Three threshold. Statistically, you should be dead. You're the anomaly—the three percent survivor."
    
    REGRET.
    
    "Do you regret manifesting?"
    
    Two blinks. No.
    
    "Do you regret the cost?"
    
    One blink. Yes. Then more spelling: NECESSARY.
    
    Tiffani wrote in her journal: *Stage Three survivor, Tide 10, second bell. Subject conscious, cognitive function intact, communication via letter board. Autonomic compromise confirmed: breathing and heart rhythm require voluntary maintenance. Tremor amplitude: 23-25mm. Vein-line coverage: complete upper body, partial lower. Luminescence: significant. Prognosis: permanent disability, unlikely to recover autonomic function. Subject expresses no regret about manifestation despite cost.*
    
    She looked at Daved. At the man who'd held a beam until his nervous system forgot how to keep him alive. At the tremor that would never stop, the vein-lines that would never fade, the breathing he'd have to consciously maintain for the rest of his life.
    
    "Was it worth it?" she asked. Not clinical documentation. Personal question.
    
    Daved spelled slowly: ASK THE FIFTY.
    
    "I'm asking you."
    
    DONT KNOW. Then: ASK ME IN A YEAR.
    
    Tiffani closed her journal. "You won't manifest again. Your capacity is gone. But we need you for other work. Teaching new manifestors what Stage Three looks like. Helping them recognize the threshold before they cross it. You're ground-truth data now."
    
    GOOD. Daved managed something like a smile, though the tremor made it difficult to read. MAKE IT COUNT.
    
    ---
    
    Jhace visited that evening. Sat beside Daved's cot, hands shaking at sixteen millimeters—decreased from the eighteen immediately post-Merchant Hall, but still severe. His vein-lines had spread across his shoulders and upper chest. Two manifestations away from the threshold Daved had crossed.
    
    "They're saying you're a hero," Jhace said.
    
    Daved spelled: JUST DID THE WORK.
    
    "You saved fifty people and paid with your autonomic nervous system. That's more than just work." Jhace flexed his trembling hands. "I'm three manifestations in. One more after this and I'm at catastrophic risk. You're showing me what happens when I cross fourth threshold."
    
    DONT.
    
    "Don't cross it, or don't let your example stop me?"
    
    Daved pointed at the letter board. Spelled with effort: METHODOLOGY SAYS DONT. YOUR HUNGER SAYS DO. WHICH ONE WINS.
    
    "I don't know." Jhace looked at his hands. At the tremor that had become his permanent baseline. "Tiffani's building a network. More operators, better rotation protocols. In theory, I never have to manifest for the fourth time. Someone else can always deploy instead."
    
    THEORY.
    
    "Yeah. Theory." Jhace met Daved's copper-gold eyes. "How do you do it? How do you keep breathing when it's not automatic anymore?"
    
    COUNT. Daved spelled it quickly, muscle memory making it faster. HEARTBEATS. RHYTHM. MAKE IT PATTERN. PATTERN BECOMES HABIT. HABIT BECOMES SURVIVAL.
    
    "You count heartbeats to remember to breathe."
    
    YES. JUST LIKE YOU COUNT NAMES.
    
    Jhace went still. "What?"
    
    JUNCTION SEVEN. TWENTY THREE NAMES. YOU COUNT THEM. I COUNT BREATHS. SAME THING. WE CARRY WHAT WE CARRY.
    
    "The names are guilt. You're counting breaths to stay alive."
    
    GUILT KEEPS YOU ALIVE TOO. Daved's tremor intensified slightly. JUST DIFFERENT METHOD.
    
    They sat in silence. Jhace counting the names he carried. Daved counting breaths he had to consciously maintain. Two manifestors marked by costs they'd accepted to save people who would never know what it had taken.
    
    "Tiffani wants me off active rotation," Jhace finally said. "Preserve my capacity. Only deploy when crisis complexity requires experience."
    
    GOOD PLAN.
    
    "I hate it. Every time the bells ring and I'm not running toward the collapse, it feels like Junction Seven again. Like I'm filing a report that no one will act on."
    
    NOT SAME. SOMEONE ELSE ACTS. NETWORK FUNCTIONS WITHOUT YOU. THATS THE POINT.
    
    "The point is saving lives."
    
    NO. Daved pointed emphatically at the letters. POINT IS SAVING LIVES SUSTAINABLY. YOUR WAY SAVES LIVES UNTIL YOU DIE. NETWORKS WAY SAVES LIVES AFTER YOU DIE. WHICH ONE MATTERS MORE.
    
    Jhace didn't have an answer for that.
    
    Daved spelled: FIFTY PEOPLE ALIVE BECAUSE I CROSSED THRESHOLD. THOUSANDS MORE ALIVE IN FUTURE BECAUSE YOU DONT. EXPECTED VALUE SAYS YOUR SURVIVAL WORTH MORE THAN MY MANIFESTATION.
    
    "Expected value doesn't account for the fifty people who needed you in that moment."
    
    EXACTLY. THATS WHY METHODOLOGY HARD. MATH SAYS ONE THING. PEOPLE DYING SAYS ANOTHER. YOU CHOOSE WHICH ONE RULES.
    
    "What did you choose?"
    
    PEOPLE DYING. Daved's expression was unreadable through the tremor. PAID THE COST. WOULD DO AGAIN. BUT WOULDNT RECOMMEND IT.
    
    Jhace stood. His legs were more stable than they'd been three days ago. The cost was settling into his body's new configuration. "I should let you rest."
    
    CANT REST. STOP BREATHING. Daved managed the smile again. SLEEP IS COMPLICATED NOW.
    
    "The breathing monitor—"
    
    WAKES ME BEFORE I DIE. USUALLY. Daved spelled it matter-of-factly. PROBABILITIES.
    
    Jhace left the medical station. Walked back to the documentation center through streets still marked by copper-gold dust. The Seam in the sky continued pouring light across Saint Merro—a permanent wound that wouldn't close. Three Anneals post-Piercing, and the city had learned to function beneath a torn atmosphere.
    
    Tiffani was at her desk, updating cost progression charts. She'd added a new section: Stage Three Survival Data. Daved's measurements filled the page—tremor amplitude, vein-line coverage, autonomic compromise markers, communication capacity, projected lifespan.
    
    "He's asking himself if it was worth it," Jhace said.
    
    "So am I." Tiffani didn't look up from her writing. "I made the call to extend duration. I knew the Stage Three risk. I chose fifty lives over one operator's autonomic nervous system. Expected value says that was correct. Methodology says it was a protocol violation. Daved says he'd do it again but wouldn't recommend it."
    
    "What do you say?"
    
    Tiffani set down her pen. "I say I'll make the same call next time the math demands it. And I'll carry the cost of that decision the same way you carry Junction Seven and Daved carries his breaths."
    
    She pulled out a new document. Crown letterhead. Administrator Corlexi's signature at the bottom.
    
    "Crown's expanding the registry," she said. "They want manifestor networks in every major city. Corlexi's using our protocols as the standard reference. Duration limits, rotation requirements, cost documentation. What we built here becomes institutional policy across six territories."
    
    "That's good, isn't it?"
    
    "It's functional. Whether it's good depends on whether Crown enforces the methodology or just the control structure." Tiffani filed the letter. "Brother Corran says Corlexi's systematic. Sees efficient systems and implements them. But systems can be efficient at many things—saving lives, consolidating power, restricting manifestation when it threatens institutional authority."
    
    "You think he'll use our methodology to suppress manifestors?"
    
    "I think he'll use our methodology to make manifestation predictable. Whether that means saving more lives or controlling more people, we'll find out when Crown starts issuing deployment restrictions."
    
    The evacuation bells rang. Eastern terrace, residential district. Standard collapse pattern.
    
    Jhace felt the heat pulse in his chest. Felt the threads reaching toward the structural failure. His tremor spiked to eighteen millimeters.
    
    "Senne and Marict are on rotation," Tiffani said without checking her schedule. She'd memorized it. "You stay here."
    
    "I know." Jhace sat down. Flexed his hands. Watched the tremor shake his fingers. "Daved asked which one wins—methodology or hunger. I still don't know."
    
    "Good," Tiffani said. "The day you stop questioning it is the day you manifest until it kills you."
    
    She left to coordinate the deployment. Jhace sat in the documentation center, surrounded by cost progression charts and Stage Three photographs and Daved's letter-board testimony.
    
    Three manifestations completed. One more before catastrophic risk. Maybe four hundred more heartbeats of holding structures before his autonomic nervous system started forgetting how to function.
    
    The exchange rate documented in clinical notation and Daved's conscious breathing.
    
    Jhace counted heartbeats and wondered if Daved would still think it was worth it in a year, when the novelty of survival had faded and breathing remained a voluntary effort.
    
    Wondered if the fifty people Daved had saved even knew what it had cost.
    
    Wondered if it mattered whether they knew.
    
    The tremor in his hands said costs accumulated regardless of gratitude.
    
    The heat in his chest said he'd pay them anyway.
    
    ---
    
    *End Chapter 8*
    

---



# CHAPTER 9: THE SHARD-BEARER
    
    The man with copper light in his chest held a building that should have killed him.
    
    Jhace watched from fifty strides away, hands shaking—three millimeters at rest now, worse when he tried to grip anything. The tremor had been constant since the Merchant Hall. Seven Tides of recovery and it hadn't improved. Tiffani had measured it yesterday: baseline tremor, Stage One progressing toward Stage Two markers.
    
    "Expected progression," she'd written in her logbook. Clinical. Accurate.
    
    The building groaned. Five stories, residential block in the Stair District. The grid-hum had doubled two hours ago. Jhace had logged it, sent the warning. Evacuation started immediately—Tiffani's protocols working exactly as designed. Spotter teams, bell sequences, systematic clearing floor by floor.
    
    Then the western wall buckled.
    
    The man—Mobel, they called him, though no one knew where he'd come from—stepped forward. Just walked up to the failing structure like approaching a conversation. Pressed both palms against cracked stone.
    
    Light erupted from his chest.
    
    Not the copper-gold threading that Jhace knew, the careful extension of Soulpulse through material stress points. This was different. A shard of something bright embedded directly in Mobel's sternum, visible through his shirt, pulsing with each heartbeat. The light spread from it like water through cloth, coating his arms, his hands, flowing into the stone.
    
    The building stopped collapsing.
    
    Jhace counted heartbeats. Two hundred. Three hundred. Four hundred.
    
    Mobel didn't shake. Didn't gasp for breath. The copper-gold light held steady, no fluctuation, no visible strain. His face remained calm. Almost serene.
    
    Five hundred heartbeats.
    
    "How long has he been holding?" Corven appeared at Jhace's shoulder, his own tremor matching Jhace's almost exactly. Stage One twins, marked by the same progression.
    
    "Five hundred twelve heartbeats," Jhace said. His chest felt hollow. The manifestation at Merchant Hall had left that emptiness, like his capacity had burned away and left only vacuum. "No tremor. No vein-lines."
    
    "No cost," Corven said quietly.
    
    They both knew that was impossible. Tiffani had documented it across eight manifestors now, twelve events total. The cost always came. Tremor first, then vein-lines, then the progressive deterioration into Stage Two. Daved had proved Stage Three cost—severe disability, near-total motor compromise, communication reduced to chalk on slate.
    
    But Mobel...
    
    Six hundred heartbeats.
    
    The evacuation bells rang in sequence: clear, clear, clear. Every floor empty. Tiffani's voice carried across the square, that calm systematic cadence Jhace had learned to trust. "Structure empty. Zero civilians remaining. Manifestor, you have termination authorization."
    
    Mobel didn't move.
    
    Seven hundred heartbeats.
    
    "He's not listening," Corven said.
    
    Jhace's hands shook harder. He knew that feeling. The hunger to hold longer, do more, save everything. The building was empty but the stone was still failing, the grid still compromised. If Mobel let go now, the collapse would spread. Damage the structures beside it. Potentially trigger cascading failure through the whole block.
    
    One manifestor holding one building to prevent six buildings from falling.
    
    The math was clean. The cost should be catastrophic.
    
    Eight hundred heartbeats.
    
    The light from Mobel's chest brightened. The shard pulsed faster, synchronizing with something Jhace couldn't see. The building's groan changed pitch—from failing to stable, from catastrophic to merely damaged.
    
    Nine hundred heartbeats.
    
    Tiffani appeared at Jhace's other side. She had her logbook open, pen moving even as she watched. "This violates every threshold we've documented," she said. Not upset. Just observing. "Duration alone should have crossed into Stage Two. Combined with load distribution..."
    
    "Stage Three," Jhace finished. "He should be locked in by now. Autonomic failure imminent."
    
    "Yes."
    
    One thousand heartbeats.
    
    Mobel stepped back.
    
    The building held. Damaged, yes. Western wall cracked through, fifth floor compromised. But stable. Not collapsing. The copper-gold light faded from the stone, retreating back into the shard in Mobel's chest. That pulse slowing, dimming, until it was just a faint glow beneath his shirt.
    
    Mobel turned. Walked away. No tremor in his stride. No hesitation in his movement.
    
    No cost.
    
    Jhace watched him go, that hollow feeling in his chest expanding into something sharper. Hunger. Want. The methodology said fourth manifestation meant catastrophic threshold crossing. Tiffani's data proved it—Daved had barely survived his Stage Three crossing, and he'd been at six hundred heartbeats.
    
    But what if there was another way?
    
    "We need to document this," Tiffani said. She was already moving, following Mobel's path through the square. "Interview, physical assessment, cost measurement. This changes the entire taxonomy."
    
    Jhace's hands shook. He pressed them against his thighs, tried to still the tremor. Failed.
    
    Corven touched his shoulder. "You're thinking about it."
    
    "Thinking about what?"
    
    "Whatever he has. However he does it." Corven's hand was shaking too. Two manifestors marked by the same price, watching someone who seemed exempt from payment. "I can see it in your face."
    
    Jhace didn't deny it.
    
    They found Mobel three streets over, sitting on a low wall outside a fountain. The copper-gold dust that coated everything in Saint Merro since the Piercing had settled thick here, turning the water bronze. Mobel dipped one hand in it, let the metallic liquid run through his fingers.
    
    Tiffani approached directly. No preamble. "I'm documenting manifestation costs. Stage progression, threshold boundaries, recovery patterns. I need to examine you."
    
    Mobel looked up. His eyes were clear—no copper-gold traces, no metallic shimmer. Just brown eyes in an unremarkable face. He couldn't have been more than thirty Turns. Compact build, practical clothing, calloused hands. Could have been anyone.
    
    Except for the shard in his chest.
    
    "There's nothing to examine," Mobel said. His voice was gentle. Patient. Like explaining something obvious to a child. "The shard provides. I hold. No cost."
    
    "That's not possible," Tiffani said. She had her measuring tape out, that mechanical precision taking over. "Every manifestation documented shows progressive deterioration. Tremor, vein-lines, motor compromise. You held for one thousand heartbeats. You should be approaching Stage Three threshold."
    
    "Should," Mobel agreed. He stood, let Tiffani circle him. She measured tremor amplitude—zero. Checked for vein-lines—none visible. Listened to his breathing—steady, unlabored.
    
    "How?" Jhace asked. The word came out harder than intended.
    
    Mobel touched his chest, right where the shard pulsed beneath fabric. "This was in the first building that collapsed. Tide Zero, the Shaping phase, right after the Piercing. I was trying to save my daughter."
    
    He paused. The copper-gold water kept running through the fountain, endless metallic flow.
    
    "I didn't save her," Mobel continued quietly. "The building came down. I felt heat in my chest, exactly where the collapse trapped me. When they dug me out three Tides later, this was embedded in my sternum. No wound. No entry point. Just... there."
    
    Tiffani was writing, pen scratching across paper with that focused intensity. "And the cost?"
    
    "No tremor. No vein-lines. I've manifested forty-seven times since Tide Zero. Saved three thousand two hundred and sixteen people." Mobel met Jhace's eyes. "The shard doesn't extract cost. It provides capacity."
    
    "Nothing provides capacity," Jhace said. His hands shook harder. "Everything has a price."
    
    "Everything you've documented has a price," Mobel corrected. "But I'm not like you."
    
    The words shouldn't have stung. They were factual. Observable. True.
    
    They stung anyway.
    
    Tiffani closed her logbook. "The shard is an external manifestation focus. It's bearing the cost instead of your body."
    
    "Maybe," Mobel said. "Or maybe some people are meant to hold without breaking."
    
    Jhace felt Corven tense beside him. That statement carried implications—if some people could manifest without cost, what did that make the rest of them? Flawed? Insufficient? The twenty-three names at Junction Seven, the five operators who'd died crossing Stage Three, Daved's shattered communication reduced to chalk marks...
    
    All of them breaking because they weren't "meant" to hold?
    
    "I should go," Mobel said. "There's a warehouse in the Rust Quarter showing grid stress. They'll need me soon."
    
    He walked away. No tremor. No hesitation. The shard in his chest pulsed faintly with each heartbeat, steady and sure.
    
    Tiffani opened her logbook again. Started writing. "Anomaly. Shard-based manifestation. Zero observable cost across forty-seven documented events. Requires further investigation."
    
    "Further investigation," Jhace repeated. The hollow feeling in his chest ached.
    
    "Yes." Tiffani's pen kept moving. "If external manifestation focus can eliminate cost progression, that changes everything. The methodology, the rotation protocols, the threshold boundaries—all of it based on assumption that cost is inevitable."
    
    "The cost is inevitable," Corven said. He held up his shaking hands. "For us."
    
    "For operators without external focus," Tiffani corrected. "But if the shard can be replicated..."
    
    She trailed off. Started a new page. Jhace could see her mind working, that geometric precision calculating possibilities. If they could create shards. Distribute them to operators. Eliminate cost progression entirely.
    
    How many buildings could they save? How many lives?
    
    "We don't know how to make shards," Jhace said.
    
    "Not yet," Tiffani agreed. "But we can study the one that exists."
    
    Jhace watched Mobel disappear around a corner, heading toward the Rust Quarter. Toward another crisis. Another thousand heartbeats of perfect, costless holding.
    
    The hunger in his chest sharpened into something almost painful.
    
    He'd manifested twice. Stage One tremor, vein-lines developing on his forearms. Tiffani's data said third manifestation would push him toward Stage Two. Fourth would be catastrophic.
    
    But Mobel had manifested forty-seven times. No cost. No breaking.
    
    "Come on," Corven said quietly. "Bram wanted us at the workshop. Something about Crown delegation."
    
    Jhace followed. His hands shook with every step, that three-millimeter tremor marking him as clearly as any brand. He'd saved twelve people in his first manifestation, one hundred ten in his second. The math was good. The methodology worked.
    
    But forty-seven manifestations.
    
    Three thousand two hundred and sixteen lives.
    
    Zero cost.
    
    He tried not to think about what he'd be willing to pay for that capacity. Tried not to calculate how many names at Junction Seven could have been saved if he'd had a shard in his chest instead of just heat and copper-gold light that left him shaking.
    
    Tried not to want.
    
    Failed.
    
    ---
    
    That night, Jhace stood in his workshop and looked at his hands. The tremor was constant now. Stage One progressing exactly as Tiffani had documented. Predictable. Measurable. The cost he'd accepted when he chose to manifest.
    
    The forge was cold. He hadn't worked metal since the Merchant Hall—fine manipulation too difficult, grip strength too compromised. The tools on the wall mocked him. Hammer, tongs, chisel. All requiring steady hands.
    
    Twenty-three names on a plaque at Junction Seven.
    
    He'd filed the report. Master Hellik had archived it. No one had acted.
    
    If he'd had Mobel's capacity then—that shard, that endless well of power—could he have held Junction Seven himself? Manifestation before the collapse, not during? Prevention instead of rescue?
    
    The mathematics were impossible to calculate. Too many variables. But the hunger didn't care about variables.
    
    Tiffani found him there an hour later. She had her logbook, always her logbook, and that look of focused intensity that meant she'd been calculating.
    
    "I measured Mobel's shard emission," she said without preamble. "The light spectrum matches Soulpulse manifestation exactly—copper-gold range, same frequency distribution. But the amplitude is forty-seven times stronger than baseline operator capacity."
    
    "Forty-seven," Jhace repeated. The same number as Mobel's manifestation count.
    
    "Linear correlation. Each manifestation adds capacity instead of subtracting it." Tiffani sat on the workshop bench, ink-stained fingers already opening to a fresh page. "It's not just cost-neutral. It's cost-inverse. He gets stronger."
    
    Jhace's hands shook. "That's not how Soulpulse works."
    
    "That's not how it works for us," Tiffani corrected. "But the data is clear. Mobel represents a different manifestation pathway. Potentially accessible if we understand the mechanism."
    
    "You want to study him."
    
    "I want to understand him." She met his eyes. "Don't you?"
    
    Yes. God, yes. Every shaking muscle, every hollow breath wanted to understand how Mobel held without breaking. Wanted that capacity. That freedom from cost.
    
    Wanted to save everyone.
    
    "The methodology works," Jhace said instead. "Rotation protocols, duration limits, threshold recognition. We've saved two hundred forty-seven lives with systematic operation."
    
    "Mobel has saved three thousand two hundred sixteen lives with individual operation," Tiffani said quietly. "The methodology works. But heroics work faster."
    
    There it was. The thing neither of them wanted to say. Their careful system, their documented protocols, their staged progression—all of it designed to prevent catastrophic threshold crossing. All of it slower than one man with a shard in his chest.
    
    Method versus heroics.
    
    Sustainability versus immediacy.
    
    Lives saved tomorrow versus lives saved today.
    
    "If we pursue the shard pathway," Jhace said slowly, "we abandon everything we've built. The rotation protocols, the cost taxonomy, the threshold boundaries. All of it becomes irrelevant if operators can manifest without cost."
    
    "Yes," Tiffani agreed.
    
    "And if we can't replicate it? If Mobel's shard is unique?"
    
    "Then we've wasted time we could have spent refining methodology." Tiffani's pen tapped against paper. "But if we can replicate it..."
    
    She didn't finish. Didn't need to.
    
    Jhace looked at his shaking hands. Stage One tremor. Predictable cost for predictable capacity. The methodology said he had one, maybe two more manifestations before catastrophic threshold crossing. That meant twelve more lives saved at current efficiency. Maybe twenty if rotation protocols improved.
    
    Mobel could save hundreds in the same timeframe.
    
    Thousands.
    
    "I'll talk to Bram," Jhace said. "See if Crown has any data on shard formation. Post-Piercing anomalies, embedded manifestation focuses."
    
    "And I'll continue documentation," Tiffani said. "Baseline measurements, capacity tracking, cost progression monitoring."
    
    She stood to leave, logbook already closing. Paused at the doorway.
    
    "Jhace?"
    
    "Yes?"
    
    "The hunger you're feeling—wanting Mobel's capacity, wanting to save more—that's dangerous." Her voice was quiet. Clinical, but not cold. "Every operator who's died crossed threshold because they wanted to hold longer. Do more. Save everyone."
    
    "I know."
    
    "Knowing doesn't always help." She touched her own chest, right where Mobel's shard would sit. "Just... be careful what you're willing to pay for capacity you don't have."
    
    Then she was gone.
    
    Jhace stood alone in the cold forge, watching his hands shake in the dim light. Three millimeters at rest. Five under stress. Stage One progressing toward Stage Two.
    
    Measurable. Documented. Known.
    
    He thought about Mobel's steady hands. Forty-seven manifestations. Zero cost.
    
    And tried very hard not to want.
    
    Failed.
    
    Again.
    

---



# CHAPTER 10: INSTITUTIONAL PRESSURE
    
    The Crown delegation arrived on Tide Fourteen with paperwork and polished boots.
    
    Jhace counted six of them. Five administrators in formal gray, one in the dark blue of Infrastructure Compliance. They spread across the workshop like an occupation force, examining the logbooks on Tiffani's desk, the cost-tracking charts on the wall, the manifestation duration records pinned above the cold forge.
    
    The one in blue spoke first. "I'm Administrator Bram Tallow, Infrastructure Compliance Office, reporting directly to the Coordination Committee." He had a neat beard, sharp eyes, hands that had never done manual labor. "You've been operating an unlicensed manifestation network for fourteen Tides."
    
    "We've been saving lives," Tiffani said. She stood beside her desk, one hand resting on her primary logbook. Protective. "Two hundred forty-seven civilians evacuated from compromised structures. Eight operators trained in systematic manifestation protocols. Five building collapses stabilized with zero civilian casualties."
    
    "Unlicensed," Bram repeated. "Unregulated. Operating outside Crown authority during a crisis that requires coordinated institutional response."
    
    Jhace's hands shook. Three millimeters baseline now, worse when stressed. He pressed them against his thighs, felt the tremor travel up through his arms. Stage One progressing exactly as Tiffani had documented.
    
    One of the gray administrators—older woman, silver hair pulled severe—stepped forward. "The Crown acknowledges your... initiative. However, manifestation events constitute structural intervention in Crown-maintained infrastructure. Such intervention requires proper licensing, documentation, and oversight."
    
    "We have documentation," Tiffani said. She gestured to the wall where twelve logbooks sat in neat sequence, every manifestation recorded with clinical precision. "Duration, cost progression, structural assessment, evacuation efficiency. Every event logged."
    
    "Crown documentation," the woman clarified. "Official registry, approved protocols, sanctioned deployment criteria."
    
    She produced a leather folder, set it on the workbench. The Crown seal pressed into the cover—formal, institutional, absolute.
    
    "The Manifestor Registry," Bram said. "Established by order of the Infrastructure Compliance Office, reviewed and approved by the Coordination Committee. As of Tide Ten, all manifestation activity in Saint Merro must be registered, documented, and deployed according to Crown-approved criteria."
    
    Jhace felt something cold settle in his chest. "Tide Ten was four Tides ago."
    
    "Yes." Bram's expression didn't change. "Which means you've conducted approximately seventeen manifestation events in violation of Crown regulation. Each event carries potential sanction—operational suspension, license revocation, criminal prosecution for unauthorized structural intervention."
    
    The silence in the workshop felt heavy. Outside, the grid-hum maintained its doubled harmonic—structural stress unresolved, buildings still failing, crisis ongoing. Inside, six Crown administrators with leather folders and legal authority.
    
    Corven appeared in the doorway. His tremor matched Jhace's almost exactly, Stage One twins. He took in the scene, the formal gray, the leather folder on the bench. "What's happening?"
    
    "Crown regulation," Jhace said quietly.
    
    Tiffani opened the leather folder. Inside: forms. Pages of them. Registration requirements, deployment authorization procedures, manifestation approval criteria, cost documentation standards, operator licensing protocols.
    
    She read for three hundred heartbeats. Jhace counted.
    
    "This is comprehensive," she said finally. Clinical assessment, not praise. "Cost taxonomy matches our framework—Stage One through Stage Three progression. Rotation protocols align with our duration limits. Evacuation coordination procedures mirror our spotter system."
    
    "We studied your methodology," Bram said. "Administrator Corlexi has been reviewing your manifestation reports since Tide Two. Your systematic approach represents the most effective crisis response we've documented post-Piercing."
    
    "So you're adopting our protocols," Tiffani said.
    
    "We're institutionalizing them," Bram corrected. "Crown authority, official sanction, legal framework. Your methods were effective. Now they'll be authorized."
    
    Jhace watched Tiffani's face. She was calculating, that geometric precision working through implications. "And if we refuse registration?"
    
    The silver-haired administrator answered. "Then you cease operations immediately. Any future manifestation events constitute criminal structural intervention. Operators face prosecution, potential imprisonment."
    
    "People are dying," Corven said from the doorway. "Buildings collapse every three Tides. Grid stress accelerating. You're going to imprison the people trying to save lives?"
    
    "We're establishing order," Bram said. Not harsh. Matter-of-fact. "Crisis response requires coordination, not individual heroics. The Registry ensures manifestors deploy according to strategic priority rather than random occurrence."
    
    Strategic priority.
    
    Jhace felt the cold in his chest spread. "What priority?"
    
    Bram produced another document from the folder. "Crown-approved crisis events. Buildings categorized by structural significance, cultural value, institutional importance. Manifestation teams deploy according to priority sequence."
    
    Tiffani took the list. Read. Her expression didn't change but Jhace saw her fingers tighten on the paper.
    
    "The Archive is priority one," she said. "Crown administrative buildings priority two. Guild warehouses priority three." Her eyes moved down the list. "Residential districts are priority seven. After merchant holdings. After temple structures."
    
    "After everything that matters to Crown continuity," Jhace finished.
    
    "After everything that ensures institutional stability," Bram corrected. "The Archive preserves our history. Administrative buildings maintain governance. Guild warehouses provide economic foundation. These structures serve the collective."
    
    "Residential districts house people," Corven said. His voice had gone quiet. Dangerous.
    
    "People can be relocated," the silver-haired administrator said. "History cannot be recreated. Governance cannot be improvised. Economy cannot be rebuilt without foundation."
    
    Jhace's hands shook harder. He thought about the first collapse—Tallow Street, residential block, two hundred people trapped. Twelve lives saved. That building wouldn't have been priority seven. It wouldn't have had manifestor deployment at all.
    
    Those twelve people would be dead.
    
    "We've been deploying to crisis events as they occur," Tiffani said carefully. "Grid stress doesn't follow priority lists. Buildings fail according to structural compromise, not institutional value."
    
    "Which is why coordinated response is necessary," Bram said. "Strategic deployment prevents cascade failures in critical infrastructure. Protecting the Archive prevents loss of irreplaceable documentation. Protecting administrative centers maintains crisis coordination capacity."
    
    "Protecting people prevents death," Jhace said.
    
    Bram looked at him directly. "You're Jhace Torrins. Junction Seven inspector. Filed the structural assessment that Master Hellik archived without action."
    
    Twenty-three names.
    
    "Yes," Jhace said.
    
    "Your report was accurate. Thoroughly documented. Proper channels followed." Bram's voice carried something that might have been regret. "Master Hellik prioritized other projects. Archive reorganization. Historical preservation. The structural assessment waited in queue."
    
    "For three months," Jhace said. "Until the collapse."
    
    "Yes." Bram didn't look away. "Institutional failure. Resource allocation error. Tragic outcome." He paused. "The Registry prevents that. Proper prioritization, systematic deployment, coordinated response. No more reports sitting in archive queues while buildings collapse."
    
    "Just different buildings collapsing," Corven said. "The ones at priority seven."
    
    "Fewer buildings total," Bram corrected. "Strategic deployment stabilizes critical infrastructure, which prevents cascade failures, which reduces total collapse events. The mathematics support coordinated response."
    
    Tiffani was still reading the Registry documentation. Page after page of protocols that mirrored the methodology she'd developed—cost taxonomy, rotation schedules, threshold boundaries. All formalized. Institutionalized. Controlled.
    
    "Who approved this?" she asked.
    
    "Administrator Corlexi, Infrastructure Compliance Office," Bram said. "Working with the Coordination Committee. Reviewing manifestation data across all active operators in Saint Merro."
    
    Jhace had heard that name before. Tide Five, when he'd filed the first manifestation report. The clerk had mentioned Administrator Corlexi reviewing post-Piercing structural documentation. Background presence. Bureaucratic machinery.
    
    Now that machinery had produced a Registry that would control every manifestation in the city.
    
    "I want to see the cost-benefit analysis," Tiffani said. "Strategic priority versus crisis response. Lives saved under Registry deployment compared to systematic response to structural failures."
    
    "That analysis is restricted," the silver-haired administrator said. "Crown internal documentation."
    
    "Then you're asking us to accept regulation without evidence it improves outcomes," Tiffani said. Not arguing. Observing.
    
    "We're requiring you to accept regulation because it's law," Bram corrected. "Crown authority during crisis conditions. The analysis supports the policy. That's sufficient."
    
    Jhace watched Tiffani's face. She was thinking about Daved—Stage Three crossing to save fifty people on Merchant Hall's upper floors. Thinking about every manifestation logged in those twelve logbooks. Every heartbeat counted, every tremor measured, every life weighed against cost.
    
    All of it effective precisely because they'd responded to immediate crisis, not institutional priority.
    
    "What happens if we register?" Tiffani asked.
    
    Bram opened the folder wider. "You receive Crown license to operate. Official manifestor designation. Access to coordinated deployment network. Legal protection from prosecution."
    
    "And we deploy according to your priority list."
    
    "You deploy according to strategic crisis response protocol," Bram said. "Developed by Administrator Corlexi based on your methodology. Tested against structural analysis models. Approved by the Coordination Committee."
    
    "We deploy to Archives instead of residential blocks," Jhace said flatly.
    
    "You deploy to prevent cascade failures that would collapse both," Bram said. "Strategic stabilization versus reactive rescue. Systematic prevention versus individual response."
    
    The logic was clean. Jhace could see it. Protect critical infrastructure first, prevent cascade failures, reduce total collapse events. The mathematics probably did support it.
    
    But mathematics didn't account for the twelve people on Tallow Street. The fifty on Merchant Hall's upper floors. The single child in a debris field that Jhace would someday—according to Tiffani's projections—manifest a fourth time to save.
    
    Priority seven. All of them.
    
    Expendable in service of Archives and administrative centers.
    
    Tiffani closed the folder. "I need time to review the documentation. Analyze deployment criteria. Calculate outcome projections."
    
    "You have until tomorrow at Shaping phase," Bram said. "After that, unregistered manifestation becomes criminal activity."
    
    "One Tide," Tiffani said.
    
    "Sufficient for review," Bram agreed. He gestured to the other administrators. They began filing out, polished boots precise on the workshop floor.
    
    Bram paused at the door. "I understand your resistance. Individual autonomy, direct crisis response, immediate results. But institutions exist for reason. Coordination prevents chaos. Order saves more lives than heroics."
    
    "Tell that to the people at priority seven," Corven said.
    
    Bram looked at him. "I will. When they're alive because cascade failure didn't destroy their entire district." Then he was gone, the delegation disappearing into the copper-gold dusk.
    
    Jhace, Tiffani, and Corven stood in the workshop surrounded by logbooks and cost-tracking charts and manifestation records that Crown had studied and institutionalized and regulated.
    
    "We register," Tiffani said after two hundred heartbeats. Not a question. Statement of fact.
    
    "We accept their priority list," Jhace said. "Let residential districts collapse while we protect Archives."
    
    "We accept legal authority to continue operations," Tiffani corrected. "Under Crown regulation, yes. Following deployment protocols, yes. But operational, documented, effective."
    
    "Effective at what?" Corven's tremor was visible, hands shaking with the same three-millimeter baseline as Jhace. "Saving buildings instead of people?"
    
    "Effective at not being imprisoned for unauthorized structural intervention," Tiffani said. Clinical. Brutal. True. "The Crown has authority. We have methodology. Registration lets us continue using methodology under their authority."
    
    "While they control where we deploy," Jhace said.
    
    "Yes."
    
    The workshop felt smaller. The twelve logbooks on the wall—documentation of autonomous response, systematic intervention, lives saved through immediate crisis assessment—all of it about to become Crown-approved, institutionally sanctioned, strategically regulated activity.
    
    Jhace thought about Mobel. Forty-seven manifestations. Three thousand two hundred sixteen lives saved. No Crown authority, no registration, no strategic priority lists.
    
    Just individual heroics.
    
    Unsustainable but immediate.
    
    "If we don't register," he said slowly, "Crown prosecutes us. We stop operating entirely. No more manifestations, no more crisis response, no more lives saved at any priority level."
    
    "Correct," Tiffani said.
    
    "But if we do register, we deploy according to their criteria. Which means some buildings fail without intervention. Some people die without rescue."
    
    "Also correct."
    
    "So we choose between saving no one and saving whoever Crown decides is strategically valuable."
    
    Tiffani met his eyes. "Yes."
    
    The mathematics were brutal. Zero lives saved versus some lives saved. No operations versus regulated operations. Complete shutdown versus institutional compromise.
    
    The answer was obvious.
    
    The answer felt like surrender.
    
    "Tomorrow at Shaping phase," Tiffani said. "I'll complete the registration documentation. We'll receive Crown license. And we'll continue operating under their authority."
    
    Corven turned away. Stared at the cold forge, the metalworking tools Jhace could no longer use with steady hands. "This is wrong."
    
    "This is survival," Tiffani said. "Institutional authority during crisis. We adapt or we disappear."
    
    "We compromise," Jhace said quietly.
    
    "Yes." No hesitation. No doubt. Just clinical assessment of available options. "We compromise. And we document everything."
    
    ---
    
    That night, Jhace found the announcement posted in Crown administrative hall. Official notice, formal language, Crown seal pressed into expensive paper:
    
    **MANIFESTOR REGISTRY ESTABLISHED**
    
    *By order of the Infrastructure Compliance Office*  
    *Administrator Corlexi presiding*
    
    *All manifestation activity within Saint Merro must be registered with Crown authority. Operators will receive official license upon completion of documentation requirements and demonstration of competency in approved protocols.*
    
    *Deployment will occur according to strategic crisis response criteria developed in coordination with the Coordination Committee. Unauthorized manifestation constitutes criminal structural intervention and carries penalty of operational suspension and potential imprisonment.*
    
    *Registration office opens Tide Fifteen, Shaping phase. Documentation requirements available at Infrastructure Compliance Office.*
    
    The signature at the bottom was neat. Precise. Administrator Corlexi's handwriting showed the same systematic clarity as Tiffani's documentation—measured letterforms, consistent spacing, absolute control.
    
    Jhace stood in the empty hall and read the notice three times.
    
    Administrator Corlexi. Background presence since Tide Two. Reviewing manifestation reports. Studying methodology. Building institutional framework that would regulate everything they'd created.
    
    Order from chaos.
    
    System from crisis.
    
    Control.
    
    Jhace's hands shook. Three millimeters at rest. Five under stress. Stage One progressing toward Stage Two with mathematical precision.
    
    He thought about Mobel—unregistered, unauthorized, saving thousands without Crown approval. Would the Registry prosecute him? Force compliance? Or would that shard in his chest exempt him from institutional authority the same way it exempted him from cost?
    
    Heroics versus method.
    
    Individual versus institution.
    
    Immediate versus sustainable.
    
    Jhace didn't know which was right anymore.
    
    He only knew that tomorrow at Shaping phase, Tiffani would complete registration documentation. They would receive Crown license. And every manifestation after would deploy according to strategic priority determined by an administrator Jhace had never met.
    
    Twenty-three names at Junction Seven.
    
    Priority seven on Crown's list.
    
    Would never have received manifestor deployment under the Registry.
    
    Jhace walked home through copper-gold streets, counting his shaking heartbeats, carrying the weight of institutional compromise like tremor in his bones.
    
    Tomorrow they would register.
    
    Tomorrow they would survive.
    
    Tomorrow some people at priority seven would die without rescue, and the Crown would call it strategic crisis response.
    
    And the mathematics would probably support it.
    
    Probably.
    

---



# CHAPTER 11: THE HUNGER
    
    Daved's rehabilitation room smelled like copper-gold dust and chalk.
    
    Jhace sat beside the bed where Daved lay propped against pillows, body motionless except for the tremor. Severe tremor—Stage Three markers, constant shaking that traveled from fingertips to shoulders in visible waves. The vein-lines covered both arms, bright copper-gold traces that glowed faintly even in daylight.
    
    "Can you feel your legs?" Jhace asked.
    
    Daved's eyes moved. That was all. Eye movement and breathing—the only voluntary control remaining after seven Tides of recovery. His slate sat propped on a wooden frame above his chest, chalk tied to a mouthpiece so he could write by moving his head.
    
    The slate read: *NO. AUTONOMIC ONLY. HEART LUNGS EYES MOUTH.*
    
    Jhace's own tremor felt minor by comparison. Three millimeters baseline, hands that shook but still functioned. Stage One progressing toward Stage Two. Predictable deterioration.
    
    Not catastrophic threshold crossing.
    
    Not this.
    
    "Tiffani says you're statistically anomalous," Jhace said. "Three percent survival rate for Stage Three crossing. Most operators die. The ones who survive usually have partial recovery—some motor function, limited mobility."
    
    *I KNOW THE STATISTICS* Daved wrote. His head moved with practiced precision, years of metalwork training translated into chalk-and-slate communication. *LIVED THEM.*
    
    "Fifty people on Merchant Hall's upper floors," Jhace said. "Would have died without your extended hold."
    
    *YES.*
    
    "Was it worth it?"
    
    Daved's eyes held Jhace's for a long moment. The tremor in his body continued its constant rhythm, involuntary shaking that would never stop. Finally his head moved, chalk scratching across slate:
    
    *ASK ME IN TEN YEARS.*
    
    Jhace felt something twist in his chest. That hollow feeling from his second manifestation, capacity burned away and replaced with vacuum. "You don't know?"
    
    *I KNOW I SAVED FIFTY PEOPLE* Daved wrote. *I KNOW I WILL NEVER WALK AGAIN. NEVER HOLD TOOLS. NEVER WORK METAL.* The chalk paused. *BOTH THINGS ARE TRUE. DONT KNOW WHICH WEIGHS MORE.*
    
    "Tiffani's methodology says you shouldn't have extended the hold," Jhace said. "Duration limits exist to prevent threshold crossing. You were at Stage Two markers. Should have terminated."
    
    *TIFFANI MADE THE CALL* Daved wrote. *CALCULATED RISK. FIFTY LIVES VERSUS ONE OPERATOR. MATHEMATICS SUPPORTED EXTENSION.*
    
    "But you paid the cost."
    
    *YES.*
    
    Jhace looked at his own hands. The tremor had worsened since yesterday—stress from Crown registration, institutional pressure, the knowledge that strategic priority lists would control every future deployment. Three millimeters becoming four. Stage One accelerating.
    
    "I think about Mobel," Jhace said quietly. "Forty-seven manifestations. Zero cost. That shard in his chest providing capacity without price."
    
    Daved's eyes sharpened.
    
    "I want that," Jhace continued. "Want to hold longer, do more, save everyone. The hunger is... constant. Knowing that somewhere in this city there's a man who can manifest without breaking, and I'm here shaking apart one heartbeat at a time."
    
    *DANGEROUS HUNGER* Daved wrote.
    
    "I know."
    
    *EVERY OPERATOR WHO DIED CROSSED THRESHOLD BECAUSE THEY WANTED MORE.* The chalk moved faster, Daved's head precise despite the tremor wracking his body. *WANTED TO SAVE ONE MORE PERSON. HOLD ONE MORE MINUTE. DO ONE MORE IMPOSSIBLE THING.*
    
    "Like you did," Jhace said.
    
    *LIKE I DID* Daved agreed. *AND NOW I LIE HERE. FUNCTIONAL MIND. BROKEN BODY. TEACHING NEW MANIFESTORS ABOUT THRESHOLD RECOGNITION BY BEING CAUTIONARY EXAMPLE.*
    
    Jhace watched the copper-gold traces in Daved's skin pulse with his heartbeat. Stage Three cost, permanent disability, life reduced to eye movement and chalk marks.
    
    Fifty people alive who would otherwise be dead.
    
    Both things true.
    
    "The methodology works," Jhace said. "Rotation protocols, duration limits, threshold boundaries. We've saved two hundred forty-seven lives using systematic operation."
    
    *MOBEL SAVED THREE THOUSAND* Daved wrote.
    
    "With heroics. Individual operation. Unsustainable."
    
    *BUT IMMEDIATE.* Daved's eyes held something sharp. *METHODOLOGY SAVES LIVES TOMORROW. HEROICS SAVES LIVES TODAY. WHICH MATTERS MORE?*
    
    Jhace didn't have an answer.
    
    The rehabilitation room's window looked out over the Rust Quarter. From here he could see three buildings showing grid stress—doubled harmonic audible even through glass, structural displacement visible in cracked facades. All three were residential. Priority seven on Crown's strategic list.
    
    Under the new Registry, manifestors would deploy to Archives first. Administrative buildings second. Guild warehouses third.
    
    Those three residential blocks would wait until strategic priority allowed intervention.
    
    How many people would die in that waiting?
    
    "Tiffani completed registration this morning," Jhace said. "We're Crown-licensed now. Official manifestor designation. Legal protection from prosecution."
    
    *AND CROWN CONTROL* Daved wrote.
    
    "Yes."
    
    *STRATEGIC DEPLOYMENT. PRIORITY LISTS. INSTITUTIONAL AUTHORITY OVER CRISIS RESPONSE.*
    
    "It was register or stop operations entirely," Jhace said. "The mathematics supported compliance."
    
    *MATHEMATICS SUPPORT MANY THINGS* Daved wrote. *DOESN'T MAKE THEM RIGHT.*
    
    Jhace felt his hands shake harder. Four millimeters now. Stress acceleration. "What would you have done?"
    
    Daved's chalk moved slowly, deliberate letterforms:
    
    *I WOULD HAVE WANTED TO FIGHT. REFUSE REGISTRATION. CONTINUE AUTONOMOUS OPERATIONS.* Pause. *BUT I WOULD HAVE REGISTERED. BECAUSE INSTITUTIONAL SURVIVAL BEATS INDIVIDUAL HEROICS.*
    
    "Even when heroics save more lives?"
    
    *ESPECIALLY THEN.* Daved's eyes were steady despite the tremor. *HEROICS BURN OUT. METHOD ENDURES. FIFTY YEARS FROM NOW NOBODY REMEMBERS MY NAME. BUT ROTATION PROTOCOLS STILL SAVE LIVES.*
    
    Jhace thought about Junction Seven. Twenty-three names on a plaque. He remembered every one. But how many people in Saint Merro walked past that memorial without reading? Without knowing?
    
    How many years until those names meant nothing except statistical data in Crown archives?
    
    "I'm afraid of wanting Mobel's capacity," Jhace said quietly. "Afraid of that hunger. Because every time I see him hold without cost, part of me thinks—if I just pushed harder, held longer, crossed one more threshold..."
    
    *YOU'D END UP LIKE ME* Daved wrote. *OR DEAD.*
    
    "Or I'd save three thousand people."
    
    *NO.* Daved's chalk moved with absolute certainty. *MOBEL SAVES THREE THOUSAND PEOPLE. YOU WOULD CROSS THRESHOLD AND DIE. BECAUSE YOU DONT HAVE A SHARD. YOU HAVE A HUMAN BODY WITH HUMAN LIMITS.*
    
    "Limits I chose to accept when I started manifesting," Jhace said.
    
    *LIMITS THAT KEEP YOU ALIVE.*
    
    The tremor in Jhace's hands felt heavier. He pressed them against his thighs, tried to still the shaking. Failed. Stage One progressing toward Stage Two. Methodology predicted he had one, maybe two more manifestations before catastrophic threshold crossing.
    
    Unless he pursued Mobel's path. Sought out whatever mechanism produced that shard. Found a way to manifest without cost.
    
    The hunger whispered that possibility with every tremor-marked heartbeat.
    
    "How do you live with it?" Jhace asked. "The wanting. Knowing you could have done more if you'd just pushed further."
    
    Daved's eyes held something that might have been pain or might have been acceptance. The chalk moved:
    
    *I DONT LIVE WITH IT. IT LIVES WITH ME. EVERY DAY I LIE HERE WANTING TO STAND. WANTING TO WORK. WANTING MY BODY BACK.* Pause. *AND EVERY DAY I CHOOSE TO ACCEPT WHAT IS INSTEAD OF WANTING WHAT WAS.*
    
    "That's not the same thing."
    
    *ITS EXACTLY THE SAME THING* Daved wrote. *YOU WANT MOBELS CAPACITY. I WANT MY MOBILITY. BOTH IMPOSSIBLE. BOTH CONSUMING. BOTH DANGEROUS IF WE LET WANTING OVERRIDE ACCEPTING.*
    
    Jhace looked at the three residential buildings through the window. Grid stress accelerating. Collapse probable within two Tides. Priority seven on Crown's list.
    
    Accept what is instead of wanting what could be.
    
    Let those buildings fail because strategic priority demanded Archives receive protection first.
    
    Let people die because institutional authority overrode immediate crisis response.
    
    Accept systematic methodology even when heroics would save more.
    
    "I don't know if I can do that," Jhace said.
    
    *THEN YOU'LL CROSS THRESHOLD* Daved wrote simply. *PURSUE MOBELS PATH. SEEK CAPACITY WITHOUT COST. MANIFEST UNTIL YOUR BODY BREAKS TRYING TO MATCH WHAT HE HAS.*
    
    "Or I accept limits. Work within methodology. Save fewer lives but survive longer."
    
    *YES.*
    
    "And live with the hunger."
    
    *YES.* Daved's eyes were steady. *WE ALL LIVE WITH IT. EVERY OPERATOR WHO MANIFESTS ONCE WANTS TO MANIFEST AGAIN. WANTS TO SAVE MORE. DO MORE. BE MORE.* The chalk moved with finality: *METHODOLOGY TEACHES US TO WANT LESS THAN WE COULD DO. THATS WHAT MAKES IT SUSTAINABLE.*
    
    Jhace sat with that for three hundred heartbeats. Outside, the grid-hum maintained its doubled harmonic. Inside, Daved's tremor continued its constant rhythm.
    
    Want less than we could do.
    
    Accept limits that keep us alive.
    
    Choose method over heroics even when heroics save more.
    
    The mathematics were brutal. But Daved was right—heroics burned out. Method endured.
    
    Individual capacity versus institutional survival.
    
    Immediate rescue versus systematic prevention.
    
    Lives saved today versus protocols that would save lives for decades.
    
    "I should go," Jhace said. "Tiffani wants to review Crown deployment criteria. Calculate outcome projections under strategic priority protocol."
    
    *TELL HER IM AVAILABLE FOR CONSULTATION* Daved wrote. His chalk added with deliberate precision: *CAUTIONARY EXAMPLE. THRESHOLD CROSSING SURVIVOR. EVIDENCE THAT WANTING MORE COSTS EVERYTHING.*
    
    Jhace stood. His tremor had settled back to baseline—three millimeters, manageable, predictable. "Thank you."
    
    *FOR WHAT?*
    
    "For showing me what threshold crossing actually costs."
    
    Daved's eyes held his. The chalk moved one final time:
    
    *SHOW OTHERS. METHODOLOGY WORKS BECAUSE PEOPLE SEE CONSEQUENCES. I AM CONSEQUENCE. USE ME.*
    
    Jhace left the rehabilitation room carrying that weight like tremor in his bones.
    
    ---
    
    He found Mobel that evening in the Stair District, holding a warehouse that was already three-quarters collapsed. The shard in his chest pulsed with steady light, copper-gold brilliance that made the dust-filled air glow. 
    
    Seven hundred heartbeats. Eight hundred. Nine hundred.
    
    No tremor. No strain. Perfect, costless power.
    
    Jhace watched from across the square. His hands shook with that baseline three-millimeter tremor, Stage One marking him as clearly as any brand. He felt the hunger rise—sharp, consuming, desperate.
    
    Want what Mobel has.
    
    Manifest without cost.
    
    Save thousands instead of hundreds.
    
    Be more than methodology allows.
    
    The warehouse stabilized. Mobel stepped back. The light faded. He walked away without tremor, without hesitation, without cost.
    
    Jhace stood alone in the copper-gold dusk and chose.
    
    Chose to turn away from Mobel's path.
    
    Chose to accept human limits instead of pursuing impossible capacity.
    
    Chose methodology over heroics even though heroics saved more lives.
    
    The hunger didn't disappear. It lived with him, exactly as Daved had said. Constant wanting, perpetual temptation, the knowledge that somewhere in this city a man manifested without breaking.
    
    But Jhace walked back to the workshop where Tiffani waited with Crown deployment criteria and systematic protocols and rotation schedules that would keep operators alive.
    
    Walked back to method instead of heroics.
    
    Survival instead of transcendence.
    
    Tomorrow they would deploy according to strategic priority. Save Archives before residential districts. Accept institutional control over autonomous crisis response.
    
    Live with the hunger for what they couldn't do.
    
    Want less than they were capable of.
    
    And survive.
    
    The tremor in his hands marked every step. Three millimeters baseline. Four under stress. Stage One progressing toward Stage Two with mathematical precision.
    
    Known cost. Documented progression. Accepted limit.
    
    Human.
    
    Not heroic.
    
    Not transcendent.
    
    Just human.
    
    And that, Jhace decided in the copper-gold twilight, would have to be enough.
    
    Even when it wasn't.
    
    Especially then.
    

---



# CHAPTER 12: METHOD
    
    The observation platform stood three hundred strides above the Rust Quarter, glass and metal framework cantilevered over empty air. Tiffani's instruments covered every surface—measuring devices, calculation sheets, lens plates positioned to track the Seam's expansion.
    
    That tear in the sky where the Piercing had punctured reality.
    
    Jhace climbed the final ladder with tremor-marked hands. Four millimeters baseline now. Worse at night. Worse under stress. Stage One accelerating toward Stage Two exactly as Tiffani's cost taxonomy predicted.
    
    She didn't look up when he reached the platform. Her focus stayed fixed on the primary lens, one hand adjusting calibration while the other wrote measurements in her logbook. Ink-stained fingers moving with geometric precision despite the exhaustion visible in every line of her body.
    
    "Seam expansion is accelerating," she said. "Point-zero-three degrees per Tide. Projection shows atmospheric tear reaching critical threshold in approximately four hundred Tides."
    
    "Critical threshold meaning what?" Jhace moved to stand beside her. The platform swayed slightly—grid stress evident even here, infrastructure compromised throughout Saint Merro.
    
    "Unknown." Tiffani's pen kept moving. "No baseline data for reality puncture. Could mean stabilization. Could mean catastrophic expansion. Could mean nothing."
    
    "So we measure and don't know why."
    
    "We measure and accumulate data. Understanding follows documentation, not the reverse." She finally looked up. Her eyes showed the same exhaustion as her posture—chronic sleep deprivation, weeks of constant observation, the toll of witnessing cost progression across a dozen manifestors.
    
    No Soulpulse cost. Just human exhaustion.
    
    Jhace noticed she was favoring her left leg. The limp that had developed over the last three Anneals, non-Soulpulse physical strain from standing at observation posts for hours. "You should rest."
    
    "I should measure Seam expansion while atmospheric conditions allow clear observation." She turned back to the lens. "Rest occurs when data collection permits."
    
    Classic Tiffani. Systematic priority, clinical assessment, work before comfort.
    
    Jhace looked out over Saint Merro. From this height he could see the grid pattern that gave the city its structure—terraced districts descending into bedrock, tunnels connecting levels, the whole thing built like one massive architectural equation. Beautiful in its precision.
    
    Broken in its function.
    
    Eight buildings had collapsed completely since the Piercing. Seventeen more showed severe structural compromise. The doubled harmonic in the grid-hum persisted—that diagnostic tone indicating stress throughout the system.
    
    And above it all, the Seam. That copper-gold tear splitting the sky like a wound that wouldn't heal.
    
    "How many manifestation events today?" Jhace asked.
    
    "Three. All Crown-approved according to strategic priority criteria." Tiffani consulted a secondary logbook without breaking her lens observation. "Archive stabilization at Kindling phase. Administrative building reinforcement at Pouring. Guild warehouse intervention at Shaping."
    
    "Residential?"
    
    "Zero deployment. No priority-seven structures on today's crisis list."
