Brother Corran didn’t wear Temple colors.

That was the first thing Jhace noticed when the man stepped into the workshop two Tides after the warehouse. No robes. No beads. Just a plain coat and a Crown badge pinned where it couldn’t be missed.

Corran’s eyes went straight to Jhace’s hands.

“For the record, I’m not here to stop you,” Corran said, before anyone asked.

Bram hovered by the door like a lock that wanted to be useful.

“Enough.” Corran’s voice was flat, and that flatness carried exhaustion. “Most die. A few don’t. The ones who don’t… wish they had.”

Jhace’s tremor ticked up at the word *die*. He forced his hands flat on the bench. “Why give this to us?”

[L1] # N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS
[L2] *Turning: GLASS (G) — Tide 0 through Tide 21 (Three Anneals post-Piercing)*
[L3] 
[L4] ---
[L5] 
[L6] ## CHAPTER 1: SOMETHING WRONG
[L7] Junction Seven wore its dead like a badge you couldn’t scrub off.
[L8] 
[L9] Twenty‑three names, cut into iron plate and bolted to stone. Jhace read them anyway. Not for ceremony. For calibration. If he let the names blur, he would let the work blur. That was the rule now.
[L10] 
[L11] Harren Corl. Bessa Tyne. Mikael Stradd.
[L12] 
[L13] His thumb traced the last groove. The plate was cold enough to bite. The Underface never fully warmed—heat rose, but the terraces drank it before it reached this depth.
[L14] 
[L15] He set his kit on the pipe shelf and unpacked by touch. Chalk. Slate. Measuring strap. Collar keys. Wedge slips. A flask of lamp oil he never used but always carried because rules were rules.
[L16] 
[L17] He pressed his palm to the main supply line.
[L18] 
[L19] The grid‑hum was there—constant low vibration carried through iron and stone, like the city breathing through a cracked lung. Baseline. Familiar.
[L20] 
[L21] Then he felt the second layer.
[L22] 
[L23] The second layer wasn’t a rattle or loose bolts; it was a doubled harmonic—the hum carrying a deepened echo underneath it, out of phase by a fraction that made his teeth itch.
[L24] 
[L25] Jhace pulled his hand away like the pipe had burned him.
[L26] 
[L27] He put it back. Same.
[L28] 
[L29] He moved two spans downslope. Same doubled echo, slightly stronger. He moved upslope. Weaker. The source was below, not above. Underface load, not terrace load.
[L30] 
[L31] He marked the pipe with chalk and wrote on his slate:
[L32] 
[L33] **Tide 0 — Kindling**  
[L34] **J7 main line: doubled harmonic present.**  
[L35] **Not local. Carries through junction.**
[L36] 
[L37] He hated writing *not local*. It meant the problem wasn’t one joint he could reseat. It meant a system shift.
[L38] 
[L39] A tunnel worker came through the junction, carrying a spool of cable on one shoulder. Chloen. Jhace knew her by the way she moved: efficient, eyes down, never wasting stride.
[L40] 
[L41] “Inspector,” she said without slowing.
[L42] 
[L43] “Chloen.” He watched the cable roll bounce against her back. “You feel it today?”
[L44] 
[L45] She paused like she hadn’t meant to. “Feel what?”
[L46] 
[L47] “The hum.”
[L48] 
[L49] Her mouth tightened. “It’s… wrong.”
[L50] 
[L51] Jhace nodded once. “Where?”
[L52] 
[L53] Chloen shifted her grip, and for a second her fingers trembled against the spool’s edge. Not exhaustion. Not cold. Contact‑dependent. The kind of tremor you only saw when skin met metal.
[L54] 
[L55] “It gets worse near Twelve,” she said. “Third branch. Sector Fourteen feed.”
[L56] 
[L57] Jhace’s gut sank. Junction Twelve fed the kiln quarter. If that line failed, half the lower terraces would riot before the stone cooled.
[L58] 
[L59] “You report it?”
[L60] 
[L61] She gave him a look that was half amusement, half bitterness. “To who? Foreman says keep flow. Crown says file forms.”
[L62] 
[L63] “File anyway,” Jhace said. “To me.”
[L64] 
[L65] He started walking.
[L66] 
[L67] Junction Twelve was eight turns of the wrist by map, but maps lied down here. Underface shifted. Stone settled. Pipes bowed a fraction every Anneal, and if you didn’t walk the route you didn’t know the city.
[L68] 
[L69] As he moved, the hum thickened. The doubled harmonic sharpened into something with edges. His mouth tasted copper, faint and metallic, like he’d bitten his own tongue.
[L70] 
[L71] He stopped at the collar that fed Sector Fourteen’s third branch.
[L72] 
[L73] The collar had been reseated recently. Fresh tool marks. New wedge slips. Someone had tightened it fast and called it good. The seam line wasn’t flush.
[L74] 
[L75] Jhace set his measuring strap, checked the angle, and felt the pressure with his fingertips. The collar was carrying load wrong—micro‑shear on the downslope lip.
[L76] 
[L77] He wrote:
[L78] 
[L79] **J12 S14‑3 collar: seam not flush. Micro‑shear present. Risk point.**  
[L80] **Recommend shutdown + reseat.**
[L81] 
[L82] Boot steps behind him. Heavy. Foreman boots.
[L83] 
[L84] Tallec—Sector Fourteen’s floor lead—appeared with two crew. Face dirty, eyes sharp, hands clean enough to prove he hadn’t been the one down here doing the work.
[L85] 
[L86] “Inspector,” Tallec said. “That line stays open. We’re already down two pours.”
[L87] 
[L88] Jhace didn’t look up from the collar. “It shouldn’t be open.”
[L89] 
[L90] “It feeds the kiln quarter. We’re in Kindling. You shut it, we lose pours.”
[L91] 
[L92] “You keep it open, you lose people.”
[L93] 
[L94] Tallec crouched beside him, close enough Jhace could smell the steam on his breath. “You always say that. Junction Seven. You want to make it your sermon.”
[L95] 
[L96] Jhace’s fingers went tight around the collar key. His hands didn’t shake yet. They would, someday. He didn’t know it. But he felt the pressure in the metal like it was already there.
[L97] 
[L98] “This seam is mis‑seated,” he said. “Not opinion. Physics.”
[L99] 
[L100] Tallec’s jaw worked. “Reseat it after the next pour.”
[L101] 
[L102] “No.” Jhace stood. “Now.”
[L103] 
[L104] Tallec rose too. “You shut us down, I’ll file you for obstruction. Crown listens to my numbers. Not your fear.”
[L105] 
[L106] Jhace held his slate between them like a shield. “Then put your name under mine when the collar fails.”
[L107] 
[L108] For a breath, the tunnel went quiet except for the hum.
[L109] 
[L110] Then the hum dipped.
[L111] 
[L112] Not in volume. In pitch. A low sag that rolled through the pipes like a living thing shifting its weight.
[L113] 
[L114] Every hair on Jhace’s arms lifted.
[L115] 
[L116] Chloen’s tremor returned hard enough to make the spool bite her shoulder. “That wasn’t local,” she whispered.
[L117] 
[L118] Jhace looked at the ceiling stone. Dust shivered loose, not from a nearby impact, but as if the whole terrace had been told to remember gravity.
[L119] 
[L120] He made his choice the way he always did. Not fast. Not heroic. Just correct.
[L121] 
[L122] “Deeper first,” he said to himself. “Then up.”
[L123] 
[L124] He grabbed his kit, shoved his slate into his coat, and moved past Tallec toward the downslope access shaft.
[L125] 
[L126] Behind him, Tallec cursed and yelled for his crew to keep flow.
[L127] 
[L128] Jhace didn’t answer.
[L129] 
[L130] The doubled harmonic followed him like a warning that had learned to walk.
[L131] 
[L132] ---
[L133] 
[L134] ## CHAPTER 2: GLASS THAT LISTENS
[L135] The observatory wasn’t a tower.
[L136] 
[L137] Saint Merro didn’t waste elevation on beauty. The Glass‑House sat half‑buried in the upper terrace wall, its lens ports bored through stone like gun barrels aimed at the sky.
[L138] 
[L139] Tiffani Merrow had been awake since the first tremor. Not the kind you felt through your feet—those came and went with machinery load. This one lived in the instruments.
[L140] 
[L141] She set her palm on the primary lens frame and felt vibration where there should have been nothing. The glass was listening.
[L142] 
[L143] Her slate already held numbers. Projection curves. Angle corrections. Reference points she’d used for years.
[L144] 
[L145] Seven‑Three was wrong.
[L146] 
[L147] Not by a fraction. By a displacement that made her stomach tighten.
[L148] 
[L149] She leaned into the eyepiece again, adjusted, checked the crosshair, and watched the star sit where it didn’t belong.
[L150] 
[L151] “Shift confirmed,” she murmured, and her voice sounded like someone else’s.
[L152] 
[L153] She wrote:
[L154] 
[L155] **Tide 0 — Kindling**  
[L156] **Seven‑Three displacement: 0.34 degrees west.**  
[L157] **Not atmospheric. Not lens warp.**
[L158] 
[L159] Copper hit her tongue.
[L160] 
[L161] It wasn’t taste from the air vents. It was sudden, metallic, and clean—like a coin held under the tongue too long.
[L162] 
[L163] She lifted her head, and the light outside the lens port changed.
[L164] 
[L165] The light wasn’t dim or bright; it was bent.
[L166] 
[L167] The sky over Saint Merro wasn’t supposed to do anything except hold weather. Today it held something else.
[L168] 
[L169] A line appeared. Not a cloud seam. Not smoke. A straight, dark cut across the upper sky that moved too fast for her eyes to track at first. Then it slowed enough for her to understand what she was seeing.
[L170] 
[L171] A spindle.
[L172] 
[L173] Long and narrow and wrong, like someone had driven a forged bar through the world.
[L174] 
[L175] Her breath stalled.
[L176] 
[L177] The spindle crossed the lens view in a single heartbeat. No friction fire. No sound. Just a displacement of light that made the air look thin.
[L178] 
[L179] Then the world punched.
[L180] 
[L181] Gravity staggered. Not a shift to one side—an up‑and‑down lurch like the planet had been grabbed and shaken once.
[L182] 
[L183] Tiffani’s slate slid across the stone desk. A lens clamp snapped. Glass sang a sharp, stressed note and then went silent.
[L184] 
[L185] She steadied herself with both hands and forced her eyes back to the port.
[L186] 
[L187] The spindle wasn’t above them anymore.
[L188] 
[L189] It was gone through.
[L190] 
[L191] A pressure wave arrived a breath later. Not a blast—more like the city inhaling and not knowing what to do with the air. Dust fell from the ceiling mortar in a slow, dry drift.
[L192] 
[L193] And then the dust outside fell too.
[L194] 
[L195] Copper‑gold particulate, glittering in the lens light like ground glass in a furnace beam. It coated the stone lip of the port and clung to the iron frame in flecks that shimmered when she moved.
[L196] 
[L197] Tiffani touched it with her fingertip.
[L198] 
[L199] It didn’t smear like ash. It gritted. Sharp, fine, abrasive.
[L200] 
[L201] Her finger tingled.
[L202] 
[L203] She pulled back, stared at the dust on her skin, and watched it catch the light as if it wanted to be noticed.
[L204] 
[L205] She wrote:
[L206] 
[L207] **Copper‑gold particulate fall begins immediately post‑impact.**  
[L208] **Particle adheres to metal preferentially. Abrasive. Conductive?**
[L209] 
[L210] A runner pounded into the observatory corridor, breath tearing.
[L211] 
[L212] “Glass‑House!” he yelled. “Buildings—Tallow Street is down. People trapped. They’re calling for hold‑hands.”
[L213] 
[L214] Hold‑hands. The street term for manifestors—people who could do what the city couldn’t: put their palms to failing structure and tell it not yet.
[L215] 
[L216] Tiffani’s fingers tightened around her slate.
[L217] 
[L218] She had heard rumors. People with heat in their chest and light in their veins. Mostly Temple talk. Mostly lies that soothed grief.
[L219] 
[L220] But she had also seen numbers: unexplained survivals, load failures that didn’t kill everyone they should have.
[L221] 
[L222] She grabbed her coat, shoved her slate into the inner pocket, and ran.
[L223] 
[L224] On her way out, she glanced back at Seven‑Three through the port one last time.
[L225] 
[L226] It still sat wrong.
[L227] 
[L228] The sky had been cut.
[L229] 
[L230] And Saint Merro was about to learn what it meant when reality’s rules stopped matching the city’s.
[L231] 
[L232] ---
[L233] 
[L234] ## CHAPTER 3: FIRST CONTACT
[L235] Tallow Street had collapsed like a bad joint.
[L236] 
[L237] The upper terrace facade had folded inward, stone slabs riding down on support beams that should have held twice that load. People screamed from inside the dust cloud. Someone rang a bell hard enough to crack its cadence: emergency, emergency, emergency.
[L238] 
[L239] Jhace reached the site with his kit bouncing against his hip and copper in his mouth so strong it made him want to spit. He didn’t.
[L240] 
[L241] Spit was a waste of moisture. Underface rule.
[L242] 
[L243] A foreman waved him toward the breach. “We need a hold. We don’t have time.”
[L244] 
[L245] Jhace looked at the structure.
[L246] 
[L247] The failure line wasn’t clean. It wasn’t a single beam snapped. The whole building was trying to become rubble, one breath at a time. Load shifts rippled through it like a living animal struggling to die.
[L248] 
[L249] No crew could wedge that fast. No straps would catch enough. The people inside didn’t have minutes.
[L250] 
[L251] Jhace stepped under the broken lintel.
[L252] 
[L253] Heat hit his chest.
[L254] 
[L255] Not external. Internal. A sudden furnace‑flare behind his sternum that made him gasp.
[L256] 
[L257] Copper‑gold dust swirled around his boots, and for a heartbeat he felt it climb his legs like static.
[L258] 
[L259] He put both hands on the main support column.
[L260] 
[L261] The grid‑hum in the stone surged into his palms. The doubled harmonic snapped into alignment, like two mis‑timed gears finally meshing.
[L262] 
[L263] Light—thin, copper‑gold, filament‑fine—threaded from his hands into the column seam.
[L264] 
[L265] The building stopped moving.
[L266] 
[L267] Not fixed. Not safe. Just *paused*.
[L268] 
[L269] Jhace’s breath shook out of him. He could feel every shift in load through his fingertips: the weight of stone, the tension in iron, the panic‑motion of bodies inside.
[L270] 
[L271] “Move,” he barked, voice raw. “Get them out.”
[L272] 
[L273] Someone yelled back. Boots ran.
[L274] 
[L275] A woman slid into his peripheral view—practical coat, hair tied back, slate already in hand despite the dust.
[L276] 
[L277] Tiffani.
[L278] 
[L279] She didn’t look at him like a savior. She looked at him like a system that could fail.
[L280] 
[L281] “How long?” she demanded.
[L282] 
[L283] Jhace’s jaw clenched. “I don’t know.”
[L284] 
[L285] “Then we find out.” She stepped into the dust, coughed once, and shouted to the evac crew. “Clear corridor A first. Count bodies out. No guessing.”
[L286] 
[L287] Jhace tried to turn his head. The moment he shifted his attention, the heat in his chest flared sharper, like punishment for looking away.
[L288] 
[L289] “I have to watch the load,” he said through his teeth.
[L290] 
[L291] “Then watch it,” Tiffani snapped, already marking a line on her slate. “I’ll watch you.”
[L292] 
[L293] People came out in bursts: three, then five, then a cluster carrying an older man whose leg bent wrong. Each time the load shifted, Jhace’s hands tightened and the filament‑light brightened in response.
[L294] 
[L295] His heartbeat counted itself in his ears.
[L296] 
[L297] One hundred.
[L298] 
[L299] One hundred and twenty.
[L300] 
[L301] His arms started to tremble.
[L302] 
[L303] Not from fatigue. From something deeper—his muscles obeying and not obeying at the same time. A fine vibration that made his grip feel unreliable.
[L304] 
[L305] “Hold steady,” Tiffani said, quieter now, close enough he could hear her through the dust.
[L306] 
[L307] He didn’t answer. Answering took breath.
[L308] 
[L309] At two hundred heartbeats, his tremor spread into his forearms. At two‑twenty, his vision narrowed at the edges, copper‑gold sparks flickering across it like welding flash.
[L310] 
[L311] “How many?” he rasped.
[L312] 
[L313] Tiffani didn’t look up from the evac line. “Ten. Eleven. Twelve—”
[L314] 
[L315] A scream from inside. A child. High and thin and suddenly cut off.
[L316] 
[L317] Jhace’s hands shook harder.
[L318] 
[L319] “No,” he hissed, and the filament‑light surged, burning his palms numb.
[L320] 
[L321] “Go,” Tiffani shouted toward the breach. “Last sweep. Now.”
[L322] 
[L323] A rescuer crawled under the paused beam line and dragged a small body out by the shoulders. The child coughed dust and cried like she’d been holding it in for hours.
[L324] 
[L325] Tiffani’s voice cut clean through the chaos. “Twelve out. Corridor clear.”
[L326] 
[L327] Jhace felt the building try to move again the instant she said it, like it had been waiting for permission.
[L328] 
[L329] “Terminate,” Tiffani said, and there was no softness in it. Not cruelty. Protocol.
[L330] 
[L331] Jhace let the filament go.
[L332] 
[L333] The building settled the last inch it had been denied, stone grinding, iron squealing, but the void where bodies had been was now empty.
[L334] 
[L335] Jhace staggered back into open air.
[L336] 
[L337] His hands wouldn’t stop shaking.
[L338] 
[L339] The tremor was small—millimeters—but it was relentless. Like a ghost living inside his muscles, counting the dead and refusing to be quiet.
[L340] 
[L341] Tiffani stepped beside him, dust streaked across her face. “Name,” she said.
[L342] 
[L343] “Jhace Torrins.”
[L344] 
[L345] She wrote it without looking. “Duration: approximately two‑forty heartbeats. Cost marker: tremor onset at—”
[L346] 
[L347] “Don’t,” he snapped. Too hard. Too fast.
[L348] 
[L349] Tiffani met his eyes. “If we don’t measure it, we kill you next time.”
[L350] 
[L351] Jhace’s hands shook between them like evidence he hadn’t wanted.
[L352] 
[L353] He swallowed copper, tasted grit, and nodded once.
[L354] 
[L355] “Measure,” he said. “Then make sure there isn’t a next time like Junction Seven.”
[L356] 
[L357] Tiffani’s slate scratched.
[L358] 
[L359] Behind them, the bell rang again.
[L360] 
[L361] And the city kept falling.
[L362] 
[L363] ---
[L364] 
[L365] ## CHAPTER 4: PROTOCOL
[L366] Jhace’s workshop was a box of iron and heat tucked into the mid‑terrace, where you could hear the grid‑hum without being crushed by it.
[L367] 
[L368] Tiffani sat on an overturned crate with her slate on her knees, left leg extended slightly to ease the knee that had started locking after long stands on stone floors. She didn’t rest her back against anything. Always ready to move. Always ready to write.
[L369] Tiffani shifted her weight off her left leg without comment, like it was just another measurement she refused to log.
[L370] 
[L371] Jhace stood at his bench, trying to thread a bolt into a collar bracket.
[L372] 
[L373] His hands shook.
[L374] 
[L375] It wasn’t violent. It wasn’t obvious to someone who didn’t live inside his fingers. But the bolt kept kissing the threads wrong, slipping, catching, refusing to start.
[L376] 
[L377] He tried again. The bolt dropped. Metal on stone.
[L378] 
[L379] He stared at it like it had betrayed him.
[L380] 
[L381] Tiffani didn’t look up. “Your tremor amplitude increased during exertion.”
[L382] 
[L383] “Because I’m exerting,” Jhace muttered, bending to pick up the bolt. His fingertips felt numb where the filament‑light had burned them. Not pain. Absence.
[L384] 
[L385] “Because your body is remembering,” Tiffani corrected. “It thinks you’re still holding.”
[L386] 
[L387] Jhace set the bolt down and flexed his hand hard. The tremor didn’t care.
[L388] 
[L389] He hated that. He hated that his own muscles had become unreliable.
[L390] 
[L391] “What did you call it out there?” he asked.
[L392] 
[L393] Tiffani flipped her slate so he could see. She’d drawn a crude timeline.
[L394] 
[L395] **Activation onset: chest heat + copper taste**  
[L396] **Light manifestation: filament contact‑dependent**  
[L397] **Tremor: post‑termination, permanent marker**
[L398] 
[L399] “I need a taxonomy,” she said. “Stages. Because right now it’s just stories.”
[L400] 
[L401] Jhace wiped his palms on a work cloth. “And you think writing stages keeps people alive.”
[L402] 
[L403] “I think it keeps them from lying to themselves.” She tapped the line where she’d written *permanent*. “You can’t call it temporary. You can’t bargain with it.”
[L404] 
[L405] Jhace’s jaw worked.
[L406] 
[L407] He had bargained with Junction Seven. He had told himself the report would matter. That somebody else would fix it. That the city didn’t need him to fight every foreman with a schedule board.
[L408] 
[L409] He looked at his shaking hands. “Stage One,” he said, voice low. “Tremor.”
[L410] 
[L411] Tiffani nodded and wrote: **Stage One: tremor + filament residue. Functional. Marked.**
[L412] 
[L413] A knock hit the workshop door.
[L414] 
[L415] Bram Tallow leaned in like he was checking whether the room was still allowed to exist. Dust on his boots, sweat at his collar, grin already loaded.
[L416] 
[L417] “Heard you two went and grabbed Tallow Street by the throat,” Bram said. “Real romantic. The city tries to crush you, you crush back. Very intimate.”
[L418] 
[L419] Jhace didn’t look up. “Who’s asking?”
[L420] 
[L421] “Crown’s asking questions,” Bram said, and somehow still made it sound like a dirty joke. “Not because they care. Because they hate unknown tools walking around without a leash and a receipt.”
[L422] 
[L423] Tiffani’s pen paused. “What questions.”
[L424] 
[L425] “Names. Locations. ‘Verified capability.’ That sort of foreplay,” Bram said. He stepped fully inside, then stopped like the heat-grid might charge him rent. “Infrastructure office. Some administrator with hands so clean it makes you wonder what he’s washing off. Loves forms. Loves categories. Loves pretending paper is stronger than stone.”
[L426] 
[L427] Jhace’s jaw tightened. “To do what.”
[L428] 
[L429] Bram spread his hands. “To decide who gets to own you. Temple will call it divine. Guild will call it labor. Crown will call it law. Everybody wants a piece. Congratulations—you’re popular.”
[L430] 
[L431] “We don’t belong to any of them,” Tiffani said.
[L432] 
[L433] Bram laughed, quick and wrong. “Adorable. Tell that to a stamp. Try explaining ‘we don’t belong’ while a ceiling is coming down and someone’s screaming for a hold. They’ll file you under ‘non-compliant’ and act like that’s a weather condition.”
[L434] 
[L435] Jhace’s tremor ticked in his fingers. “So you’re here to warn us.”
[L436] 
[L437] “I’m here to tell you the door is already being measured,” Bram said. “I can buy you a Tide or two. Maybe. After that, they’ll show up with paperwork and smiles and start deciding which parts of you are ‘public infrastructure.’”
[L438] 
[L439] Tiffani’s mouth went flat. “Then we build methodology fast.”
[L440] 
[L441] Bram pointed at her slate like it offended him personally. “Yeah. Before they build it for you. Because if the Crown writes your protocol, it’s going to read like a job description. And you won’t like the benefits package.”
[L442] 
[L443] Jhace breathed in, tasted copper‑gold dust that still clung to his clothes, and felt the city’s hum through the floorboards.
[L444] 
[L445] “Then we build it fast,” he said. “Before they decide to build it for us.”
[L446] 
[L447] ---
[L448] 
[L449] ## CHAPTER 5: FIRST NETWORK
[L450] The grain warehouse sat in Sector Nine, where the terraces were newer and the beams were honest enough to still pretend they could be trusted.
[L451] 
[L452] The bell signal came late. That was the first problem.
[L453] 
[L454] When Jhace and Tiffani arrived, the warehouse wasn’t collapsed—yet. The support columns were bowing, groaning under a load they shouldn’t have been carrying because somebody had stacked wet grain too high to meet a schedule.
[L455] 
[L456] A foreman screamed, “Hold it! Hold it now!”
[L457] 
[L458] Jhace moved toward the nearest column out of habit.
[L459] 
[L460] Tiffani caught his sleeve. “Not yet.”
[L461] 
[L462] Jhace’s jaw tightened. “People are inside.”
[L463] 
[L464] “People are *leaving*,” she corrected, pointing.
[L465] 
[L466] She was right. Workers were already pouring out of the side doors, coughing, eyes wide. The structure wasn’t in free fall. It was in warning.
[L467] 
[L468] “We wedge first,” Tiffani said. “Then you hold if wedges fail.”
[L469] 
[L470] Jhace’s tremor wanted to argue. His chest heat wanted to flare. The hunger for that sudden alignment—pipe hum into palm, world pausing—sat behind his ribs like an itch.
[L471] 
[L472] He didn’t scratch it.
[L473] 
[L474] He dropped to one knee and shoved wedge slips under the first bowing brace. Bram’s crew followed, hands fast, straps ready. For once, labor did what labor could do.
[L475] 
[L476] The warehouse groaned again. Dust spilled from a seam.
[L477] 
[L478] A young man stepped into the brace line, hands hovering like he didn’t know what to do with them. Corven. Jhace had seen him in the tunnels, quiet, competent.
[L479] 
[L480] Corven swallowed. “I… I can feel it,” he said. “The hum. It’s—”
[L481] 
[L482] “Louder,” Jhace finished.
[L483] 
[L484] Corven nodded hard. “Like it’s in my teeth.”
[L485] 
[L486] Tiffani stepped close, slate already up. “If it hits your chest, tell me. If you taste copper, tell me. Do not improvise.”
[L487] 
[L488] Corven’s eyes flicked to the bending column. “If it goes—”
[L489] 
[L490] “Then we act,” Jhace said. “But not before.”
[L491] 
[L492] The wedges held for two breaths.
[L493] 
[L494] Then the central column shifted.
[L495] 
[L496] Not collapse. A decisive, ugly move—stone settling, iron flexing. The wedges screamed as they compressed.
[L497] 
[L498] The workers inside were still running.
[L499] 
[L500] “Now,” Tiffani said.
[L501] 
[L502] Jhace put both hands on the column.
[L503] 
[L504] Heat flared in his chest—familiar now, terrifying in its familiarity—and filament‑light threaded into the grain‑dust coated seam.
[L505] 
[L506] The column stopped shifting.
[L507] 
[L508] Corven stared at Jhace’s hands like they were a language he’d almost learned.
[L509] 
[L510] “Your turn,” Jhace said through gritted teeth.
[L511] 
[L512] Corven stepped forward and touched the secondary brace.
[L513] 
[L514] His breath hitched. Copper taste. Chest heat. Then light—thinner than Jhace’s, but real—threaded into the brace line.
[L515] 
[L516] Corven’s hands started to shake immediately.
[L517] 
[L518] “Stage One onset during hold,” Tiffani murmured, writing. “Early marker.”
[L519] 
[L520] “Keep moving! If you stop, you become debris!” Bram shouted to the evac crew. “Count them out! Loud. Fast. Like the Crown’s listening!”
[L521] 
[L522] Jhace’s tremor surged in sympathy, like the grid‑hum had tied their nervous systems together. His arms burned. His vision tunneled at the edges.
[L523] 
[L524] “How long?” Tiffani asked.
[L525] 
[L526] “Eighty,” Jhace rasped, counting heartbeats without thinking.
[L527] 
[L528] Corven’s jaw clenched. “I can do—”
[L529] 
[L530] “Do *exactly* what the structure needs,” Jhace snapped. “Nothing more.”
[L531] 
[L532] At one‑fifty, the last workers stumbled out. Two hundred‑plus bodies, alive because they’d been allowed time.
[L533] 
[L534] “Terminate,” Tiffani said.
[L535] 
[L536] Jhace released first. The column held under wedges now that the load had shifted down.
[L537] 
[L538] Corven released a heartbeat later and nearly fell.
[L539] 
[L540] Jhace caught him by the elbow, and the tremor in his own hands made the grip feel unreliable.
[L541] 
[L542] Corven looked at his shaking fingers with a kind of horror. “It’s not stopping.”
[L543] 
[L544] “It doesn’t,” Jhace said.
[L545] 
[L546] Tiffani wrote the number anyway: **219 evacuated. Zero casualties. Two operators marked.**
[L547] 
[L548] Bram approached, face hard. “Crown’s going to hear about this—and they’ll bring forms instead of shovels.”
[L549] 
[L550] Tiffani didn’t look up. “Good.”
[L551] 
[L552] Jhace’s chest still burned with the after‑heat of holding. The hunger whispered: *You could have held longer. You could have made it perfect.*
[L553] 
[L554] He swallowed copper and told the hunger to shut up.
[L555] 
[L556] For now.
[L557] 
[L558] ---
[L559] 
[L560] 
[L561] ---
[L562] 
[L563] ### ROUTE FAILURE (THREE DEAD)
[L564] 
[L565] The bell came from Sector Nine, not as a roar—just a single strike repeated until someone answered.
[L566] 
[L567] Residential stairwell. Upper-terrace split. Twelve families still inside, one route.
[L568] 
[L569] Jhace was already marked from the warehouse. Corven was worse. Senne was fresh enough to stand without shaking.
[L570] 
[L571] Tiffani looked at the slate wall and made the decision she would later pretend was only math.
[L572] 
[L573] Two operators at one‑eighty each meant coordination, handoffs, spotters, and failure points. One operator at two‑forty meant simplicity. A clean line.
[L574] 
[L575] “Single deployment,” she said. “Senne. Duration cap: two‑forty. Terminate on my bell.”
[L576] 
[L577] Senne went pale as the heat took her chest. Copper-gold light laced her forearms. Not pretty. Functional.
[L578] 
[L579] They reached the stairwell mouth as stone dust sifted from the ceiling like flour. The grid-hum underfoot carried the doubled harmonic, deep and insistent, a warning that didn’t care who listened.
[L580] 
[L581] Senne braced under the central landing. Jhace watched her shoulders set—metalworker’s posture in a glassworker’s frame.
[L582] 
[L583] Tiffani counted. Heartbeats, not seconds. Seconds lied when panic touched them.
[L584] 
[L585] One hundred.
[L586] 
[L587] One forty.
[L588] 
[L589] One eighty.
[L590] 
[L591] At one‑ninety, a secondary beam—one Tiffani hadn’t walked because she’d trusted the map—shifted with a sound like teeth grinding.
[L592] 
[L593] The evacuation lane kinked. The next family stumbled. The stone didn’t collapse all at once. It folded, patient and specific, closing the corridor into a narrow pocket.
[L594] 
[L595] Three people were on the wrong side when it did: two adults and a child coughing copper into his sleeve.
[L596] 
[L597] Tiffani saw them, and for a moment the slate in her hands felt obscene.
[L598] 
[L599] “Hold,” she said, voice flat. “Keep holding.”
[L600] 
[L601] Senne’s eyes didn’t move. She couldn’t spare the motion.
[L602] 
[L603] Two‑ten.
[L604] 
[L605] Two‑twenty.
[L606] 
[L607] Two‑thirty.
[L608] 
[L609] The pocket didn’t clear. The child’s coughing turned wet.
[L610] 
[L611] At two‑forty, Tiffani rang the termination sequence anyway. Because protocols existed for a reason. Because if she didn’t, she would never stop.
[L612] 
[L613] Senne released.
[L614] 
[L615] The stairwell collapsed into itself with a slow, final sound—stone settling into the shape it had wanted all along.
[L616] 
[L617] They dug for hours.
[L618] 
[L619] They found three bodies. The child first, because he was small enough to slip into the wrong angles.
[L620] 
[L621] Back in the workshop, Tiffani logged it with perfect precision.
[L622] 
[L623] **Deployment:** Senne — 240 heartbeats  
[L624] **Evacuated:** 47  
[L625] **Casualties:** 3  
[L626] **Cause:** Inadequate route assessment. Secondary beam shear.
[L627] 
[L628] She wrote “cause” as if it made the number less personal.
[L629] 
[L630] Later, alone, she re-read the line **Casualties: 3** until the ink blurred.
[L631] 
[L632] She didn’t cry. She didn’t speak. She just stared at the digit like it might change if she stared hard enough.
[L633] 
[L634] The next Tide, she rewrote the protocol.
[L635] 
[L636] **Route spotters required. Three per deployment. No exceptions.**
[L637] 
[L638] She underlined it twice.
[L639] 
[L640] The number stayed.
[L641] 
[L642] ---
[L643] 
[L644] ## CHAPTER 6: WITNESS
[L645] Brother Corran didn’t wear Temple colors.
[L646] 
[L647] That was the first thing Jhace noticed when the man stepped into the workshop two Tides after the warehouse. No robes. No beads. Just a plain coat and a Crown badge pinned where it couldn’t be missed.
[L648] 
[L649] Corran’s eyes went straight to Jhace’s hands.
[L650] 
[L651] “For the record, I’m not here to stop you,” Corran said, before anyone asked.
[L652] 
[L653] Bram hovered by the door like a lock that wanted to be useful.
[L654] 
[L655] Tiffani set her slate down deliberately. “Then why are you here?”
[L656] 
[L657] “Because you’re real,” Corran said. “And because real things attract predators.”
[L658] 
[L659] Jhace’s mouth tightened. “Crown.”
[L660] 
[L661] Corran’s gaze didn’t flinch. “Yes. Also Guild. Also Temple. Anyone who thinks they can turn you into a lever.”
[L662] 
[L663] He pulled a folded packet from his coat and set it on Jhace’s bench. Paper. Old style. Archive weight.
[L664] 
[L665] “Stage Three cases,” Corran said. “Documented. Not rumors.”
[L666] 
[L667] Tiffani’s hands went still. “How many?”
[L668] 
[L669] “Enough.” Corran’s voice was flat, and that flatness carried exhaustion. “Most die. A few don’t. The ones who don’t… wish they had.”
[L670] 
[L671] Jhace’s tremor ticked up at the word *die*. He forced his hands flat on the bench. “Why give this to us?”
[L672] 
[L673] Corran hesitated. That hesitation was the crack in the Crown.
[L674] 
[L675] “Because someone above me wants a registry,” he said. “Names. Locations. Deployment authority. They’re calling it order. It’s control.”
[L676] 
[L677] Tiffani’s eyes narrowed. “Who?”
[L678] 
[L679] Corran looked toward the door as if the walls might report him. “Administrator Corlexi. Infrastructure Compliance.”
[L680] 
[L681] Bram gave a low whistle. “Clean hands,” he muttered. “That one.”
[L682] 
[L683] Jhace stared at the packet. “So you’re here to warn us.”
[L684] 
[L685] “I’m here to give you data,” Corran corrected. “If you want to fight control, you need numbers. Not speeches.”
[L686] 
[L687] Tiffani opened the packet and read, eyes moving fast.
[L688] 
[L689] Jhace watched her face tighten at certain lines—durations, costs, outcomes. She didn’t gasp. She didn’t dramatize. She just absorbed.
[L690] 
[L691] “This case,” she said, tapping a page. “Stage Three survival. How?”
[L692] 
[L693] Corran’s mouth went thin. “He didn’t terminate. He couldn’t. The hold locked. Autonomic failure started and the manifestation kept running anyway. The structure held until it didn’t matter.”
[L694] 
[L695] Jhace felt cold crawl up his spine despite the workshop heat. “Locked hold.”
[L696] 
[L697] Corran nodded. “It’s not a tool you put down when you’re done. Sometimes it’s a hook in your chest that drags you until something breaks.”
[L698] 
[L699] Tiffani’s pen scratched. **Stage Three: autonomic failure. Locked hold possible. Termination may fail.**
[L700] 
[L701] Bram shifted. “You said predators.”
[L702] 
[L703] Corran’s eyes met Bram’s. “Tallec is filing complaints. Says you’re obstructing industry. Says your protocols slow production.”
[L704] 
[L705] Jhace’s jaw clenched so hard it hurt.
[L706] 
[L707] Corran leaned closer, voice low. “Here’s the fracture you wanted. I don’t like Corlexi’s registry. I don’t like Temple’s sermons. I don’t like Guild’s schedules. But I like fewer dead.”
[L708] 
[L709] He straightened. “If you build methodology, build it fast. Because the Crown is already drafting theirs. And theirs won’t care what it costs you.”
[L710] 
[L711] He turned to leave.
[L712] 
[L713] Tiffani called after him. “Why risk telling us this?”
[L714] 
[L715] Corran paused at the door. “Because Junction Seven wasn’t the first ignored report,” he said. “And I’m tired of pretending ignorance is the same as innocence.”
[L716] 
[L717] Then he was gone.
[L718] 
[L719] Tiffani climbed the workshop stairs slower than she had a Tide ago. Her right knee caught on the third step—not pain, just resistance, like the joint was learning to refuse.
[L720] 
[L721] She didn’t log it.
[L722] 
[L723] Some costs weren’t manifestation. Some were just labor.
[L724] 
[L725] Jhace’s hands shook on the bench like they were trying to answer him.
[L726] 
[L727] ---
[L728] 
[L729] ## CHAPTER 7: MERCHANT HALL
[L730] Merchant Hall was a cathedral for trade: iron ribs, stone spine, glass skylights that made everything inside look clean even when it wasn’t.
[L731] 
[L732] It started failing at mid‑Tide.
[L733] 
[L734] The bell sequence came sharp and frantic—no warning cadence, no time to wedge first. The Hall had been packed when the terrace shifted again.
[L735] 
[L736] Four hundred inside, Bram said. Maybe more.
[L737] 
[L738] Tiffani arrived before Jhace. When he pushed through the crowd, she was already on the steps, slate in hand, shouting like a foreman who’d learned math.
[L739] 
[L740] “Operators on me. Two per span. Spotters assigned. No hero holds.”
[L741] “Duration limit. One-eighty heartbeats maximum. You terminate on my signal. No argument.”
[L742] 
[L743] Merra shoved through the dust with a slate clutched to her chest. Ash streaked her hair like she’d tried to comb through a fire.
[L744] “We have a problem. Southern stairwell collapsed. Fifty people trapped on upper floors. Can’t reach main exits.”
[L745] 
[L746] “Time estimate,” Tiffani said. “Low and high.”
[L747] 
[L748] “Unknown. Debris blocking secondary stairs. Could be two hundred heartbeats. Could be six hundred.”
[L749] 
[L750] “Then we don’t gamble.” Tiffani didn’t raise her voice. She didn’t have to. “Clear lanes. Count bodies out. You want prayer, pray while you walk.”
[L751] 
[L752] She swung her slate toward the operators.
[L753] “Corven—baseline check. When chest heat starts, report it. Don’t interpret. Report.”
[L754] 
[L755] Corven swallowed. “If it hits, I tell you. If I taste copper, I tell you.”
[L756] 
[L757] Jhace didn’t look at him. “Don’t spiral. Do the minimum.”
[L758] 
[L759] “Minimum,” Corven echoed, like repetition could make it true.
[L760] 
[L761] “Senne—status.”
[L762] 
[L763] “Getting hollow,” Senne said. “Chest feels empty. Light’s harder to maintain.”
[L764] 
[L765] “That’s Stage Two warning.” Tiffani’s eyes cut sharp. “You terminate on my signal or I terminate you myself.”
[L766] 
[L767] Senne gave her a tired half-smile. “Sure. Die on schedule. Very professional.”
[L768] 
[L769] “Kess—report.”
[L770] 
[L771] “I am present and available for assignment.”
[L772] 
[L773] “Available doesn’t mean useful. Report symptoms. Stop performing.”
[L774] 
[L775] “Symptoms are within acceptable parameters for operational requirements.”
[L776] 
[L777] Bram leaned in, voice too bright for the amount of people about to die. “Translation: Kess is bleeding and pretending it’s fashion.”
[L778] 
[L779] “I am not bleeding.”
[L780] 
[L781] Merra didn’t bother being gentle. “Kess—your nose is bleeding.”
[L782] 
[L783] “I am aware of the nasal discharge. It is minor. Barely noteworthy. I’ve had worse nosebleeds from seasonal changes.”
[L784] 
[L785] “Yeah,” Bram said. “And I’ve had worse ideas than this hold. Doesn’t make them good.”
[L786] 
[L787] From the crowd, a Temple Observer screamed, “Let the worthy hold! Let the unworthy fall!”
[L788] 
[L789] Bram snapped his head toward the sound. “Hey—shut up. If we’re picking who falls, you’re first. That’s democracy.”
[L790] 
[L791] “Ignore them,” Tiffani said. “Move.”
[L792] 
[L793] “Spotters, call markers.”
[L794] 
[L795] “Jhace tremor increasing!”
[L796] “Corven tremor increasing!”
[L797] “Senne vein-line onset!”
[L798] 
[L799] Bram barked at the evac line like a foreman with a broken sense of humor. “Keep moving! Nobody stops! You stop, you become part of the debris! Count them out!”
[L800] 
[L801] Merra’s voice cut through the dust. “Alternate route open. Final evacuation commencing.”
[L802] 
[L803] “Count them,” Tiffani said. “Call numbers.”
[L804] 
[L805] Jhace’s breath came tight. “One hundred.”
[L806] 
[L807] “State exit count,” Tiffani said, “not your suffering.”
[L808] 
[L809] “One hundred. Out.”
[L810] 
[L811] Daved’s voice came rough through the grit. “They’re not out.”
[L812] 
[L813] Jhace’s head snapped. “No.”
[L814] 
[L815] “They’re not out.”
[L816] 
[L817] “Daved—state the variable,” Tiffani said. “How many still inside?”
[L818] 
[L819] “Enough.”
[L820] 
[L821] Tiffani didn’t blink. “Extend. Thirty heartbeats. No more.”
[L822] 
[L823] “Tiff—” Jhace started.
[L824] 
[L825] “I am not debating physics with the dead in the hallway,” she said. “Extend.”
[L826] 
[L827] Thirty heartbeats passed like a slow knife.
[L828] 
[L829] Merra shouted from the corridor mouth, “Final group emerging. Ten heartbeats!”
[L830] 
[L831] “Ten heartbeats is still ten,” Tiffani said. “Move.”
[L832] 
[L833] Daved’s hands tightened. “Hold.”
[L834] 
[L835] “Daved!” Jhace lunged a step closer.
[L836] 
[L837] “Hold.”
[L838] 
[L839] Merra’s slate slapped stone. “Clear! All civilians safe!”
[L840] 
[L841] “Terminate all operators,” Tiffani snapped. “Immediate coordinated shutdown.”
[L842] 
[L843] Lights guttered and went out—one by one—except Daved’s.
[L844] 
[L845] “Can’t,” Daved said.
[L846] 
[L847] “Daved. Breathe,” Jhace said, too close, too desperate.
[L848] 
[L849] “Can’t.”
[L850] 
[L851] “Terminate,” Tiffani said. “That is not a negotiation.”
[L852] 
[L853] “They’re not out.”
[L854] 
[L855] “They are out. Merra confirmed. Terminate now.”
[L856] 
[L857] Daved’s jaw worked like he was chewing copper. “Already crossed.”
[L858] 
[L859] Bram’s voice barged in where it shouldn’t have fit. “Daved—buddy—love the commitment, hate the method acting.”
[L860] 
[L861] “Bram, shut up,” Jhace hissed.
[L862] 
[L863] “I can’t,” Bram said. “It’s a disorder.”
[L864] 
[L865] Tiffani stepped in until Daved had to see her. “Look at me. If you extend past this, you cross Stage Three threshold.”
[L866] 
[L867] Daved’s eyes stayed flat. “Already crossed.”
[L868] 
[L869] Jhace’s throat tightened. “He locked.”
[L870] 
[L871] “I see it,” Tiffani said.
[L872] 
[L873] They hauled Daved back from the structure like pulling a hook out of meat.
[L874] 
[L875] “Terminate!” Tiffani shouted.
[L876] 
[L877] The light finally cut.
[L878] 
[L879] Daved folded like a tool dropped from numb hands.
[L880] 
[L881] Tiffani’s voice went small for one syllable. “I know.”
[L882] 
[L883] 
[L884] They carried Daved out on a door panel when the Hall finally stopped shifting enough to allow hands near him.
[L885] 
[L886] His eyes were open.
[L887] 
[L888] His body was too still.
[L889] 
[L890] Tiffani walked beside the makeshift stretcher, writing with one hand, wiping dust from her face with the other.
[L891] ---
[L892] 
[L893] Tiffani sat alone in the documentation room after they carried Daved out.
[L894] 
[L895] Her slate lay open to the cost taxonomy. Stage Three. Locked hold. Autonomic failure.
[L896] 
[L897] She had written it perfectly. Every marker. Every heartbeat. Every outcome.
[L898] 
[L899] Her hand moved to add the summary line.
[L900] 
[L901] She stared at the slate. At the line that read: **Casualties: 3**.
[L902] 
[L903] She tried to write the justification. The calculation. The reason extending duration had been correct under the circumstances.
[L904] 
[L905] The chalk snapped.
[L906] 
[L907] Not from pressure. From tremor. Her tremor. Not manifestation cost. Exhaustion.
[L908] 
[L909] She stared at her shaking fingers.
[L910] 
[L911] She didn’t log her tremor. She didn’t log that she’d miscounted heartbeats during the hold because her vision had blurred. She didn’t log that she’d wanted to ring the termination bell at one-eighty but hadn’t, because Daved’s eyes had asked her not to.
[L912] 
[L913] Some data points weren’t for the ledger.
[L914] 
[L915] Some were just for carrying.
[L916] 
[L917] 
[L918] ---
[L919] 
[L920] 
[L921] Jhace’s tremor made his jaw ache.
[L922] 
[L923] He had wanted method.
[L924] 
[L925] Method had just asked for a price it didn’t have the right to demand.
[L926] 
[L927] ---
[L928] 
[L929] ## CHAPTER 8: THREE TIDES
[L930] Daved woke on the third Tide.
[L931] 
[L932] That was the number that mattered first. Not the miracle. Not the rumor. The count.
[L933] 
[L934] Jhace stood by the cot in the infirmary room, hands shaking in front of him like they were arguing with air. Tiffani stood on the other side, slate ready, eyes red at the edges from lack of sleep she refused to name.
[L935] 
[L936] Daved’s eyes opened slowly.
[L937] 
[L938] His hands were strapped to the cot rails—not as restraint, but as prevention. His fingers still twitched with phantom hold.
[L939] 
[L940] He tried to speak. His throat made a sound like stone rubbing stone.
[L941] 
[L942] Tiffani leaned in. “Don’t force voice yet. Use slate.”
[L943] 
[L944] A small writing board lay on his chest with chalk tied to it by cord.
[L945] 
[L946] Daved’s fingers moved, slow and ugly, as if the tendons had forgotten the path.
[L947] 
[L948] He wrote:
[L949] 
[L950] **Did it work**
[L951] 
[L952] Jhace swallowed. “They got thirty‑two out.”
[L953] 
[L954] Daved stared at the ceiling for a long time.
[L955] 
[L956] Then his fingers scraped
[L957] The file is too long and its contents have been truncated.

[L1] # N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS
[L2] *Turning: GLASS (G) — Tide 0 through Tide 21 (Three Anneals post-Piercing)*
[L3] 
[L4] ---
[L5] 
[L6] ## CHAPTER 1: SOMETHING WRONG
[L7] Junction Seven wore its dead like a badge you couldn’t scrub off.
[L8] 
[L9] Twenty‑three names, cut into iron plate and bolted to stone. Jhace read them anyway. Not for ceremony. For calibration. If he let the names blur, he would let the work blur. That was the rule now.
[L10] 
[L11] Harren Corl. Bessa Tyne. Mikael Stradd.
[L12] 
[L13] His thumb traced the last groove. The plate was cold enough to bite. The Underface never fully warmed—heat rose, but the terraces drank it before it reached this depth.
[L14] 
[L15] He set his kit on the pipe shelf and unpacked by touch. Chalk. Slate. Measuring strap. Collar keys. Wedge slips. A flask of lamp oil he never used but always carried because rules were rules.
[L16] 
[L17] He pressed his palm to the main supply line.
[L18] 
[L19] The grid‑hum was there—constant low vibration carried through iron and stone, like the city breathing through a cracked lung. Baseline. Familiar.
[L20] 
[L21] Then he felt the second layer.
[L22] 
[L23] The second layer wasn’t a rattle or loose bolts; it was a doubled harmonic—the hum carrying a deepened echo underneath it, out of phase by a fraction that made his teeth itch.
[L24] 
[L25] Jhace pulled his hand away like the pipe had burned him.
[L26] 
[L27] He put it back. Same.
[L28] 
[L29] He moved two spans downslope. Same doubled echo, slightly stronger. He moved upslope. Weaker. The source was below, not above. Underface load, not terrace load.
[L30] 
[L31] He marked the pipe with chalk and wrote on his slate:
[L32] 
[L33] **Tide 0 — Kindling**  
[L34] **J7 main line: doubled harmonic present.**  
[L35] **Not local. Carries through junction.**
[L36] 
[L37] He hated writing *not local*. It meant the problem wasn’t one joint he could reseat. It meant a system shift.
[L38] 
[L39] A tunnel worker came through the junction, carrying a spool of cable on one shoulder. Chloen. Jhace knew her by the way she moved: efficient, eyes down, never wasting stride.
[L40] 
[L41] “Inspector,” she said without slowing.
[L42] 
[L43] “Chloen.” He watched the cable roll bounce against her back. “You feel it today?”
[L44] 
[L45] She paused like she hadn’t meant to. “Feel what?”
[L46] 
[L47] “The hum.”
[L48] 
[L49] Her mouth tightened. “It’s… wrong.”
[L50] 
[L51] Jhace nodded once. “Where?”
[L52] 
[L53] Chloen shifted her grip, and for a second her fingers trembled against the spool’s edge. Not exhaustion. Not cold. Contact‑dependent. The kind of tremor you only saw when skin met metal.
[L54] 
[L55] “It gets worse near Twelve,” she said. “Third branch. Sector Fourteen feed.”
[L56] 
[L57] Jhace’s gut sank. Junction Twelve fed the kiln quarter. If that line failed, half the lower terraces would riot before the stone cooled.
[L58] 
[L59] “You report it?”
[L60] 
[L61] She gave him a look that was half amusement, half bitterness. “To who? Foreman says keep flow. Crown says file forms.”
[L62] 
[L63] “File anyway,” Jhace said. “To me.”
[L64] 
[L65] He started walking.
[L66] 
[L67] Junction Twelve was eight turns of the wrist by map, but maps lied down here. Underface shifted. Stone settled. Pipes bowed a fraction every Anneal, and if you didn’t walk the route you didn’t know the city.
[L68] 
[L69] As he moved, the hum thickened. The doubled harmonic sharpened into something with edges. His mouth tasted copper, faint and metallic, like he’d bitten his own tongue.
[L70] 
[L71] He stopped at the collar that fed Sector Fourteen’s third branch.
[L72] 
[L73] The collar had been reseated recently. Fresh tool marks. New wedge slips. Someone had tightened it fast and called it good. The seam line wasn’t flush.
[L74] 
[L75] Jhace set his measuring strap, checked the angle, and felt the pressure with his fingertips. The collar was carrying load wrong—micro‑shear on the downslope lip.
[L76] 
[L77] He wrote:
[L78] 
[L79] **J12 S14‑3 collar: seam not flush. Micro‑shear present. Risk point.**  
[L80] **Recommend shutdown + reseat.**
[L81] 
[L82] Boot steps behind him. Heavy. Foreman boots.
[L83] 
[L84] Tallec—Sector Fourteen’s floor lead—appeared with two crew. Face dirty, eyes sharp, hands clean enough to prove he hadn’t been the one down here doing the work.
[L85] 
[L86] “Inspector,” Tallec said. “That line stays open. We’re already down two pours.”
[L87] 
[L88] Jhace didn’t look up from the collar. “It shouldn’t be open.”
[L89] 
[L90] “It feeds the kiln quarter. We’re in Kindling. You shut it, we lose pours.”
[L91] 
[L92] “You keep it open, you lose people.”
[L93] 
[L94] Tallec crouched beside him, close enough Jhace could smell the steam on his breath. “You always say that. Junction Seven. You want to make it your sermon.”
[L95] 
[L96] Jhace’s fingers went tight around the collar key. His hands didn’t shake yet. They would, someday. He didn’t know it. But he felt the pressure in the metal like it was already there.
[L97] 
[L98] “This seam is mis‑seated,” he said. “Not opinion. Physics.”
[L99] 
[L100] Tallec’s jaw worked. “Reseat it after the next pour.”
[L101] 
[L102] “No.” Jhace stood. “Now.”
[L103] 
[L104] Tallec rose too. “You shut us down, I’ll file you for obstruction. Crown listens to my numbers. Not your fear.”
[L105] 
[L106] Jhace held his slate between them like a shield. “Then put your name under mine when the collar fails.”
[L107] 
[L108] For a breath, the tunnel went quiet except for the hum.
[L109] 
[L110] Then the hum dipped.
[L111] 
[L112] Not in volume. In pitch. A low sag that rolled through the pipes like a living thing shifting its weight.
[L113] 
[L114] Every hair on Jhace’s arms lifted.
[L115] 
[L116] Chloen’s tremor returned hard enough to make the spool bite her shoulder. “That wasn’t local,” she whispered.
[L117] 
[L118] Jhace looked at the ceiling stone. Dust shivered loose, not from a nearby impact, but as if the whole terrace had been told to remember gravity.
[L119] 
[L120] He made his choice the way he always did. Not fast. Not heroic. Just correct.
[L121] 
[L122] “Deeper first,” he said to himself. “Then up.”
[L123] 
[L124] He grabbed his kit, shoved his slate into his coat, and moved past Tallec toward the downslope access shaft.
[L125] 
[L126] Behind him, Tallec cursed and yelled for his crew to keep flow.
[L127] 
[L128] Jhace didn’t answer.
[L129] 
[L130] The doubled harmonic followed him like a warning that had learned to walk.
[L131] 
[L132] ---
[L133] 
[L134] ## CHAPTER 2: GLASS THAT LISTENS
[L135] The observatory wasn’t a tower.
[L136] 
[L137] Saint Merro didn’t waste elevation on beauty. The Glass‑House sat half‑buried in the upper terrace wall, its lens ports bored through stone like gun barrels aimed at the sky.
[L138] 
[L139] Tiffani Merrow had been awake since the first tremor. Not the kind you felt through your feet—those came and went with machinery load. This one lived in the instruments.
[L140] 
[L141] She set her palm on the primary lens frame and felt vibration where there should have been nothing. The glass was listening.
[L142] 
[L143] Her slate already held numbers. Projection curves. Angle corrections. Reference points she’d used for years.
[L144] 
[L145] Seven‑Three was wrong.
[L146] 
[L147] Not by a fraction. By a displacement that made her stomach tighten.
[L148] 
[L149] She leaned into the eyepiece again, adjusted, checked the crosshair, and watched the star sit where it didn’t belong.
[L150] 
[L151] “Shift confirmed,” she murmured, and her voice sounded like someone else’s.
[L152] 
[L153] She wrote:
[L154] 
[L155] **Tide 0 — Kindling**  
[L156] **Seven‑Three displacement: 0.34 degrees west.**  
[L157] **Not atmospheric. Not lens warp.**
[L158] 
[L159] Copper hit her tongue.
[L160] 
[L161] It wasn’t taste from the air vents. It was sudden, metallic, and clean—like a coin held under the tongue too long.
[L162] 
[L163] She lifted her head, and the light outside the lens port changed.
[L164] 
[L165] The light wasn’t dim or bright; it was bent.
[L166] 
[L167] The sky over Saint Merro wasn’t supposed to do anything except hold weather. Today it held something else.
[L168] 
[L169] A line appeared. Not a cloud seam. Not smoke. A straight, dark cut across the upper sky that moved too fast for her eyes to track at first. Then it slowed enough for her to understand what she was seeing.
[L170] 
[L171] A spindle.
[L172] 
[L173] Long and narrow and wrong, like someone had driven a forged bar through the world.
[L174] 
[L175] Her breath stalled.
[L176] 
[L177] The spindle crossed the lens view in a single heartbeat. No friction fire. No sound. Just a displacement of light that made the air look thin.
[L178] 
[L179] Then the world punched.
[L180] 
[L181] Gravity staggered. Not a shift to one side—an up‑and‑down lurch like the planet had been grabbed and shaken once.
[L182] 
[L183] Tiffani’s slate slid across the stone desk. A lens clamp snapped. Glass sang a sharp, stressed note and then went silent.
[L184] 
[L185] She steadied herself with both hands and forced her eyes back to the port.
[L186] 
[L187] The spindle wasn’t above them anymore.
[L188] 
[L189] It was gone through.
[L190] 
[L191] A pressure wave arrived a breath later. Not a blast—more like the city inhaling and not knowing what to do with the air. Dust fell from the ceiling mortar in a slow, dry drift.
[L192] 
[L193] And then the dust outside fell too.
[L194] 
[L195] Copper‑gold particulate, glittering in the lens light like ground glass in a furnace beam. It coated the stone lip of the port and clung to the iron frame in flecks that shimmered when she moved.
[L196] 
[L197] Tiffani touched it with her fingertip.
[L198] 
[L199] It didn’t smear like ash. It gritted. Sharp, fine, abrasive.
[L200] 
[L201] Her finger tingled.
[L202] 
[L203] She pulled back, stared at the dust on her skin, and watched it catch the light as if it wanted to be noticed.
[L204] 
[L205] She wrote:
[L206] 
[L207] **Copper‑gold particulate fall begins immediately post‑impact.**  
[L208] **Particle adheres to metal preferentially. Abrasive. Conductive?**
[L209] 
[L210] A runner pounded into the observatory corridor, breath tearing.
[L211] 
[L212] “Glass‑House!” he yelled. “Buildings—Tallow Street is down. People trapped. They’re calling for hold‑hands.”
[L213] 
[L214] Hold‑hands. The street term for manifestors—people who could do what the city couldn’t: put their palms to failing structure and tell it not yet.
[L215] 
[L216] Tiffani’s fingers tightened around her slate.
[L217] 
[L218] She had heard rumors. People with heat in their chest and light in their veins. Mostly Temple talk. Mostly lies that soothed grief.
[L219] 
[L220] But she had also seen numbers: unexplained survivals, load failures that didn’t kill everyone they should have.
[L221] 
[L222] She grabbed her coat, shoved her slate into the inner pocket, and ran.
[L223] 
[L224] On her way out, she glanced back at Seven‑Three through the port one last time.
[L225] 
[L226] It still sat wrong.
[L227] 
[L228] The sky had been cut.
[L229] 
[L230] And Saint Merro was about to learn what it meant when reality’s rules stopped matching the city’s.
[L231] 
[L232] ---
[L233] 
[L234] ## CHAPTER 3: FIRST CONTACT
[L235] Tallow Street had collapsed like a bad joint.
[L236] 
[L237] The upper terrace facade had folded inward, stone slabs riding down on support beams that should have held twice that load. People screamed from inside the dust cloud. Someone rang a bell hard enough to crack its cadence: emergency, emergency, emergency.
[L238] 
[L239] Jhace reached the site with his kit bouncing against his hip and copper in his mouth so strong it made him want to spit. He didn’t.
[L240] 
[L241] Spit was a waste of moisture. Underface rule.
[L242] 
[L243] A foreman waved him toward the breach. “We need a hold. We don’t have time.”
[L244] 
[L245] Jhace looked at the structure.
[L246] 
[L247] The failure line wasn’t clean. It wasn’t a single beam snapped. The whole building was trying to become rubble, one breath at a time. Load shifts rippled through it like a living animal struggling to die.
[L248] 
[L249] No crew could wedge that fast. No straps would catch enough. The people inside didn’t have minutes.
[L250] 
[L251] Jhace stepped under the broken lintel.
[L252] 
[L253] Heat hit his chest.
[L254] 
[L255] Not external. Internal. A sudden furnace‑flare behind his sternum that made him gasp.
[L256] 
[L257] Copper‑gold dust swirled around his boots, and for a heartbeat he felt it climb his legs like static.
[L258] 
[L259] He put both hands on the main support column.
[L260] 
[L261] The grid‑hum in the stone surged into his palms. The doubled harmonic snapped into alignment, like two mis‑timed gears finally meshing.
[L262] 
[L263] Light—thin, copper‑gold, filament‑fine—threaded from his hands into the column seam.
[L264] 
[L265] The building stopped moving.
[L266] 
[L267] Not fixed. Not safe. Just *paused*.
[L268] 
[L269] Jhace’s breath shook out of him. He could feel every shift in load through his fingertips: the weight of stone, the tension in iron, the panic‑motion of bodies inside.
[L270] 
[L271] “Move,” he barked, voice raw. “Get them out.”
[L272] 
[L273] Someone yelled back. Boots ran.
[L274] 
[L275] A woman slid into his peripheral view—practical coat, hair tied back, slate already in hand despite the dust.
[L276] 
[L277] Tiffani.
[L278] 
[L279] She didn’t look at him like a savior. She looked at him like a system that could fail.
[L280] 
[L281] “How long?” she demanded.
[L282] 
[L283] Jhace’s jaw clenched. “I don’t know.”
[L284] 
[L285] “Then we find out.” She stepped into the dust, coughed once, and shouted to the evac crew. “Clear corridor A first. Count bodies out. No guessing.”
[L286] 
[L287] Jhace tried to turn his head. The moment he shifted his attention, the heat in his chest flared sharper, like punishment for looking away.
[L288] 
[L289] “I have to watch the load,” he said through his teeth.
[L290] 
[L291] “Then watch it,” Tiffani snapped, already marking a line on her slate. “I’ll watch you.”
[L292] 
[L293] People came out in bursts: three, then five, then a cluster carrying an older man whose leg bent wrong. Each time the load shifted, Jhace’s hands tightened and the filament‑light brightened in response.
[L294] 
[L295] His heartbeat counted itself in his ears.
[L296] 
[L297] One hundred.
[L298] 
[L299] One hundred and twenty.
[L300] 
[L301] His arms started to tremble.
[L302] 
[L303] Not from fatigue. From something deeper—his muscles obeying and not obeying at the same time. A fine vibration that made his grip feel unreliable.
[L304] 
[L305] “Hold steady,” Tiffani said, quieter now, close enough he could hear her through the dust.
[L306] 
[L307] He didn’t answer. Answering took breath.
[L308] 
[L309] At two hundred heartbeats, his tremor spread into his forearms. At two‑twenty, his vision narrowed at the edges, copper‑gold sparks flickering across it like welding flash.
[L310] 
[L311] “How many?” he rasped.
[L312] 
[L313] Tiffani didn’t look up from the evac line. “Ten. Eleven. Twelve—”
[L314] 
[L315] A scream from inside. A child. High and thin and suddenly cut off.
[L316] 
[L317] Jhace’s hands shook harder.
[L318] 
[L319] “No,” he hissed, and the filament‑light surged, burning his palms numb.
[L320] 
[L321] “Go,” Tiffani shouted toward the breach. “Last sweep. Now.”
[L322] 
[L323] A rescuer crawled under the paused beam line and dragged a small body out by the shoulders. The child coughed dust and cried like she’d been holding it in for hours.
[L324] 
[L325] Tiffani’s voice cut clean through the chaos. “Twelve out. Corridor clear.”
[L326] 
[L327] Jhace felt the building try to move again the instant she said it, like it had been waiting for permission.
[L328] 
[L329] “Terminate,” Tiffani said, and there was no softness in it. Not cruelty. Protocol.
[L330] 
[L331] Jhace let the filament go.
[L332] 
[L333] The building settled the last inch it had been denied, stone grinding, iron squealing, but the void where bodies had been was now empty.
[L334] 
[L335] Jhace staggered back into open air.
[L336] 
[L337] His hands wouldn’t stop shaking.
[L338] 
[L339] The tremor was small—millimeters—but it was relentless. Like a ghost living inside his muscles, counting the dead and refusing to be quiet.
[L340] 
[L341] Tiffani stepped beside him, dust streaked across her face. “Name,” she said.
[L342] 
[L343] “Jhace Torrins.”
[L344] 
[L345] She wrote it without looking. “Duration: approximately two‑forty heartbeats. Cost marker: tremor onset at—”
[L346] 
[L347] “Don’t,” he snapped. Too hard. Too fast.
[L348] 
[L349] Tiffani met his eyes. “If we don’t measure it, we kill you next time.”
[L350] 
[L351] Jhace’s hands shook between them like evidence he hadn’t wanted.
[L352] 
[L353] He swallowed copper, tasted grit, and nodded once.
[L354] 
[L355] “Measure,” he said. “Then make sure there isn’t a next time like Junction Seven.”
[L356] 
[L357] Tiffani’s slate scratched.
[L358] 
[L359] Behind them, the bell rang again.
[L360] 
[L361] And the city kept falling.
[L362] 
[L363] ---
[L364] 
[L365] ## CHAPTER 4: PROTOCOL
[L366] Jhace’s workshop was a box of iron and heat tucked into the mid‑terrace, where you could hear the grid‑hum without being crushed by it.
[L367] 
[L368] Tiffani sat on an overturned crate with her slate on her knees, left leg extended slightly to ease the knee that had started locking after long stands on stone floors. She didn’t rest her back against anything. Always ready to move. Always ready to write.
[L369] Tiffani shifted her weight off her left leg without comment, like it was just another measurement she refused to log.
[L370] 
[L371] Jhace stood at his bench, trying to thread a bolt into a collar bracket.
[L372] 
[L373] His hands shook.
[L374] 
[L375] It wasn’t violent. It wasn’t obvious to someone who didn’t live inside his fingers. But the bolt kept kissing the threads wrong, slipping, catching, refusing to start.
[L376] 
[L377] He tried again. The bolt dropped. Metal on stone.
[L378] 
[L379] He stared at it like it had betrayed him.
[L380] 
[L381] Tiffani didn’t look up. “Your tremor amplitude increased during exertion.”
[L382] 
[L383] “Because I’m exerting,” Jhace muttered, bending to pick up the bolt. His fingertips felt numb where the filament‑light had burned them. Not pain. Absence.
[L384] 
[L385] “Because your body is remembering,” Tiffani corrected. “It thinks you’re still holding.”
[L386] 
[L387] Jhace set the bolt down and flexed his hand hard. The tremor didn’t care.
[L388] 
[L389] He hated that. He hated that his own muscles had become unreliable.
[L390] 
[L391] “What did you call it out there?” he asked.
[L392] 
[L393] Tiffani flipped her slate so he could see. She’d drawn a crude timeline.
[L394] 
[L395] **Activation onset: chest heat + copper taste**  
[L396] **Light manifestation: filament contact‑dependent**  
[L397] **Tremor: post‑termination, permanent marker**
[L398] 
[L399] “I need a taxonomy,” she said. “Stages. Because right now it’s just stories.”
[L400] 
[L401] Jhace wiped his palms on a work cloth. “And you think writing stages keeps people alive.”
[L402] 
[L403] “I think it keeps them from lying to themselves.” She tapped the line where she’d written *permanent*. “You can’t call it temporary. You can’t bargain with it.”
[L404] 
[L405] Jhace’s jaw worked.
[L406] 
[L407] He had bargained with Junction Seven. He had told himself the report would matter. That somebody else would fix it. That the city didn’t need him to fight every foreman with a schedule board.
[L408] 
[L409] He looked at his shaking hands. “Stage One,” he said, voice low. “Tremor.”
[L410] 
[L411] Tiffani nodded and wrote: **Stage One: tremor + filament residue. Functional. Marked.**
[L412] 
[L413] A knock hit the workshop door.
[L414] 
[L415] Bram Tallow leaned in like he was checking whether the room was still allowed to exist. Dust on his boots, sweat at his collar, grin already loaded.
[L416] 
[L417] “Heard you two went and grabbed Tallow Street by the throat,” Bram said. “Real romantic. The city tries to crush you, you crush back. Very intimate.”
[L418] 
[L419] Jhace didn’t look up. “Who’s asking?”
[L420] 
[L421] “Crown’s asking questions,” Bram said, and somehow still made it sound like a dirty joke. “Not because they care. Because they hate unknown tools walking around without a leash and a receipt.”
[L422] 
[L423] Tiffani’s pen paused. “What questions.”
[L424] 
[L425] “Names. Locations. ‘Verified capability.’ That sort of foreplay,” Bram said. He stepped fully inside, then stopped like the heat-grid might charge him rent. “Infrastructure office. Some administrator with hands so clean it makes you wonder what he’s washing off. Loves forms. Loves categories. Loves pretending paper is stronger than stone.”
[L426] 
[L427] Jhace’s jaw tightened. “To do what.”
[L428] 
[L429] Bram spread his hands. “To decide who gets to own you. Temple will call it divine. Guild will call it labor. Crown will call it law. Everybody wants a piece. Congratulations—you’re popular.”
[L430] 
[L431] “We don’t belong to any of them,” Tiffani said.
[L432] 
[L433] Bram laughed, quick and wrong. “Adorable. Tell that to a stamp. Try explaining ‘we don’t belong’ while a ceiling is coming down and someone’s screaming for a hold. They’ll file you under ‘non-compliant’ and act like that’s a weather condition.”
[L434] 
[L435] Jhace’s tremor ticked in his fingers. “So you’re here to warn us.”
[L436] 
[L437] “I’m here to tell you the door is already being measured,” Bram said. “I can buy you a Tide or two. Maybe. After that, they’ll show up with paperwork and smiles and start deciding which parts of you are ‘public infrastructure.’”
[L438] 
[L439] Tiffani’s mouth went flat. “Then we build methodology fast.”
[L440] 
[L441] Bram pointed at her slate like it offended him personally. “Yeah. Before they build it for you. Because if the Crown writes your protocol, it’s going to read like a job description. And you won’t like the benefits package.”
[L442] 
[L443] Jhace breathed in, tasted copper‑gold dust that still clung to his clothes, and felt the city’s hum through the floorboards.
[L444] 
[L445] “Then we build it fast,” he said. “Before they decide to build it for us.”
[L446] 
[L447] ---
[L448] 
[L449] ## CHAPTER 5: FIRST NETWORK
[L450] The grain warehouse sat in Sector Nine, where the terraces were newer and the beams were honest enough to still pretend they could be trusted.
[L451] 
[L452] The bell signal came late. That was the first problem.
[L453] 
[L454] When Jhace and Tiffani arrived, the warehouse wasn’t collapsed—yet. The support columns were bowing, groaning under a load they shouldn’t have been carrying because somebody had stacked wet grain too high to meet a schedule.
[L455] 
[L456] A foreman screamed, “Hold it! Hold it now!”
[L457] 
[L458] Jhace moved toward the nearest column out of habit.
[L459] 
[L460] Tiffani caught his sleeve. “Not yet.”
[L461] 
[L462] Jhace’s jaw tightened. “People are inside.”
[L463] 
[L464] “People are *leaving*,” she corrected, pointing.
[L465] 
[L466] She was right. Workers were already pouring out of the side doors, coughing, eyes wide. The structure wasn’t in free fall. It was in warning.
[L467] 
[L468] “We wedge first,” Tiffani said. “Then you hold if wedges fail.”
[L469] 
[L470] Jhace’s tremor wanted to argue. His chest heat wanted to flare. The hunger for that sudden alignment—pipe hum into palm, world pausing—sat behind his ribs like an itch.
[L471] 
[L472] He didn’t scratch it.
[L473] 
[L474] He dropped to one knee and shoved wedge slips under the first bowing brace. Bram’s crew followed, hands fast, straps ready. For once, labor did what labor could do.
[L475] 
[L476] The warehouse groaned again. Dust spilled from a seam.
[L477] 
[L478] A young man stepped into the brace line, hands hovering like he didn’t know what to do with them. Corven. Jhace had seen him in the tunnels, quiet, competent.
[L479] 
[L480] Corven swallowed. “I… I can feel it,” he said. “The hum. It’s—”
[L481] 
[L482] “Louder,” Jhace finished.
[L483] 
[L484] Corven nodded hard. “Like it’s in my teeth.”
[L485] 
[L486] Tiffani stepped close, slate already up. “If it hits your chest, tell me. If you taste copper, tell me. Do not improvise.”
[L487] 
[L488] Corven’s eyes flicked to the bending column. “If it goes—”
[L489] 
[L490] “Then we act,” Jhace said. “But not before.”
[L491] 
[L492] The wedges held for two breaths.
[L493] 
[L494] Then the central column shifted.
[L495] 
[L496] Not collapse. A decisive, ugly move—stone settling, iron flexing. The wedges screamed as they compressed.
[L497] 
[L498] The workers inside were still running.
[L499] 
[L500] “Now,” Tiffani said.
[L501] 
[L502] Jhace put both hands on the column.
[L503] 
[L504] Heat flared in his chest—familiar now, terrifying in its familiarity—and filament‑light threaded into the grain‑dust coated seam.
[L505] 
[L506] The column stopped shifting.
[L507] 
[L508] Corven stared at Jhace’s hands like they were a language he’d almost learned.
[L509] 
[L510] “Your turn,” Jhace said through gritted teeth.
[L511] 
[L512] Corven stepped forward and touched the secondary brace.
[L513] 
[L514] His breath hitched. Copper taste. Chest heat. Then light—thinner than Jhace’s, but real—threaded into the brace line.
[L515] 
[L516] Corven’s hands started to shake immediately.
[L517] 
[L518] “Stage One onset during hold,” Tiffani murmured, writing. “Early marker.”
[L519] 
[L520] “Keep moving! If you stop, you become debris!” Bram shouted to the evac crew. “Count them out! Loud. Fast. Like the Crown’s listening!”
[L521] 
[L522] Jhace’s tremor surged in sympathy, like the grid‑hum had tied their nervous systems together. His arms burned. His vision tunneled at the edges.
[L523] 
[L524] “How long?” Tiffani asked.
[L525] 
[L526] “Eighty,” Jhace rasped, counting heartbeats without thinking.
[L527] 
[L528] Corven’s jaw clenched. “I can do—”
[L529] 
[L530] “Do *exactly* what the structure needs,” Jhace snapped. “Nothing more.”
[L531] 
[L532] At one‑fifty, the last workers stumbled out. Two hundred‑plus bodies, alive because they’d been allowed time.
[L533] 
[L534] “Terminate,” Tiffani said.
[L535] 
[L536] Jhace released first. The column held under wedges now that the load had shifted down.
[L537] 
[L538] Corven released a heartbeat later and nearly fell.
[L539] 
[L540] Jhace caught him by the elbow, and the tremor in his own hands made the grip feel unreliable.
[L541] 
[L542] Corven looked at his shaking fingers with a kind of horror. “It’s not stopping.”
[L543] 
[L544] “It doesn’t,” Jhace said.
[L545] 
[L546] Tiffani wrote the number anyway: **219 evacuated. Zero casualties. Two operators marked.**
[L547] 
[L548] Bram approached, face hard. “Crown’s going to hear about this—and they’ll bring forms instead of shovels.”
[L549] 
[L550] Tiffani didn’t look up. “Good.”
[L551] 
[L552] Jhace’s chest still burned with the after‑heat of holding. The hunger whispered: *You could have held longer. You could have made it perfect.*
[L553] 
[L554] He swallowed copper and told the hunger to shut up.
[L555] 
[L556] For now.
[L557] 
[L558] ---

[L560]

[L563] ### ROUTE FAILURE (THREE DEAD)
[L564] 
[L565] The bell came from Sector Nine, not as a roar—just a single strike repeated until someone answered.
[L566] 
[L567] Residential stairwell. Upper-terrace split. Twelve families still inside, one route.
[L568] 
[L569] Jhace was already marked from the warehouse. Corven was worse. Senne was fresh enough to stand without shaking.
[L570] 
[L571] Tiffani looked at the slate wall and made the decision she would later pretend was only math.
[L572] 
[L573] Two operators at one‑eighty each meant coordination, handoffs, spotters, and failure points. One operator at two‑forty meant simplicity. A clean line.
[L574] 
[L575] “Single deployment,” she said. “Senne. Duration cap: two‑forty. Terminate on my bell.”
[L576] 
[L577] Senne went pale as the heat took her chest. Copper-gold light laced her forearms. Not pretty. Functional.
[L578] 
[L579] They reached the stairwell mouth as stone dust sifted from the ceiling like flour. The grid-hum underfoot carried the doubled harmonic, deep and insistent, a warning that didn’t care who listened.
[L580] 
[L581] Senne braced under the central landing. Jhace watched her shoulders set—metalworker’s posture in a glassworker’s frame.
[L582] 
[L583] Tiffani counted. Heartbeats, not seconds. Seconds lied when panic touched them.
[L584] 
[L585] One hundred.
[L586] 
[L587] One forty.
[L588] 
[L589] One eighty.
[L590] 
[L591] At one‑ninety, a secondary beam—one Tiffani hadn’t walked because she’d trusted the map—shifted with a sound like teeth grinding.
[L592] 
[L593] The evacuation lane kinked. The next family stumbled. The stone didn’t collapse all at once. It folded, patient and specific, closing the corridor into a narrow pocket.
[L594] 
[L595] Three people were on the wrong side when it did: two adults and a child coughing copper into his sleeve.
[L596] 
[L597] Tiffani saw them, and for a moment the slate in her hands felt obscene.
[L598] 
[L599] “Hold,” she said, voice flat. “Keep holding.”
[L600] 
[L601] Senne’s eyes didn’t move. She couldn’t spare the motion.
[L602] 
[L603] Two‑ten.
[L604] 
[L605] Two‑twenty.
[L606] 
[L607] Two‑thirty.
[L608] 
[L609] The pocket didn’t clear. The child’s coughing turned wet.
[L610] 
[L611] At two‑forty, Tiffani rang the termination sequence anyway. Because protocols existed for a reason. Because if she didn’t, she would never stop.
[L612] 
[L613] Senne released.
[L614] 
[L615] The stairwell collapsed into itself with a slow, final sound—stone settling into the shape it had wanted all along.
[L616] 
[L617] They dug for hours.
[L618] 
[L619] They found three bodies. The child first, because he was small enough to slip into the wrong angles.
[L620] 
[L621] Back in the workshop, Tiffani logged it with perfect precision.
[L622] 
[L623] **Deployment:** Senne — 240 heartbeats  
[L624] **Evacuated:** 47  
[L625] **Casualties:** 3  
[L626] **Cause:** Inadequate route assessment. Secondary beam shear.
[L627] 
[L628] She wrote “cause” as if it made the number less personal.
[L629] 
[L630] Later, alone, she re-read the line **Casualties: 3** until the ink blurred.
[L631] 
[L632] She didn’t cry. She didn’t speak. She just stared at the digit like it might change if she stared hard enough.
[L633] 
[L634] The next Tide, she rewrote the protocol.
[L635] 
[L636] **Route spotters required. Three per deployment. No exceptions.**
[L637] 
[L638] She underlined it twice.
[L639] 
[L640] The number stayed.
[L641]
