[L217] ## CHAPTER 2: THE PATTERN
[L218] 
[L219] The theodolite wouldn't settle.
[L220] 
[L221] Tiffani had calibrated it three times in the last hour. Clean lens. Level base. Reference stars locked. Everything aligned the way it had been for twenty-six Turns of lens work.
[L222] 
[L223] The crosshair still drifted.
[L224] 
[L225] Not atmospheric shimmer. Not lens warp. A displacement that arrived in the instrument itself—like the glass was listening to something she couldn't hear.
[L226] 
[L227] She pressed her palm to the primary lens frame.
[L228] 
[L229] Vibration.
[L230] 
[L231] Faint. Constant. A doubled harmonic riding underneath the baseline hum she'd felt in stone her whole life. The frequency was wrong. Too low. Too persistent. It made her back teeth ache.
[L232] 
[L233] She pulled her hand away and wrote on her slate:
[L234] 
[L235] **Tide 0 — Kindling**  
[L236] **Lens frame vibration: doubled harmonic present.**  
[L237] **Star Seven-Three displacement: 0.34 degrees west.**  
[L238] **Not atmospheric. Not mechanical.**
[L239] 
[L240] Her mouth tasted copper.
[L241] 
[L242] She'd been ignoring it for the last two Tides. Faint metallic tang that came and went with air currents from the ventilation shafts. But this was different. This arrived sharp and immediate, flooding her tongue like she'd been chewing wire.
[L243] 
[L244] The light outside the lens port changed.
[L245] 
[L246] Not dimmed. Not brightened. *Bent*.
[L247] 
[L248] Tiffani looked up.
[L249] 
[L250] The sky over Saint Merro wasn't supposed to do anything except hold weather and stars. Today it held something else.
[L251] 
[L252] A line. Straight. Dark. Moving too fast for her eyes to track at first, then slowing enough for her to understand what she was seeing.
[L253] 
[L254] A spindle.
[L255] 
[L256] Long and narrow and *wrong*, like someone had driven a forged bar through the world and walked away.
[L257] 
[L258] Her breath stopped.
[L259] 
[L260] The spindle crossed her view in a single heartbeat. No friction fire. No sound. Just a displacement of light that made the air look thin.
[L261] 
[L262] Then gravity lurched.
[L263] 
[L264] Not a shift sideways. An up-and-down stagger, like the planet had been grabbed and shaken once. Her slate slid across the stone desk. A lens clamp snapped. Glass sang a sharp, stressed note and went silent.
[L265] 
[L266] Tiffani caught herself on the observation frame and forced her eyes back to the port.
[L267] 
[L268] The spindle was gone.
[L269] 
[L270] Punched through. Somewhere below. Into stone or ocean or the Underface—she couldn't tell.
[L271] 
[L272] Pressure arrived a breath later. Not a blast. More like the city inhaling and forgetting how to exhale. Dust fell from the ceiling mortar in a slow, dry drift.
[L273] 
[L274] Then copper-gold particulate began falling outside.
[L275] 
[L276] Glittering. Fine. Catching the lamplight like ground glass in a furnace beam. It coated the stone lip of the observation port and clung to the iron frame in flecks that shimmered when she moved.
[L277] 
[L278] Tiffani touched it with her fingertip.
[L279] 
[L280] It didn't smear like ash. It gritted. Abrasive. Her finger tingled where the dust touched skin.
[L281] 
[L282] She wrote:
[L283] 
[L284] **Atmospheric intrusion event. Spindle-form object. Estimated velocity: extreme.**  
[L285] **Copper-gold particulate fall: immediate post-impact.**  
[L286] **Adheres to metal preferentially. Abrasive. Conductive?**
[L287] 
[L288] A bell rang.
[L289] 
[L290] Not the Glass-House alert. Distant. Down-terrace. The frantic peal of something already falling.
[L291] 
[L292] Then another bell. Different direction. Same cadence.
[L293] 
[L294] Emergency. Emergency. Emergency.
[L295] 
[L296] Tiffani's stomach tightened.
[L297] 
[L298] She grabbed her slate, shoved it into her coat pocket, and ran.
[L299] 
[L300] The doubled harmonic followed her down the Glass-House stairs. She could feel it in the stone railing—vibration that hadn't been there this morning. The walls themselves were listening to something new.
[L301] 
[L302] At the mid-terrace landing, she stopped.
[L303] 
[L304] Tallow Street was visible from here: upper facade collapsed inward, dust cloud thick enough to choke on. People running. Voices screaming coordinates she couldn't parse.
[L305] 
[L306] A third bell rang. Sector Nine. Grain district.
[L307] 
[L308] Closer.
[L309] 
[L310] Tiffani made her choice the way she always did: follow the data to its source.
[L311] 
[L312] She ran toward Sector Nine.
[L313] 
[L314] The copper taste got stronger with every stride.
[L315] 
[L316] ---
[L317] ## CHAPTER 3: SECOND HOLD
[L318] 
[L319] The grain warehouse sat in Sector Nine, where the terraces were newer and the beams were honest enough to still pretend they could be trusted.
[L320] 
[L321] The collapse wasn't honest.
[L322] 
[L323] Support columns had sheared at the base—clean breaks that didn't match load failure. The upper floor sagged like a shelf someone had stepped on. Dust poured from the breach in thick, choking waves.
[L324] 
[L325] Jhace stood three strides from the entrance, hands shaking so hard he had to grip his belt to keep them still.
[L326] 
[L327] He'd been here five minutes.
[L328] 
[L329] The tremor from Tallow Street hadn't stopped. It had spread from his hands into his forearms, up to his shoulders. A fine, relentless vibration that made every tool on his belt rattle.
[L330] 
[L331] The heat in his chest was still there. Banked. Waiting.
[L332] 
[L333] A foreman ran past him toward the breach, saw his Crown inspector marks, and grabbed his shoulder hard enough to bruise.
[L334] 
[L335] "You held Tallow," the foreman said. "Do it again."
[L336] 
[L337] Jhace pulled free. "I don't—"
[L338] 
[L339] "There's forty people in there," the foreman cut in. "Maybe more. Wedges won't hold. Straps won't reach. You're what we've got."
[L340] 
[L341] The copper taste flooded Jhace's mouth again.
[L342] 
[L343] His chest heat flared.
[L344] 
[L345] The doubled harmonic he'd felt in the grid-hum arrived inside him—two mis-timed pulses locking into the same rhythm. His heartbeat and the failing building's stress pattern aligned like they were the same system.
[L346] 
[L347] He didn't want this.
[L348] 
[L349] He didn't understand this.
[L350] 
[L351] But the city was asking, and his body was already answering.
[L352] 
[L353] "Clear the approach," Jhace said, voice rough. "Get spotters. I need someone counting."
[L354] 
[L355] He stepped under the sagging upper floor.
[L356] 
[L357] The heat detonated behind his sternum.
[L358] 
[L359] He put both hands on the nearest support column. Filament-light threaded from his palms into the stone—brighter this time, thicker, like the copper-gold dust in the air was feeding it.
[L360] 
[L361] The warehouse stopped settling.
[L362] 
[L363] The groan of stressed iron went quiet.
[L364] 
[L365] Jhace's breath shook. He could feel the load distribution through his fingertips: uneven, shifting, trying to collapse in three directions at once. His hands tightened. The filament brightened in response.
[L366] 
[L367] The tremor in his arms worsened.
[L368] 
[L369] Not fine anymore. Visible. His whole body shaking with the effort of holding something that should have already fallen.
[L370] 
[L371] "Move!" someone shouted. "Get them out!"
[L372] 
[L373] Boots ran. Voices called coordinates. Bodies crawled through gaps in the rubble.
[L374] 
[L375] A woman appeared beside him—practical coat, slate already open, hair tied back with a strip of cloth.
[L376] 
[L377] She looked at the filament-light threading from his hands. Looked at his face. Looked at the tremor shaking his shoulders.
[L378] 
[L379] "You're the Tallow Street hold," she said.
[L380] 
[L381] Not a question. A confirmation.
[L382] 
[L383] "Yeah," Jhace managed.
[L384] 
[L385] "How long have you been holding?"
[L386] 
[L387] "I don't know. Five minutes? Ten?"
[L388] 
[L389] Her pen scratched. "Tremor amplitude increased since first manifestation. Cost progression visible."
[L390] 
[L391] "What?" Jhace's vision narrowed at the edges. Copper-gold sparks flickered across it.
[L392] 
[L393] "Your body is paying for this," she said, still writing. "In increments. If you don't terminate before the cost exceeds tolerance, you won't be able to let go."
[L394] 
[L395] "I can't let go," Jhace said through clenched teeth. "Not until they're out."
[L396] 
[L397] The woman looked at the evacuation line. Counted bodies being carried past. "Twenty-three so far. Estimate forty total inside."
[L398] 
[L399] "Then I hold for forty."
[L400] 
[L401] "Then you might not walk away."
[L402] 
[L403] Jhace's hands shook harder. The heat in his chest flared so bright it hurt. "I already know that."
[L404] 
[L405] The woman's jaw tightened. She moved to where he could see her without turning his head and held up her slate like a mirror.
[L406] 
[L407] "I'm documenting this," she said. "Duration. Cost markers. Termination threshold. If we don't measure it, the next person who tries this dies without knowing why."
[L408] 
[L409] "What's your name?" Jhace asked.
[L410] 
[L411] "Tiffani Merrow. Glass-House observatory."
[L412] 
[L413] "Jhace Torrins."
[L414] 
[L415] "I know," Tiffani said. "I wrote it down at Tallow Street."
[L416] 
[L417] She showed him her slate:
[L418] 
[L419] **Jhace Torrins. Inspector. First manifestation. Duration: 240 heartbeats. Cost: tremor onset, progressive.**
[L420] 
[L421] **Second manifestation. Sector Nine warehouse. Onset: immediate. Tremor amplitude: increased. Copper-gold filament visible, enhanced density.**
[L422] 
[L423] Jhace stared at the notes. At his own name written like data.
[L424] 
[L425] "What caused this?" he asked.
[L426] 
[L427] Tiffani's pen paused. "Something punched through the sky two hours ago. Spindle. Metallic. Fast enough to ignore friction. Left this—"
[L428] 
[L429] She held up her fingertip. Copper-gold grit caught the lamplight.
[L430] 
[L431] "—everywhere."
[L432] 
[L433] Jhace looked at the dust swirling around his boots. At the filament threading from his hands. At the way the copper-gold particles moved toward him like they recognized him.
[L434] 
[L435] "The doubled harmonic," he said.
[L436] 
[L437] "You felt it too?" Tiffani's eyes sharpened.
[L438] 
[L439] "In the grid-hum. Two Tides before this. Wrong frequency. Made my teeth itch."
[L440] 
[L441] Tiffani wrote: **Doubled harmonic: confirmed across multiple observers. Grid infrastructure + atmospheric intrusion event = correlation.**
[L442] 
[L443] A rescue crew leader shouted. "Thirty-eight out. Two still inside. Back corner. Pinned."
[L444] 
[L445] Jhace's vision blurred. The heat in his chest spiked.
[L446] 
[L447] "How long have I been holding?" he rasped.
[L448] 
[L449] Tiffani checked a small timepiece on her belt. "Eighteen minutes."
[L450] 
[L451] "Tallow Street was four."
[L452] 
[L453] "Your cost is accelerating," Tiffani said. "Tremor spread. Vision impairment onset. Nosebleed—"
[L454] 
[L455] Jhace tasted blood before she finished the sentence. Copper-warm, running over his lip.
[L456] 
[L457] "Terminate after extraction," Tiffani said. "Not before. But the instant they're clear, you let go."
[L458] 
[L459] "What if I can't?"
[L460] 
[L461] "Then I make you."
[L462] 
[L463] The last two bodies came out on stretchers. Alive. Barely.
[L464] 
[L465] "Clear!" the crew leader yelled.
[L466] 
[L467] Tiffani's hand closed on Jhace's shoulder—hard enough to hurt, grounding enough to feel real.
[L468] 
[L469] "Terminate," she said. "Now."
[L470] 
[L471] Jhace let the filament go.
[L472] 
[L473] The warehouse settled the last inch it had been denied. Stone grinding. Iron squealing. A beam snapped somewhere in the back, but the void where people had been was empty.
[L474] 
[L475] Jhace staggered back.
[L476] 
[L477] His legs didn't hold. He hit the ground on his knees, hands still shaking, blood dripping from his nose onto the dust-covered stone.
[L478] 
[L479] Tiffani crouched beside him, slate open, pen moving.
[L480] 
[L481] **Second manifestation terminated. Duration: 19 minutes, 40 seconds. Cost: tremor progression (shoulders, torso), vision impairment (peripheral narrowing, copper-gold visual artifacts), epistaxis (nosebleed), motor control compromise (collapse post-termination).**
[L482] 
[L483] **Survival: confirmed. Function: degraded.**
[L484] 
[L485] Jhace wiped blood from his mouth with a shaking hand. "You write everything like it's going to behave."
[L486] 
[L487] "Everything does behave," Tiffani said. "Even this. It has markers. Progression. Termination criteria."
[L488] 
[L489] "It's killing me in increments."
[L490] 
[L491] "Yes," Tiffani said. "Which means we can measure the increments. Track them. Find the threshold before it crosses fatal."
[L492] 
[L493] Jhace looked at her slate. At the notes written in clean, precise handwriting. At his own degradation catalogued like data.
[L494] 
[L495] "Why are you doing this?" he asked.
[L496] 
[L497] Tiffani met his eyes. Hers were sharp, clear, absolutely certain.
[L498] 
[L499] "Because the city is breaking faster than crews can fix it," she said. "Because people like you are going to keep holding until they die. Because if we don't measure the cost, the next bell will kill someone without them knowing why."
[L500] 
[L501] A bell rang in the distance.
[L502] 
[L503] Fourth collapse. Different sector.
[L504] 
[L505] The copper taste surged in Jhace's mouth.
[L506] 
[L507] The heat in his chest flared.
[L508] 
[L509] Tiffani wrote: **Third manifestation event probable. Subject response: autonomic. Cost accumulation: ongoing.**
[L510] 
[L511] She looked at Jhace. "Can you stand?"
[L512] 
[L513] "I don't know."
[L514] 
[L515] "Try."
[L516] 
[L517] Jhace pushed himself to his feet. His legs shook. His hands wouldn't stop trembling. But he stood.
[L518] 
[L519] "Good," Tiffani said. "Because we're going to the next one. And I'm counting every heartbeat."
[L520] 
[L521] She walked toward the sound of the bell.
[L522] 
[L523] Jhace followed.
[L524] 
[L525] The city kept falling.
[L526] 
[L527] And they kept measuring.
[L528] 
[L529] ---
[L530] ## CHAPTER 4: PROTOCOL
[L531] Jhace’s workshop was a box of iron and heat tucked into the mid‑terrace, where you could hear the grid‑hum without being crushed by it.
[L532] 
[L533] Tiffani sat on an overturned crate with her slate on her knees, left leg extended slightly to ease the knee that had started locking after long stands on stone floors. She didn’t rest her back against anything. Always ready to move. Always ready to write.
[L534] Tiffani shifted her weight off her left leg without comment, like it was just another measurement she refused to log.
[L535] 
[L536] Jhace stood at his bench, trying to thread a bolt into a collar bracket.
[L537] 
[L538] His hands shook.
[L539] 
[L540] It wasn’t violent. It wasn’t obvious to someone who didn’t live inside his fingers. But the bolt kept kissing the threads wrong, slipping, catching, refusing to start.
[L541] 
[L542] He tried again. The bolt dropped. Metal on stone.
[L543] 
[L544] He stared at it like it had betrayed him.
[L545] 
[L546] Tiffani didn’t look up. “Your tremor amplitude increased during exertion.”
[L547] 
[L548] “Because I’m exerting,” Jhace muttered, bending to pick up the bolt. His fingertips felt numb where the filament‑light had burned them. Not pain. Absence.
[L549] 
[L550] “Because your body is remembering,” Tiffani corrected. “It thinks you’re still holding.”
[L551] 
[L552] Jhace set the bolt down and flexed his hand hard. The tremor didn’t care.
[L553] 
[L554] He hated that. He hated that his own muscles had become unreliable.
[L555] 
[L556] “What did you call it out there?” he asked.
[L557] 
[L558] Tiffani flipped her slate so he could see. She’d drawn a crude timeline.
[L559] 
[L560] **Activation onset: chest heat + copper taste**  
[L561] **Light manifestation: filament contact‑dependent**  
[L562] **Tremor: post‑termination, permanent marker**
[L563] 
[L564] “I need a taxonomy,” she said. “Stages. Because right now it’s just stories.”
[L565] 
[L566] Jhace wiped his palms on a work cloth. “And you think writing stages keeps people alive.”
[L567] 
[L568] “I think it keeps them from lying to themselves.” She tapped the line where she’d written *permanent*. “You can’t call it temporary. You can’t bargain with it.”
[L569] 
[L570] Jhace’s jaw worked.
[L571] 
[L572] He had bargained with Junction Seven. He had told himself the report would matter. That somebody else would fix it. That the city didn’t need him to fight every foreman with a schedule board.
[L573] 
[L574] He looked at his shaking hands. “Stage One,” he said, voice low. “Tremor.”
[L575] 
[L576] Tiffani nodded and wrote: **Stage One: tremor + filament residue. Functional. Marked.**
[L577] 
[L578] A knock hit the workshop door.
[L579] 
[L580] Bram Tallow leaned in like he was checking whether the room was still allowed to exist. Dust on his boots, sweat at his collar, grin already loaded.
[L581] 
[L582] “Heard you two went and grabbed Tallow Street by the throat,” Bram said. “Real romantic. The city tries to crush you, you crush back. Very intimate.”
[L583] 
[L584] Jhace didn’t look up. “Who’s asking?”
[L585] 
[L586] “Crown’s asking questions,” Bram said, and somehow still made it sound like a dirty joke. “Not because they care. Because they hate unknown tools walking around without a leash and a receipt.”
[L587] 
[L588] Tiffani’s pen paused. “What questions.”
[L589] 
[L590] “Names. Locations. ‘Verified capability.’ That sort of foreplay,” Bram said. He stepped fully inside, then stopped like the heat-grid might charge him rent. “Infrastructure office. Some administrator with hands so clean it makes you wonder what he’s washing off. Loves forms. Loves categories. Loves pretending paper is stronger than stone.”
[L591] 
[L592] Jhace’s jaw tightened. “To do what.”
[L593] 
[L594] Bram spread his hands. “To decide who gets to own you. Temple will call it divine. Guild will call it labor. Crown will call it law. Everybody wants a piece. Congratulations—you’re popular.”
[L595] 
[L596] “We don’t belong to any of them,” Tiffani said.
[L597] 
[L598] Bram laughed, quick and wrong. “Adorable. Tell that to a stamp. Try explaining ‘we don’t belong’ while a ceiling is coming down and someone’s screaming for a hold. They’ll file you under ‘non-compliant’ and act like that’s a weather condition.”
[L599] 
[L600] Jhace’s tremor ticked in his fingers. “So you’re here to warn us.”
[L601] 
[L602] “I’m here to tell you the door is already being measured,” Bram said. “I can buy you a Tide or two. Maybe. After that, they’ll show up with paperwork and smiles and start deciding which parts of you are ‘public infrastructure.’”
[L603] 
[L604] Tiffani’s mouth went flat. “Then we build methodology fast.”
[L605] 
[L606] Bram pointed at her slate like it offended him personally. “Yeah. Before they build it for you. Because if the Crown writes your protocol, it’s going to read like a job description. And you won’t like the benefits package.”
[L607] 
[L608] Jhace breathed in, tasted copper‑gold dust that still clung to his clothes, and felt the city’s hum through the floorboards.
[L609] 
[L610] “Then we build it fast,” he said. “Before they decide to build it for us.”
[L611] 
[L612] ---
[L613] 
[L614] ## CHAPTER 5: FIRST NETWORK
[L615] The grain warehouse sat in Sector Nine, where the terraces were newer and the beams were honest enough to still pretend they could be trusted.
[L616] 
[L617] The bell signal came late. That was the first problem.
[L618] 
[L619] When Jhace and Tiffani arrived, the warehouse wasn’t collapsed—yet. The support columns were bowing, groaning under a load they shouldn’t have been carrying because somebody had stacked wet grain too high to meet a schedule.
[L620] 
[L621] A foreman screamed, “Hold it! Hold it now!”
[L622] 
[L623] Jhace moved toward the nearest column out of habit.
[L624] 
[L625] Tiffani caught his sleeve. “Not yet.”
[L626] 
[L627] Jhace’s jaw tightened. “People are inside.”
[L628] 
[L629] “People are *leaving*,” she corrected, pointing.
[L630] 
[L631] She was right. Workers were already pouring out of the side doors, coughing, eyes wide. The structure wasn’t in free fall. It was in warning.
[L632] 
[L633] “We wedge first,” Tiffani said. “Then you hold if wedges fail.”
[L634] 
[L635] Jhace’s tremor wanted to argue. His chest heat wanted to flare. The hunger for that sudden alignment—pipe hum into palm, world pausing—sat behind his ribs like an itch.
[L636] 
[L637] He didn’t scratch it.
[L638] 
[L639] He dropped to one knee and shoved wedge slips under the first bowing brace. Bram’s crew followed, hands fast, straps ready. For once, labor did what labor could do.
[L640] 
[L641] The warehouse groaned again. Dust spilled from a seam.
[L642] 
[L643] A young man stepped into the brace line, hands hovering like he didn’t know what to do with them. Corven. Jhace had seen him in the tunnels, quiet, competent.
[L644] 
[L645] Corven swallowed. “I… I can feel it,” he said. “The hum. It’s—”
[L646] 
[L647] “Louder,” Jhace finished.
[L648] 
[L649] Corven nodded hard. “Like it’s in my teeth.”
[L650] 
[L651] Tiffani stepped close, slate already up. “If it hits your chest, tell me. If you taste copper, tell me. Do not improvise.”
[L652] 
[L653] Corven’s eyes flicked to the bending column. “If it goes—”
[L654] 
[L655] “Then we act,” Jhace said. “But not before.”
[L656] 
[L657] The wedges held for two breaths.
[L658] 
[L659] Then the central column shifted.
[L660] 
[L661] Not collapse. A decisive, ugly move—stone settling, iron flexing. The wedges screamed as they compressed.
[L662] 
[L663] The workers inside were still running.
[L664] 
[L665] “Now,” Tiffani said.
[L666] 
[L667] Jhace put both hands on the column.
[L668] 
[L669] Heat flared in his chest—familiar now, terrifying in its familiarity—and filament‑light threaded into the grain‑dust coated seam.
[L670] 
[L671] The column stopped shifting.
[L672] 
[L673] Corven stared at Jhace’s hands like they were a language he’d almost learned.
[L674] 
[L675] “Your turn,” Jhace said through gritted teeth.
[L676] 
[L677] Corven stepped forward and touched the secondary brace.
[L678] 
[L679] His breath hitched. Copper taste. Chest heat. Then light—thinner than Jhace’s, but real—threaded into the brace line.
[L680] 
[L681] Corven’s hands started to shake immediately.
[L682] 
[L683] “Stage One onset during hold,” Tiffani murmured, writing. “Early marker.”
[L684] 
[L685] “Keep moving! If you stop, you become debris!” Bram shouted to the evac crew. “Count them out! Loud. Fast. Like the Crown’s listening!”
[L686] 
[L687] Jhace’s tremor surged in sympathy, like the grid‑hum had tied their nervous systems together. His arms burned. His vision tunneled at the edges.
[L688] 
[L689] “How long?” Tiffani asked.
[L690] 
[L691] “Eighty,” Jhace rasped, counting heartbeats without thinking.
[L692] 
[L693] Corven’s jaw clenched. “I can do—”
[L694] 
[L695] “Do *exactly* what the structure needs,” Jhace snapped. “Nothing more.”
[L696] 
[L697] At one‑fifty, the last workers stumbled out. Two hundred‑plus bodies, alive because they’d been allowed time.
[L698] 
[L699] “Terminate,” Tiffani said.
[L700] 
[L701] Jhace released first. The column held under wedges now that the load had shifted down.
[L702] 
[L703] Corven released a heartbeat later and nearly fell.
[L704] 
[L705] Jhace caught him by the elbow, and the tremor in his own hands made the grip feel unreliable.
[L706] 
[L707] Corven looked at his shaking fingers with a kind of horror. “It’s not stopping.”
[L708] 
[L709] “It doesn’t,” Jhace said.
[L710] 
[L711] Corven stared at his shaking fingers like they’d betrayed him mid-shift.
[L712] 
[L713] He’d watched Jhace hold. Had seen the tremor. Had understood it in theory.
[L714] 
[L715] Theory didn’t matter when your hand wouldn’t listen.
[L716] 
[L717] “How long?” he asked.
[L718] 
[L719] Jhace’s jaw tightened. “Until you stop manifesting.”
[L720] 
[L721] Corven’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. “So never.”
[L722] 
[L723] “Yeah.”
[L724] 
[L725] Corven set his palm on the edge of the bench. The tremor rattled bone against metal.
[L726] 
[L727] He pulled back like it burned. Flexed. Tried again. Harder.
[L728] 
[L729] It kept moving.
[L730] 
[L731] Tiffani wrote the number anyway: **219 evacuated. Zero casualties. Two operators marked.**
[L732] 
[L733] Bram approached, face hard. “Crown’s going to hear about this—and they’ll bring forms instead of shovels.”
[L734] 
[L735] Tiffani didn’t look up. “Good.”
[L736] 
[L737] Jhace’s chest still burned with the after‑heat of holding. The hunger whispered: *You could have held longer. You could have made it perfect.*
[L738] 
[L739] He swallowed copper and told the hunger to shut up.
[L740] 
[L741] For now.
[L742] 
[L743] ---
[L744] 
[L745] 
[L746] ---
[L747] 
[L748] ### ROUTE FAILURE (THREE DEAD)
[L749] 
[L750] The bell came from Sector Nine, not as a roar—just a single strike repeated until someone answered.
[L751] 
[L752] Residential stairwell. Upper-terrace split. Twelve families still inside, one route.
[L753] 
[L754] Jhace was already marked from the warehouse. Corven was worse. Senne was fresh enough to stand without shaking.
[L755] 
[L756] Tiffani looked at the slate wall and made the decision she would later pretend was only math.
[L757] 
[L758] Two operators at one‑eighty each meant coordination, handoffs, spotters, and failure points. One operator at two‑forty meant simplicity. A clean line.
[L759] 
[L760] “Single deployment,” she said. “Senne. Duration cap: two‑forty. Terminate on my bell.”
[L761] 
[L762] Senne went pale as the heat took her chest. Copper-gold light laced her forearms. Not pretty. Functional.
[L763] 
[L764] They reached the stairwell mouth as stone dust sifted from the ceiling like flour. The grid-hum underfoot carried the doubled harmonic, deep and insistent, a warning that didn’t care who listened.
[L765] 
[L766] Senne braced under the central landing. Jhace watched her shoulders set—metalworker’s posture in a glassworker’s frame.
[L767] 
[L768] Tiffani counted. Heartbeats, not seconds. Seconds lied when panic touched them.
[L769] 
[L770] One hundred.
[L771] 
[L772] One forty.
[L773] 
[L774] One eighty.
[L775] 
[L776] At one‑ninety, a secondary beam—one Tiffani hadn’t walked because she’d trusted the map—shifted with a sound like teeth grinding.
[L777] 
[L778] The evacuation lane kinked. The next family stumbled. The stone didn’t collapse all at once. It folded, patient and specific, closing the corridor into a narrow pocket.
[L779] 
[L780] Three people were on the wrong side when it did: two adults and a child coughing copper into his sleeve.
[L781] 
[L782] Tiffani saw them, and for a moment the slate in her hands felt obscene.
[L783] 
[L784] “Hold,” she said, voice flat. “Keep holding.”
[L785] 
[L786] Senne’s eyes didn’t move. She couldn’t spare the motion.
[L787] 
[L788] Two‑ten.
[L789] 
[L790] Two‑twenty.
[L791] 
[L792] Two‑thirty.
[L793] 
[L794] The pocket didn’t clear. The child’s coughing turned wet.
[L795] 
[L796] At two‑forty, Tiffani rang the termination sequence anyway. Because protocols existed for a reason. Because if she didn’t, she would never stop.
[L797] 
[L798] Senne released.
[L799] 
[L800] The stairwell collapsed into itself with a slow, final sound—stone settling into the shape it had wanted all along.
[L801] 
[L802] They dug for hours.
[L803] 
[L804] They found three bodies. The child first, because he was small enough to slip into the wrong angles.
[L805] 
[L806] Back in the workshop, Tiffani logged it with perfect precision.
[L807] 
[L808] **Deployment:** Senne — 240 heartbeats  
[L809] **Evacuated:** 47  
[L810] **Casualties:** 3  
[L811] **Cause:** Inadequate route assessment. Secondary beam shear.
[L812] 
[L813] She wrote “cause” as if precision could make it reversible.
[L814] 
[L815] Her hand moved to write the next revision. The chalk touched slate.
[L816] 
[L817] Nothing came out.
[L818] 
[L819] She stared at the number.
[L820] 
[L821] Three.
[L822] 
[L823] Not a count. Three names she would never know. Three families who would ask why their—
[L824] 
[L825] The chalk snapped in her grip.
[L826] 
[L827] She set the broken pieces down carefully. Picked up a new stick. Wrote:
[L828] 
[L829] **Route spotters required. Three per deployment. No exceptions.**
[L830] 
[L831] Underlined it twice.
[L832] 
[L833] Then she closed the slate and sat in the dark workshop for six hundred heartbeats, doing nothing but breathing and failing to justify the number.
[L834] 
[L835] 
[L836] 
[L837] ---
[L838] 
[L839] ## CHAPTER 6: WITNESS
[L840] Brother Corran didn’t wear Temple colors.
[L841] 
[L842] That was the first thing Jhace noticed when the man stepped into the workshop two Tides after the warehouse. No robes. No beads. Just a plain coat and a Crown badge pinned where it couldn’t be missed.
[L843] 
[L844] Corran’s eyes went straight to Jhace’s hands.
[L845] 
[L846] “For the record, I’m not here to stop you,” Corran said, before anyone asked.
[L847] 
[L848] Bram hovered by the door like a lock that wanted to be useful.
[L849] 
[L850] Tiffani set her slate down deliberately. “Then why are you here?”
[L851] 
[L852] “Because you’re real,” Corran said. “And because real things attract predators.”
[L853] 
[L854] Jhace’s mouth tightened. “Crown.”
[L855] 
[L856] Corran’s gaze didn’t flinch. “Yes. Also Guild. Also Temple. Anyone who thinks they can turn you into a lever.”
[L857] 
[L858] Bram’s voice came dry. “Ah yes. The holy trinity of getting fucked: prayer, production, paperwork.”
[L859] 
[L860] Tiffani shot him a look. “Bram.”
[L861] 
[L862] He raised his hands. “What? I’m helping.”
[L863] 
[L864] Bram gestured toward the door. “Temple says you’re divine. Guild says you’re labor. Crown says you’re property. Everybody’s trying to fuck the same lever.”
[L865] 
[L866] He pulled a folded packet from his coat and set it on Jhace’s bench. Paper. Old style. Archive weight.
[L867] 
[L868] “Stage Three cases,” Corran said. “Documented. Not rumors.”
[L869] 
[L870] Tiffani’s hands went still. “How many?”
[L871] 
[L872] “Enough.” Corran’s voice was flat, and that flatness carried exhaustion. “Most die. A few don’t. The ones who don’t… wish they had.”
[L873] 
[L874] Jhace’s tremor ticked up at the word *die*. He forced his hands flat on the bench. “Why give this to us?”
[L875] 
[L876] Corran hesitated. That hesitation was the crack in the Crown.
[L877] 
[L878] “Because someone above me wants a registry,” he said. “Names. Locations. Deployment authority. They’re calling it order. It’s control.”
[L879] 
[L880] Tiffani’s eyes narrowed. “Who?”
[L881] 
[L882] Corran looked toward the door as if the walls might report him. “Administrator Corlexi. Infrastructure Compliance.”
[L883] 
[L884] Bram gave a low whistle. “Clean hands. Clean coat. Probably wipes his ass with forms.”
[L885] 
[L886] Jhace stared at the packet. “So you’re here to warn us.”
[L887] 
[L888] “I’m here to give you data,” Corran corrected. “If you want to fight control, you need numbers. Not speeches.”
[L889] 
[L890] Tiffani opened the packet and read, eyes moving fast.
[L891] 
[L892] Jhace watched her face tighten at certain lines—durations, costs, outcomes. She didn’t gasp. She didn’t dramatize. She just absorbed.
[L893] 
[L894] “This case,” she said, tapping a page. “Stage Three survival. How?”
[L895] 
[L896] Corran’s mouth went thin. “He didn’t terminate. He couldn’t. The hold locked. Autonomic failure started and the manifestation kept running anyway. The structure held until it didn’t matter.”
[L897] 
[L898] Jhace felt cold crawl up his spine despite the workshop heat. “Locked hold.”
[L899] 
[L900] Corran nodded. “It’s not a tool you put down when you’re done. Sometimes it’s a hook in your chest that drags you until something breaks.”
[L901] 
[L902] Tiffani’s pen scratched. **Stage Three: autonomic failure. Locked hold possible. Termination may fail.**
[L903] 
[L904] Bram shifted. “You said predators.”
[L905] 
[L906] Corran’s eyes met Bram’s. “Tallec is filing complaints. Says you’re obstructing industry. Says your protocols slow production.”
[L907] 
[L908] Jhace’s jaw clenched so hard it hurt.
[L909] 
[L910] Corran leaned closer, voice low. “Here’s the fracture you wanted. I don’t like Corlex

[L1] # N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS
[L2] *Turning: GLASS (G) — Tide 0 through Tide 21 (Three Anneals post-Piercing)*
[L3] 
[L4] ---
[L5] 
[L6] ## CHAPTER 1: THE BELL
[L7] 
[L8] The bell rang wrong.
[L9] 
[L10] Not the slow warning cadence. Not the measured strike that meant "check your seals, tighten your brackets." This was the frantic peal of something already falling, the sound of a structure begging for a hold.
[L11] 
[L12] Jhace was moving before the bell finished its third strike.
[L13] 
[L14] His kit bounced against his hip. Chalk. Slate. Collar keys. Tools for measuring failure, not stopping it. He didn't know why his legs were carrying him toward the sound instead of away. Didn't know why his heart counted the bell strikes like they were orders.
[L15] 
[L16] One. Two. Three. Four.
[L17] 
[L18] The doubled harmonic hit him at Junction Twelve.
[L19] 
[L20] Not through the floor. Not vibrating the pipes. Arriving in his chest like his body had become a tuning fork for something broken in the city's bones. His teeth ached with it. The back of his tongue went metallic.
[L21] 
[L22] Copper.
[L23] 
[L24] Sharp and immediate, like he'd bitten through wire.
[L25] 
[L26] He'd been tasting it for two Tides now. Faint at first. A ghost-flavor that came and went with the grid-hum. But this was different. This flooded his mouth so thick he wanted to spit.
[L27] 
[L28] He didn't.
[L29] 
[L30] Underface rule: waste nothing.
[L31] 
[L32] His hands started shaking.
[L33] 
[L34] Not from cold. Not from exertion. A fine vibration that made his grip feel unreliable, like his muscles were obeying and not obeying at the same time.
[L35] 
[L36] He jammed them into his coat pockets and kept running.
[L37] 
[L38] He forced his breath to slow. Counted to ten without looking at the numbers.
[L39] 
[L40] The tremor kept going.
[L41] 
[L42] He pulled one hand out. Squeezed it into a fist until the knuckles burned. Released.
[L43] 
[L44] Still shaking.
[L45] 
[L46] Not the aftershock of exertion. Not cold. A new baseline that didn’t care what he wanted.
[L47] 
[L48] He’d seen it on old manifestors in the deep tunnels. Hands that never found stillness. He’d called it age.
[L49] 
[L50] Now he understood.
[L51] 
[L52] Tallow Street came into view through the access tunnel: upper terrace facade folded inward, stone slabs riding down on support beams that should have held twice that load. Dust cloud thick enough to choke on. People screaming from inside the collapse. Someone rang a hand-bell hard enough to crack its cadence.
[L53] 
[L54] Emergency. Emergency. Emergency.
[L55] 
[L56] A foreman waved at him, face streaked with ash. "We need a hold. We don't have time."
[L57] 
[L58] Jhace looked at the structure.
[L59] 
[L60] The failure line wasn't clean. The whole building was trying to become rubble, one breath at a time. Load shifts rippled through it like a living animal struggling to die. Stone groaned. Iron squealed.
[L61] 
[L62] No crew could wedge that fast. No straps would catch enough.
[L63] 
[L64] The people inside didn't have minutes.
[L65] 
[L66] "I'm not—" Jhace started.
[L67] 
[L68] The heat arrived.
[L69] 
[L70] Not external. Internal. A sudden furnace-flare behind his sternum that made him gasp. Like someone had lit a coal bed under his ribs and told it to burn clean.
[L71] 
[L72] Copper-gold dust swirled around his boots.
[L73] 
[L74] He watched it climb his legs like static. Like it was looking for him.
[L75] 
[L76] His chest burned hotter.
[L77] 
[L78] The doubled harmonic snapped into alignment inside him—two mis-timed gears finally meshing. The wrongness in the grid-hum became rightness in his blood. His heartbeat and the city's failing pulse locked into the same rhythm.
[L79] 
[L80] He didn't know what he was doing.
[L81] 
[L82] He didn't know why his feet carried him under the broken lintel.
[L83] 
[L84] But the city was asking, and his body was already answering.
[L85] 
[L86] He put both hands on the main support column.
[L87] 
[L88] Light—thin, copper-gold, filament-fine—threaded from his palms into the stone seam.
[L89] 
[L90] The building stopped moving.
[L91] 
[L92] Not fixed. Not safe. Just *paused*.
[L93] 
[L94] Jhace's breath shook out of him. He could feel every shift in load through his fingertips: the weight of stone, the tension in iron, the panic-motion of bodies inside. The heat in his chest flared with each micro-adjustment, like his body was paying for every heartbeat of stability in increments he couldn't count.
[L95] 
[L96] "Move," he rasped. "Get them out."
[L97] 
[L98] Boots ran. Voices shouted coordinates. Someone coughed dust and kept crawling.
[L99] 
[L100] A woman slid into his peripheral vision—practical coat, hair tied back, slate already in hand despite the chaos.
[L101] 
[L102] She didn't look at him like a miracle.
[L103] 
[L104] She looked at him like a system that could fail.
[L105] 
[L106] "How long can you hold?" she demanded.
[L107] 
[L108] Jhace's jaw clenched. The heat in his chest spiked. "I don't know."
[L109] 
[L110] "Then we find out." She stepped into the dust, coughed once, and shouted to the evac crew. "Clear corridor A first. Count bodies out. No guessing."
[L111] 
[L112] Jhace tried to turn his head. The moment he shifted his attention, the heat flared sharper—punishment for looking away.
[L113] 
[L114] "I have to watch the load," he said through his teeth.
[L115] 
[L116] "Then watch it," she snapped, already marking a line on her slate. "I'll watch you."
[L117] 
[L118] People came out in bursts: three, then five, then a cluster carrying an older man whose leg bent wrong. Each time the load shifted, Jhace's hands tightened and the filament-light brightened in response.
[L119] 
[L120] His heartbeat counted itself in his ears.
[L121] 
[L122] One hundred.
[L123] 
[L124] One hundred twenty.
[L125] 
[L126] His arms started to tremble.
[L127] 
[L128] Not from fatigue. From something deeper. A fine vibration that spread into his forearms, up to his shoulders, making his grip feel like it belonged to someone else.
[L129] 
[L130] "Hold steady," the woman said, quieter now, close enough he could hear her through the dust.
[L131] 
[L132] He didn't answer. Answering took breath.
[L133] 
[L134] At two hundred heartbeats, the tremor spread into his chest. At two-twenty, his vision narrowed at the edges, copper-gold sparks flickering across it like welding flash.
[L135] 
[L136] "How many?" he rasped.
[L137] 
[L138] The woman didn't look up from the evac line. "Ten. Eleven. Twelve—"
[L139] 
[L140] A scream from inside. A child. High and thin and suddenly cut off.
[L141] 
[L142] Jhace's hands shook harder.
[L143] 
[L144] "No," he hissed, and the filament-light surged, burning his palms numb.
[L145] 
[L146] "Go," the woman shouted toward the breach. "Last sweep. Now."
[L147] 
[L148] A rescuer crawled under the paused beam line and dragged a small body out by the shoulders. The child coughed dust and cried like she'd been holding it in for hours.
[L149] 
[L150] The woman's voice cut clean through the chaos. "Twelve out. Corridor clear."
[L151] 
[L152] Jhace felt the building try to move again the instant she said it, like it had been waiting for permission.
[L153] 
[L154] "Terminate," she said. No softness in it. Not cruelty. Protocol.
[L155] 
[L156] Jhace let the filament go.
[L157] 
[L158] The building settled the last inch it had been denied. Stone grinding. Iron squealing. But the void where bodies had been was now empty.
[L159] 
[L160] He staggered back into open air.
[L161] 
[L162] His hands wouldn't stop shaking.
[L163] 
[L164] The tremor was small—millimeters—but it was relentless. Like a ghost living inside his muscles, counting the dead he'd prevented and refusing to be quiet about the cost.
[L165] 
[L166] The woman stepped beside him, dust streaked across her face. "Name."
[L167] 
[L168] "Jhace Torrins."
[L169] 
[L170] She wrote it without looking. "Duration: approximately two-forty heartbeats. Cost marker: tremor onset at—"
[L171] 
[L172] "Don't," he snapped. Too hard. Too fast.
[L173] 
[L174] She met his eyes. Her gaze was flat, measuring, absolutely certain.
[L175] 
[L176] "If we don't measure it," she said, "we kill you next time."
[L177] 
[L178] Between bells, they made it back to the workshop long enough to rinse dust off skin and reset slates.
[L179] 
[L180] Jhace sat at his bench and waited for the chest heat to fade.
[L181] 
[L182] It didn’t. Not fully.
[L183] 
[L184] A banked warmth stayed behind his sternum—steady as a coal bed that refused to go cold. He pressed his palm to his chest. Felt nothing through shirt and ribs.
[L185] 
[L186] Inside, the warmth answered anyway. Once. Twice.
[L187] 
[L188] Like the world remembering how it had fit together when he held.
[L189] 
[L190] His hand lingered there a heartbeat too long. Not thinking. Just reaching.
[L191] 
[L192] He pulled it away like it burned. The tremor kept moving, but for one heartbeat—only one—the shake eased, like his body preferred the burn to the break.
[L193] 
[L194] That was the first time the hunger felt like a vote.
[L195] 
[L196] Jhace’s hands shook between them like evidence he hadn't wanted.
[L197] 
[L198] He swallowed copper. Tasted grit. Looked at the child being carried past them on a makeshift stretcher—alive, crying, covered in dust but breathing.
[L199] 
[L200] He nodded once.
[L201] 
[L202] "Measure," he said. "Then make sure there isn't a next time."
[L203] 
[L204] The woman's slate scratched.
[L205] 
[L206] She wrote: **Jhace Torrins. Inspector. First manifestation. Tallow Street. Tide 0.**
[L207] 
[L208] Behind them, the bell rang again.
[L209] 
[L210] Different street. Different collapse.
[L211] 
[L212] The city kept falling.
[L213] 
[L214] And Jhace's body kept answering.
[L215] 
[L216] ---
[L217] ## CHAPTER 2: THE PATTERN
[L218] 
[L219] The theodolite wouldn't settle.
[L220] 
[L221] Tiffani had calibrated it three times in the last hour. Clean lens. Level base. Reference stars locked. Everything aligned the way it had been for twenty-six Turns of lens work.
[L222] 
[L223] The crosshair still drifted.
[L224] 
[L225] Not atmospheric shimmer. Not lens warp. A displacement that arrived in the instrument itself—like the glass was listening to something she couldn't hear.
[L226] 
[L227] She pressed her palm to the primary lens frame.
[L228] 
[L229] Vibration.
[L230] 
[L231] Faint. Constant. A doubled harmonic riding underneath the baseline hum she'd felt in stone her whole life. The frequency was wrong. Too low. Too persistent. It made her back teeth ache.
[L232] 
[L233] She pulled her hand away and wrote on her slate:
[L234] 
[L235] **Tide 0 — Kindling**  
[L236] **Lens frame vibration: doubled harmonic present.**  
[L237] **Star Seven-Three displacement: 0.34 degrees west.**  
[L238] **Not atmospheric. Not mechanical.**
[L239] 
[L240] Her mouth tasted copper.
[L241] 
[L242] She'd been ignoring it for the last two Tides. Faint metallic tang that came and went with air currents from the ventilation shafts. But this was different. This arrived sharp and immediate, flooding her tongue like she'd been chewing wire.
[L243] 
[L244] The light outside the lens port changed.
[L245] 
[L246] Not dimmed. Not brightened. *Bent*.
[L247] 
[L248] Tiffani looked up.
[L249] 
[L250] The sky over Saint Merro wasn't supposed to do anything except hold weather and stars. Today it held something else.
[L251] 
[L252] A line. Straight. Dark. Moving too fast for her eyes to track at first, then slowing enough for her to understand what she was seeing.
[L253] 
[L254] A spindle.
[L255] 
[L256] Long and narrow and *wrong*, like someone had driven a forged bar through the world and walked away.
[L257] 
[L258] Her breath stopped.
[L259] 
[L260] The spindle crossed her view in a single heartbeat. No friction fire. No sound. Just a displacement of light that made the air look thin.
[L261] 
[L262] Then gravity lurched.
[L263] 
[L264] Not a shift sideways. An up-and-down stagger, like the planet had been grabbed and shaken once. Her slate slid across the stone desk. A lens clamp snapped. Glass sang a sharp, stressed note and went silent.
[L265] 
[L266] Tiffani caught herself on the observation frame and forced her eyes back to the port.
[L267] 
[L268] The spindle was gone.
[L269] 
[L270] Punched through. Somewhere below. Into stone or ocean or the Underface—she couldn't tell.
[L271] 
[L272] Pressure arrived a breath later. Not a blast. More like the city inhaling and forgetting how to exhale. Dust fell from the ceiling mortar in a slow, dry drift.
[L273] 
[L274] Then copper-gold particulate began falling outside.
[L275] 
[L276] Glittering. Fine. Catching the lamplight like ground glass in a furnace beam. It coated the stone lip of the observation port and clung to the iron frame in flecks that shimmered when she moved.
[L277] 
[L278] Tiffani touched it with her fingertip.
[L279] 
[L280] It didn't smear like ash. It gritted. Abrasive. Her finger tingled where the dust touched skin.
[L281] 
[L282] She wrote:
[L283] 
[L284] **Atmospheric intrusion event. Spindle-form object. Estimated velocity: extreme.**  
[L285] **Copper-gold particulate fall: immediate post-impact.**  
[L286] **Adheres to metal preferentially. Abrasive. Conductive?**
[L287] 
[L288] A bell rang.
[L289] 
[L290] Not the Glass-House alert. Distant. Down-terrace. The frantic peal of something already falling.
[L291] 
[L292] Then another bell. Different direction. Same cadence.
[L293] 
[L294] Emergency. Emergency. Emergency.
[L295] 
[L296] Tiffani's stomach tightened.
[L297] 
[L298] She grabbed her slate, shoved it into her coat pocket, and ran.
[L299] 
[L300] The doubled harmonic followed her down the Glass-House stairs. She could feel it in the stone railing—vibration that hadn't been there this morning. The walls themselves were listening to something new.
[L301] 
[L302] At the mid-terrace landing, she stopped.
[L303] 
[L304] Tallow Street was visible from here: upper facade collapsed inward, dust cloud thick enough to choke on. People running. Voices screaming coordinates she couldn't parse.
[L305] 
[L306] A third bell rang. Sector Nine. Grain district.
[L307] 
[L308] Closer.
[L309] 
[L310] Tiffani made her choice the way she always did: follow the data to its source.
[L311] 
[L312] She ran toward Sector Nine.
[L313] 
[L314] The copper taste got stronger with every stride.
[L315] 
[L316] ---
[L317] ## CHAPTER 3: SECOND HOLD
[L318] 
[L319] The grain warehouse sat in Sector Nine, where the terraces were newer and the beams were honest enough to still pretend they could be trusted.
[L320] 
[L321] The collapse wasn't honest.
[L322] 
[L323] Support columns had sheared at the base—clean breaks that didn't match load failure. The upper floor sagged like a shelf someone had stepped on. Dust poured from the breach in thick, choking waves.
[L324] 
[L325] Jhace stood three strides from the entrance, hands shaking so hard he had to grip his belt to keep them still.
[L326] 
[L327] He'd been here five minutes.
[L328] 
[L329] The tremor from Tallow Street hadn't stopped. It had spread from his hands into his forearms, up to his shoulders. A fine, relentless vibration that made every tool on his belt rattle.
[L330] 
[L331] The heat in his chest was still there. Banked. Waiting.
[L332] 
[L333] A foreman ran past him toward the breach, saw his Crown inspector marks, and grabbed his shoulder hard enough to bruise.
[L334] 
[L335] "You held Tallow," the foreman said. "Do it again."
[L336] 
[L337] Jhace pulled free. "I don't—"
[L338] 
[L339] "There's forty people in there," the foreman cut in. "Maybe more. Wedges won't hold. Straps won't reach. You're what we've got."
[L340] 
[L341] The copper taste flooded Jhace's mouth again.
[L342] 
[L343] His chest heat flared.
[L344] 
[L345] The doubled harmonic he'd felt in the grid-hum arrived inside him—two mis-timed pulses locking into the same rhythm. His heartbeat and the failing building's stress pattern aligned like they were the same system.
[L346] 
[L347] He didn't want this.
[L348] 
[L349] He didn't understand this.
[L350] 
[L351] But the city was asking, and his body was already answering.
[L352] 
[L353] "Clear the approach," Jhace said, voice rough. "Get spotters. I need someone counting."
[L354] 
[L355] He stepped under the sagging upper floor.
[L356] 
[L357] The heat detonated behind his sternum.
[L358] 
[L359] He put both hands on the nearest support column. Filament-light threaded from his palms into the stone—brighter this time, thicker, like the copper-gold dust in the air was feeding it.
[L360] 
[L361] The warehouse stopped settling.
[L362] 
[L363] The groan of stressed iron went quiet.
[L364] 
[L365] Jhace's breath shook. He could feel the load distribution through his fingertips: uneven, shifting, trying to collapse in three directions at once. His hands tightened. The filament brightened in response.
[L366] 
[L367] The tremor in his arms worsened.
[L368] 
[L369] Not fine anymore. Visible. His whole body shaking with the effort of holding something that should have already fallen.
[L370] 
[L371] "Move!" someone shouted. "Get them out!"
[L372] 
[L373] Boots ran. Voices called coordinates. Bodies crawled through gaps in the rubble.
[L374] 
[L375] A woman appeared beside him—practical coat, slate already open, hair tied back with a strip of cloth.
[L376] 
[L377] She looked at the filament-light threading from his hands. Looked at his face. Looked at the tremor shaking his shoulders.
[L378] 
[L379] "You're the Tallow Street hold," she said.
[L380] 
[L381] Not a question. A confirmation.
[L382] 
[L383] "Yeah," Jhace managed.
[L384] 
[L385] "How long have you been holding?"
[L386] 
[L387] "I don't know. Five minutes? Ten?"
[L388] 
[L389] Her pen scratched. "Tremor amplitude increased since first manifestation. Cost progression visible."
[L390] 
[L391] "What?" Jhace's vision narrowed at the edges. Copper-gold sparks flickered across it.
[L392] 
[L393] "Your body is paying for this," she said, still writing. "In increments. If you don't terminate before the cost exceeds tolerance, you won't be able to let go."
[L394] 
[L395] "I can't let go," Jhace said through clenched teeth. "Not until they're out."
[L396] 
[L397] The woman looked at the evacuation line. Counted bodies being carried past. "Twenty-three so far. Estimate forty total inside."
[L398] 
[L399] "Then I hold for forty."
[L400] 
[L401] "Then you might not walk away."
[L402] 
[L403] Jhace's hands shook harder. The heat in his chest flared so bright it hurt. "I already know that."
[L404] 
[L405] The woman's jaw tightened. She moved to where he could see her without turning his head and held up her slate like a mirror.
[L406] 
[L407] "I'm documenting this," she said. "Duration. Cost markers. Termination threshold. If we don't measure it, the next person who tries this dies without knowing why."
[L408] 
[L409] "What's your name?" Jhace asked.
[L410] 
[L411] "Tiffani Merrow. Glass-House observatory."
[L412] 
[L413] "Jhace Torrins."
[L414] 
[L415] "I know," Tiffani said. "I wrote it down at Tallow Street."
[L416] 
[L417] She showed him her slate:
[L418] 
[L419] **Jhace Torrins. Inspector. First manifestation. Duration: 240 heartbeats. Cost: tremor onset, progressive.**
[L420] 
[L421] **Second manifestation. Sector Nine warehouse. Onset: immediate. Tremor amplitude: increased. Copper-gold filament visible, enhanced density.**
[L422] 
[L423] Jhace stared at the notes. At his own name written like data.
[L424] 
[L425] "What caused this?" he asked.
[L426] 
[L427] Tiffani's pen paused. "Something punched through the sky two hours ago. Spindle. Metallic. Fast enough to ignore friction. Left this—"
[L428] 
[L429] She held up her fingertip. Copper-gold grit caught the lamplight.
[L430] 
[L431] "—everywhere."
[L432] 
[L433] Jhace looked at the dust swirling around his boots. At the filament threading from his hands. At the way the copper-gold particles moved toward him like they recognized him.
[L434] 
[L435] "The doubled harmonic," he said.
[L436] 
[L437] "You felt it too?" Tiffani's eyes sharpened.
[L438] 
[L439] "In the grid-hum. Two Tides before this. Wrong frequency. Made my teeth itch."
[L440] 
[L441] Tiffani wrote: **Doubled harmonic: confirmed across multiple observers. Grid infrastructure + atmospheric intrusion event = correlation.**
[L442] 
[L443] A rescue crew leader shouted. "Thirty-eight out. Two still inside. Back corner. Pinned."
[L444] 
[L445] Jhace's vision blurred. The heat in his chest spiked.
[L446] 
[L447] "How long have I been holding?" he rasped.
[L448] 
[L449] Tiffani checked a small timepiece on her belt. "Eighteen minutes."
[L450] 
[L451] "Tallow Street was four."
[L452] 
[L453] "Your cost is accelerating," Tiffani said. "Tremor spread. Vision impairment onset. Nosebleed—"
[L454] 
[L455] Jhace tasted blood before she finished the sentence. Copper-warm, running over his lip.
[L456] 
[L457] "Terminate after extraction," Tiffani said. "Not before. But the instant they're clear, you let go."
[L458] 
[L459] "What if I can't?"
[L460] 
[L461] "Then I make you."
[L462] 
[L463] The last two bodies came out on stretchers. Alive. Barely.
[L464] 
[L465] "Clear!" the crew leader yelled.
[L466] 
[L467] Tiffani's hand closed on Jhace's shoulder—hard enough to hurt, grounding enough to feel real.
[L468] 
[L469] "Terminate," she said. "Now."
[L470] 
[L471] Jhace let the filament go.
[L472] 
[L473] The warehouse settled the last inch it had been denied. Stone grinding. Iron squealing. A beam snapped somewhere in the back, but the void where people had been was empty.
[L474] 
[L475] Jhace staggered back.
[L476] 
[L477] His legs didn't hold. He hit the ground on his knees, hands still shaking, blood dripping from his nose onto the dust-covered stone.
[L478] 
[L479] Tiffani crouched beside him, slate open, pen moving.
[L480] 
[L481] **Second manifestation terminated. Duration: 19 minutes, 40 seconds. Cost: tremor progression (shoulders, torso), vision impairment (peripheral narrowing, copper-gold visual artifacts), epistaxis (nosebleed), motor control compromise (collapse post-termination).**
[L482] 
[L483] **Survival: confirmed. Function: degraded.**
[L484] 
[L485] Jhace wiped blood from his mouth with a shaking hand. "You write everything like it's going to behave."
[L486] 
[L487] "Everything does behave," Tiffani said. "Even this. It has markers. Progression. Termination criteria."
[L488] 
[L489] "It's killing me in increments."
[L490] 
[L491] "Yes," Tiffani said. "Which means we can measure the increments. Track them. Find the threshold before it crosses fatal."
[L492] 
[L493] Jhace looked at her slate. At the notes written in clean, precise handwriting. At his own degradation catalogued like data.
[L494] 
[L495] "Why are you doing this?" he asked.
[L496] 
[L497] Tiffani met his eyes. Hers were sharp, clear, absolutely certain.
[L498] 
[L499] "Because the city is breaking faster than crews can fix it," she said. "Because people like you are going to keep holding until they die. Because if we don't measure the cost, the next bell will kill someone without them knowing why."
[L500] 
[L501] A bell rang in the distance.
[L502] 
[L503] Fourth collapse. Different sector.
[L504] 
[L505] The copper taste surged in Jhace's mouth.
[L506] 
[L507] The heat in his chest flared.
[L508] 
[L509] Tiffani wrote: **Third manifestation event probable. Subject response: autonomic. Cost accumulation: ongoing.**
[L510] 
[L511] She looked at Jhace. "Can you stand?"
[L512] 
[L513] "I don't know."
[L514] 
[L515] "Try."
[L516] 
[L517] Jhace pushed himself to his feet. His legs shook. His hands wouldn't stop trembling. But he stood.
[L518] 
[L519] "Good," Tiffani said. "Because we're going to the next one. And I'm counting every heartbeat."
[L520] 
[L521] She walked toward the sound of the bell.
[L522] 
[L523] Jhace followed.
[L524] 
[L525] The city kept falling.
[L526] 
[L527] And they kept measuring.
[L528] 
[L529] ---
[L530] ## CHAPTER 4: PROTOCOL
[L531] Jhace’s workshop was a box of iron and heat tucked into the mid‑terrace, where you could hear the grid‑hum without being crushed by it.
[L532] 
[L533] Tiffani sat on an overturned crate with her slate on her knees, left leg extended slightly to ease the knee that had started locking after long stands on stone floors. She didn’t rest her back against anything. Always ready to move. Always ready to write.
[L534] Tiffani shifted her weight off her left leg without comment, like it was just another measurement she refused to log.
[L535] 
[L536] Jhace stood at his bench, trying to thread a bolt into a collar bracket.
[L537] 
[L538] His hands shook.
[L539] 
[L540] It wasn’t violent. It wasn’t obvious to someone who didn’t live inside his fingers. But the bolt kept kissing the threads wrong, slipping, catching, refusing to start.
[L541] 
[L542] He tried again. The bolt dropped. Metal on stone.
[L543] 
[L544] He stared at it like it had betrayed him.
[L545] 
[L546] Tiffani didn’t look up. “Your tremor amplitude increased during exertion.”
[L547] 
[L548] “Because I’m exerting,” Jhace muttered, bending to pick up the bolt. His fingertips felt numb where the filament‑light had burned them. Not pain. Absence.
[L549] 
[L550] “Because your body is remembering,” Tiffani corrected. “It thinks you’re still holding.”
[L551] 
[L552] Jhace set the bolt down and flexed his hand hard. The tremor didn’t care.
[L553] 
[L554] He hated that. He hated that his own muscles had become unreliable.
[L555] 
[L556] “What did you call it out there?” he asked.
[L557] 
[L558] Tiffani flipped her slate so he could see. She’d drawn a crude timeline.
[L559] 
[L560] **Activation onset: chest heat + copper taste**  
[L561] **Light manifestation: filament contact‑dependent**  
[L562] **Tremor: post‑termination, permanent marker**
[L563] 
[L564] “I need a taxonomy,” she said. “Stages. Because right now it’s just stories.”
[L565] 
[L566] Jhace wiped his palms on a work cloth. “And you think writing stages keeps people alive.”
[L567] 
[L568] “I think it keeps them from lying to themselves.” She tapped the line where she’d written *permanent*. “You can’t call it temporary. You can’t bargain with it.”
[L569] 
[L570] Jhace’s jaw worked.
[L571] 
[L572] He had bargained with Junction Seven. He had told himself the report would matter. That somebody else would fix it. That the city didn’t need him to fight every foreman with a schedule board.
[L573] 
[L574] He looked at his shaking hands. “Stage One,” he said, voice low. “Tremor.”
[L575] 
[L576] Tiffani nodded and wrote: **Stage One: tremor + filament residue. Functional. Marked.**
[L577] 
[L578] A knock hit the workshop door.
[L579] 
[L580] Bram Tallow leaned in like he was checking whether the room was still allowed to exist. Dust on his boots, sweat at his collar, grin already loaded.
[L581] 
[L582] “Heard you two went and grabbed Tallow Street by the throat,” Bram said. “Real romantic. The city tries to crush you, you crush back. Very intimate.”
[L583] 
[L584] Jhace didn’t look up. “Who’s asking?”
[L585] 
[L586] “Crown’s asking questions,” Bram said, and somehow still made it sound like a dirty joke. “Not because they care. Because they hate unknown tools walking around without a leash and a receipt.”
[L587] 
[L588] Tiffani’s pen paused. “What questions.”
[L589] 
[L590] “Names. Locations. ‘Verified capability.’ That sort of foreplay,” Bram said. He stepped fully inside, then stopped like the heat-grid might charge him rent. “Infrastructure office. Some administrator with hands so clean it makes you wonder what he’s washing off. Loves forms. Loves categories. Loves pretending paper is stronger than stone.”
[L591] 
[L592] Jhace’s jaw tightened. “To do what.”
[L593] 
[L594] Bram spread his hands. “To decide who gets to own you. Temple will call it divine. Guild will call it labor. Crown will call it law. Everybody wants a piece. Congratulations—you’re popular.”
[L595] 
[L596] “We don’t belong to any of them,” Tiffani said.
[L597] 
[L598] Bram laughed, quick and wrong. “Adorable. Tell that to a stamp. Try explaining ‘we don’t belong’ while a ceiling is coming down and someone’s screaming for a hold. They’ll file you under ‘non-compliant’ and act like that’s a weather condition.”
[L599] 
[L600] Jhace’s tremor ticked in his fingers. “So you’re here to warn us.”
[L601] 
[L602] “I’m here to tell you the door is already being measured,” Bram said. “I can buy you a Tide or two. Maybe. After that, they’ll show up with paperwork and smiles and start deciding which parts of you are ‘public infrastructure.’”
[L603] 
[L604] Tiffani’s mouth went flat. “Then we build methodology fast.”
[L605] 
[L606] Bram pointed at her slate like it offended him personally. “Yeah. Before they build it for you. Because if the Crown writes your protocol, it’s going to read like a job description. And you won’t like the benefits package.”
[L607] 
[L608] Jhace breathed in, tasted copper‑gold dust that still clung to his clothes, and felt the city’s hum through the floorboards.
[L609] 
[L610] “Then we build it fast,” he said. “Before they decide to build it for us.”
[L611] 
[L612] ---
[L613] 
[L614] ## CHAPTER 5: FIRST NETWORK
[L615] The grain warehouse sat in Sector Nine, where the terraces were newer and the beams were honest enough to still pretend they could be trusted.
[L616] 
[L617] The bell signal came late. That was the first problem.
[L618] 
[L619] When Jhace and Tiffani arrived, the warehouse wasn’t collapsed—yet. The support columns were bowing, groaning under a load they shouldn’t have been carrying because somebody had stacked wet grain too high to meet a schedule.
[L620] 
[L621] A foreman screamed, “Hold it! Hold it now!”
[L622] 
[L623] Jhace moved toward the nearest column out of habit.
[L624] 
[L625] Tiffani caught his sleeve. “Not yet.”
[L626] 
[L627] Jhace’s jaw tightened. “People are inside.”
[L628] 
[L629] “People are *leaving*,” she corrected, pointing.
[L630] 
[L631] She was right. Workers were already pouring out of the side doors, coughing, eyes wide. The structure wasn’t in free fall. It was in warning.
[L632] 
[L633] “We wedge first,” Tiffani said. “Then you hold if wedges fail.”
[L634] 
[L635] Jhace’s tremor wanted to argue. His chest heat wanted to flare. The hunger for that sudden alignment—pipe hum into palm, world pausing—sat behind his ribs like an itch.
[L636] 
[L637] He didn’t scratch it.
[L638] 
[L639] He dropped to one knee and shoved wedge slips under the first bowing brace. Bram’s crew followed, hands fast, straps ready. For once, labor did what labor could do.
[L640] 
[L641] The warehouse groaned again. Dust spilled from a seam.
[L642] 
[L643] A young man stepped into the brace line, hands hovering like he didn’t know what to do with them. Corven. Jhace had seen him in the tunnels, quiet, competent.
[L644] 
[L645] Corven swallowed. “I… I can feel it,” he said. “The hum. It’s—”
[L646] 
[L647] “Louder,” Jhace finished.
[L648] 
[L649] Corven nodded hard. “Like it’s in my teeth.”
[L650] 
[L651] Tiffani stepped close, slate already up. “If it hits your chest, tell me. If you taste copper, tell me. Do not improvise.”
[L652] 
[L653] Corven’s eyes flicked to the bending column. “If it goes—”
[L654] 
[L655] “Then we act,” Jhace said. “But not before.”
[L656] 
[L657] The wedges held for two breaths.
[L658] 
[L659] Then the central column shifted.
[L660] 
[L661] Not collapse. A decisive, ugly move—stone settling, iron flexing. The wedges screamed as they compressed.
[L662] 
[L663] The workers inside were still running.
[L664] 
[L665] “Now,” Tiffani said.
[L666] 
[L667] Jhace put both hands on the column.
[L668] 
[L669] Heat flared in his chest—familiar now, terrifying in its familiarity—and filament‑light threaded into the grain‑dust coated seam.
[L670] 
[L671] The column stopped shifting.
[L672] 
[L673] Corven stared at Jhace’s hands like they were a language he’d almost learned.
[L674] 
[L675] “Your turn,” Jhace said through gritted teeth.
[L676] 
[L677] Corven stepped forward and touched the secondary brace.
[L678] 
[L679] His breath hitched. Copper taste. Chest heat. Then light—thinner than Jhace’s, but real—threaded into the brace line.
[L680] 
[L681] Corven’s hands started to shake immediately.
[L682] 
[L683] “Stage One onset during hold,” Tiffani murmured, writing. “Early marker.”
[L684] 
[L685] “Keep moving! If you stop, you become debris!” Bram shouted to the evac crew. “Count them out! Loud. Fast. Like the Crown’s listening!”
[L686] 
[L687] Jhace’s tremor surged in sympathy, like the grid‑hum had tied their nervous systems together. His arms burned. His vision tunneled at the edges.
[L688] 
[L689] “How long?” Tiffani asked.
[L690] 
[L691] “Eighty,” Jhace rasped, counting heartbeats without thinking.
[L692] 
[L693] Corven’s jaw clenched. “I can do—”
[L694] 
[L695] “Do *exactly* what the structure needs,” Jhace snapped. “Nothing more.”
[L696] 
[L697] At one‑fifty, the last workers stumbled out. Two hundred‑plus bodies, alive because they’d been allowed time.
[L698] 
[L699] “Terminate,” Tiffani said.
[L700] 
[L701] Jhace released first. The column held under wedges now that the load had shifted down.
[L702] 
[L703] Corven released a heartbeat later and nearly fell.
[L704] 
[L705] Jhace caught him by the elbow, and the tremor in his own hands made the grip feel unreliable.
[L706] 
[L707] Corven looked at his shaking fingers with a kind of horror. “It’s not stopping.”
[L708] 
[L709] “It doesn’t,” Jhace said.
[L710] 
[L711] Corven stared at his shaking fingers like they’d betrayed him mid-shift.
[L712] 
[L713] He’d watched Jhace hold. Had seen the tremor. Had understood it in theory.
[L714] 
[L715] Theory didn’t matter when your hand wouldn’t listen.
[L716] 
[L717] “How long?” he asked.
[L718] 
[L719] Jhace’s jaw tightened. “Until you stop manifesting.”
[L720] 
[L721] Corven’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. “So never.”
[L722] 
[L723] “Yeah.”
[L724] 
[L725] Corven set his palm on the edge of the bench. The tremor rattled bone against metal.
[L726] 
[L727] He pulled back like it burned. Flexed. Tried again. Harder.
[L728] 
[L729] It kept moving.
[L730] 
[L731] Tiffani wrote the number anyway: **219 evacuated. Zero casualties. Two operators marked.**
[L732] 
[L733] Bram approached, face hard. “Crown’s going to hear about this—and they’ll bring forms instead of shovels.”
[L734] 
[L735] Tiffani didn’t look up. “Good.”
[L736] 
[L737] Jhace’s chest still burned with the after‑heat of holding. The hunger whispered: *You could have held longer. You could have made it perfect.*
[L738] 
[L739] He swallowed copper and told the hunger to shut up.
[L740] 
[L741] For now.
[L742] 
[L743] ---
[L744] 
[L745] 
[L746] ---
[L747] 
[L748] ### ROUTE FAILURE (THREE DEAD)
[L749] 
[L750] The bell came from Sector Nine, not as a roar—just a single strike repeated until someone answered.
[L751] 
[L752] Residential stairwell. Upper-terrace split. Twelve families still inside, one route.
[L753] 
[L754] Jhace was already marked from the warehouse. Corven was worse. Senne was fresh enough to stand without shaking.
[L755] 
[L756] Tiffani looked at the slate wall and made the decision she would later pretend was only math.
[L757] 
[L758] Two operators at one‑eighty each meant coordination, handoffs, spotters, and failure points. One operator at two‑forty meant simplicity. A clean line.
[L759] 
[L760] “Single deployment,” she said. “Senne. Duration cap: two‑forty. Terminate on my bell.”
[L761] 
[L762] Senne went pale as the heat took her chest. Copper-gold light laced her forearms. Not pretty. Functional.
[L763] 
[L764] They reached the stairwell mouth as stone dust sifted from the ceiling like flour. The grid-hum underfoot carried the doubled harmonic, deep and insistent, a warning that didn’t care who listened.
[L765] 
[L766] Senne braced under the central landing. Jhace watched her shoulders set—metalworker’s posture in a glassworker’s frame.
[L767] 
[L768] Tiffani counted. Heartbeats, not seconds. Seconds lied when panic touched them.
[L769] 
[L770] One hundred.
[L771] 
[L772] One forty.
[L773] 
[L774] One eighty.
[L775] 
[L776] At one‑ninety, a secondary beam—one Tiffani hadn’t walked because she’d trusted the map—shifted with a sound like teeth grinding.
[L777] 
[L778] The evacuation lane kinked. The next family stumbled. The stone didn’t collapse all at once. It folded, patient and specific, closing the corridor into a narrow pocket.
[L779] 
[L780] Three people were on the wrong side when it did: two adults and a child coughing copper into his sleeve.
[L781] 
[L782] Tiffani saw them, and for a moment the slate in her hands felt obscene.
[L783] 
[L784] “Hold,” she said, voice flat. “Keep holding.”
[L785] 
[L786] Senne’s eyes didn’t move. She couldn’t spare the motion.
[L787] 
[L788] Two‑ten.
[L789] 
[L790] Two‑twenty.
[L791] 
[L792] Two‑thirty.
[L793] 
[L794] The pocket didn’t clear. The child’s coughing turned wet.
[L795] 
[L796] At two‑forty, Tiffani rang the termination sequence anyway. Because protocols existed for a reason. Because if she didn’t, she would never stop.
[L797] 
[L798] Senne released.
[L799] 
[L800] The stairwell collapsed into itself with a slow, final sound—stone settling into the shape it had wanted all along.
[L801] 
[L802] They dug for hours.
[L803] 
[L804] They found three bodies. The child first, because he was small enough to slip into the wrong angles.
[L805] 
[L806] Back in the workshop, Tiffani logged it with perfect precision.
[L807] 
[L808] **Deployment:** Senne — 240 heartbeats  
[L809] **Evacuated:** 47  
[L810] **Casualties:** 3  
[L811] **Cause:** Inadequate route assessment. Secondary beam shear.
[L812] 
[L813] She wrote “cause” as if precision could make it reversible.
[L814] 
[L815] Her hand moved to write the next revision. The chalk touched slate.
[L816] 
[L817] Nothing came out.
[L818] 
[L819] She stared at the number.
[L820] 
[L821] Three.
[L822] 
[L823] Not a count. Three names she would never know. Three families who would ask why their—
[L824] 
[L825] The chalk snapped in her grip.
[L826] 
[L827] She set the broken pieces down carefully. Picked up a new stick. Wrote:
[L828] 
[L829] **Route spotters required. Three per deployment. No exceptions.**
[L830] 
[L831] Underlined it twice.
[L832] 
[L833] Then she closed the slate and sat in the dark workshop for six hundred heartbeats, doing nothing but breathing and failing to justify the number.
[L834] 
[L835] 
[L836] 
[L837] ---
[L838] 
[L839] ## CHAPTER 6: WITNESS
[L840] Brother Corran didn’t wear Temple colors.
[L841] 
[L842] That was the first thing Jhace noticed when the man stepped into the workshop two Tides after the warehouse. No robes. No beads. Just a plain coat and a Crown badge pinned where it couldn’t be missed.
[L843] 
[L844] Corran’s eyes went straight to Jhace’s hands.
[L845] 
[L846] “For the record, I’m not here to stop you,” Corran said, before anyone asked.
[L847] 
[L848] Bram hovered by the door like a lock that wanted to be useful.
[L849] 
[L850] Tiffani set her slate down deliberately. “Then why are you here?”
[L851] 
[L852] “Because you’re real,” Corran said. “And because real things attract predators.”
[L853] 
[L854] Jhace’s mouth tightened. “Crown.”
[L855] 
[L856] Corran’s gaze didn’t flinch. “Yes. Also Guild. Also Temple. Anyone who thinks they can turn you into a lever.”
[L857] 
[L858] Bram’s voice came dry. “Ah yes. The holy trinity of getting fucked: prayer, production, paperwork.”
[L859] 
[L860] Tiffani shot him a look. “Bram.”
[L861] 
[L862] He raised his hands. “What? I’m helping.”
[L863] 
[L864] Bram gestured toward the door. “Temple says you’re divine. Guild says you’re labor. Crown says you’re property. Everybody’s trying to fuck the same lever.”
[L865] 
[L866] He pulled a folded packet from his coat and set it on Jhace’s bench. Paper. Old style. Archive weight.
[L867] 
[L868] “Stage Three cases,” Corran said. “Documented. Not rumors.”
[L869] 
[L870] Tiffani’s hands went still. “How many?”
[L871] 
[L872] “Enough.” Corran’s voice was flat, and that flatness carried exhaustion. “Most die. A few don’t. The ones who don’t… wish they had.”
[L873] 
[L874] Jhace’s tremor ticked up at the word *die*. He forced his hands flat on the bench. “Why give this to us?”
[L875] 
[L876] Corran hesitated. That hesitation was the crack in the Crown.
[L877] 
[L878] “Because someone above me wants a registry,” he said. “Names. Locations. Deployment authority. They’re calling it order. It’s control.”
[L879] 
[L880] Tiffani’s eyes narrowed. “Who?”
[L881] 
[L882] Corran looked toward the door as if the walls might report him. “Administrator Corlexi. Infrastructure Compliance.”
[L883] 
[L884] Bram gave a low whistle. “Clean hands. Clean coat. Probably wipes his ass with forms.”
[L885] 
[L886] Jhace stared at the packet. “So you’re here to warn us.”
[L887] 
[L888] “I’m here to give you data,” Corran corrected. “If you want to fight control, you need numbers. Not speeches.”
[L889] 
[L890] Tiffani opened the packet and read, eyes moving fast.
[L891] 
[L892] Jhace watched her face tighten at certain lines—durations, costs, outcomes. She didn’t gasp. She didn’t dramatize. She just absorbed.
[L893] 
[L894] “This case,” she said, tapping a page. “Stage Three survival. How?”
[L895] 
[L896] Corran’s mouth went thin. “He didn’t terminate. He couldn’t. The hold locked. Autonomic failure started and the manifestation kept running anyway. The structure held until it didn’t matter.”
[L897] 
[L898] Jhace felt cold crawl up his spine despite the workshop heat. “Locked hold.”
[L899] 
[L900] Corran nodded. “It’s not a tool you put down when you’re done. Sometimes it’s a hook in your chest that drags you until something breaks.”
[L901] 
[L902] Tiffani’s pen scratched. **Stage Three: autonomic failure. Locked hold possible. Termination may fail.**
[L903] 
[L904] Bram shifted. “You said predators.”
[L905] 
[L906] Corran’s eyes met Bram’s. “Tallec is filing complaints. Says you’re obstructing industry. Says your protocols slow production.”
[L907] 
[L908] Jhace’s jaw clenched so hard it hurt.
[L909] 
[L910] Corran leaned closer, voice low. “Here’s the fracture you wanted. I don’t like Corlex
